questions.question: Your team is defining a Service Level Indicator (SLI) for a Cloud Run checkout API. The product manager wants the SLI to reflect the actual end-user experience during purchase flows. Which approach best defines the availability SLI?
options: Use a Cloud Monitoring uptime check that probes the public endpoint every 60 seconds from a single region and counts each 200 response as a successful request for availability
Use a synthetic monitor running every 5 minutes that posts a fixed payload to /checkout and reports the percentage of probe runs that return any non-5xx HTTP status code
Measure the ratio of HTTP requests to /checkout that return a non-5xx status within 800ms over total valid requests, computed from Cloud Run request logs and load balancer metrics
Track the average CPU utilization of the underlying Cloud Run revision and treat any minute under 80% saturation as available, alerting only when utilization sustains above that threshold
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because uptime checks measure probe-level reachability from monitoring infrastructure, not the real user-traffic success ratio that an SLI requires. Option B is wrong because synthetic probes still do not reflect actual user requests and 'non-5xx' alone ignores latency, which is part of the user experience. Option C is correct because a good availability SLI is a ratio of good events (non-5xx within a latency budget) to total valid events measured from real user traffic. Option D is wrong because CPU utilization is a resource/saturation signal, not a user-facing SLI; high CPU does not mean the user request succeeded.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: A new internal API has no SLO yet. Leadership wants you to publish one within two weeks. According to SRE practice, which approach should you take to set the initial SLO target?
options: Measure baseline performance over a representative period using existing telemetry, then set an SLO slightly below current performance so the team has realistic headroom and room to improve
Adopt 99.99% availability because four nines is the industry default for production APIs and aligns with what most Google Cloud managed services advertise as their reliability targets
Set the SLO to match the strictest downstream consumer's SLA so that contractual obligations cascade upstream automatically and no consumer can ever be impacted by your service
Defer publishing any SLO until the service has accumulated 12 months of production traffic, at which point a statistically valid target can be derived from long-term trend data
questions.explanation: Option A is correct: SRE practice recommends measuring real baseline performance and setting an initial SLO slightly below it, then iterating. Option B is wrong because adopting 99.99% arbitrarily ignores the cost of reliability and what users actually need; nines should be justified by user impact. Option C is wrong because an internal SLO is not the same as a downstream SLA and chaining to the strictest consumer creates unachievable targets and budget mismatches. Option D is wrong because delaying SLO publication for 12 months prevents the team from using error budgets to make decisions; iteration is expected.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: Your service has a 99.5% availability SLO measured over a rolling 28-day window. Over the last 28 days the service served 40,000,000 valid requests and 260,000 of them failed. What is the state of the error budget?
options: The error budget has 35% remaining because 260,000 failures equal 0.65% of total requests, which is well within the 1% allowance derived from a 99% baseline target over the window
The error budget is exhausted with a small overage because failures equal 0.65% of requests while the allowed failure rate is 0.5%, so the team must invoke its error budget policy
The error budget cannot be calculated from these numbers without first normalizing by user sessions and weighting requests by criticality across the service's critical user journeys

The error budget is exhausted and overrun: 260,000 failures exceed the 200,000-failure monthly allowance, consuming 130% of the budget and leaving a 30% deficit that must be addressed by the error budget policy
questions.explanation: Allowed failures = (1 - 0.995) * 40,000,000 = 200,000. Consumed failures = 260,000, so the budget is 130% consumed (overspent by 30%). Option A is wrong because it cites the wrong SLO baseline (99% rather than 99.5%) and a remaining-budget percentage that does not match the numbers. Option B is wrong because the math description is imprecise ”” labeling 260k vs 200k as a 'small overage' understates a 30% overrun. Option C is wrong because SLIs are computed from event ratios directly; session weighting is not required for the basic calculation. Option D is correct: 260,000/200,000 = 130% consumed, the budget is fully exhausted plus 30% over, and the policy must be invoked.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: You are configuring multi-window, multi-burn-rate alerts in Cloud Monitoring for a service with a 99.9% SLO over 30 days. Which configuration most accurately implements the SRE workbook recommendation for a 'fast burn' page?
options: Trigger when the 24-hour error rate exceeds 0.1%, gated by a 1-hour error rate above 0.1%, so any small breach over either window will page the on-call engineer immediately
Trigger when the 1-hour burn rate exceeds 14.4x, gated by a 5-minute burn rate also above 14.4x, which corresponds to consuming 2% of the 30-day budget within the alerting window
Trigger only when the 30-day rolling SLO falls below 99.9% in real time, which guarantees no alert fires until the budget is fully exhausted and customers are clearly impacted
Trigger when the 7-day burn rate exceeds 1x, gated by a 24-hour burn rate above 1x, which captures slow long-term drift but avoids paging during transient spikes in error rate
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because a raw error-rate threshold without a burn-rate framing does not scale with SLO and tends to be noisy. Option B is correct: the SRE workbook recommends a fast-burn page at 14.4x burn rate (2% of 30-day budget in 1 hour) with a short secondary window to suppress flapping. Option C is wrong because alerting only when the SLO is already breached defeats the purpose of proactive paging. Option D is wrong because 1x burn over 7/24 hours is the slow-burn ticket configuration, not a page; it is intentionally non-urgent.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: Which of the following best matches the SRE definition of toil for an on-call engineer's workload assessment?
options: Work that is manual, repetitive, automatable, tactical, devoid of enduring value, and that scales linearly with service growth ”” for example, manually restarting a stuck batch job each week
Any operational work performed outside of project hours, including incident response, postmortem authoring, capacity reviews, and architecture documentation that supports the production system
Work that is technically interesting but unplanned, such as debugging a novel race condition discovered through a customer report and writing a one-off fix that ships with the next release
Work that requires deep engineering judgment and produces lasting infrastructure improvements, such as designing a new sharding strategy for a database that is approaching its write limit
questions.explanation: Option A is correct: SRE defines toil as manual, repetitive, automatable, tactical, devoid of enduring value, and scaling with service growth. Option B is wrong because it conflates toil with all operational work; postmortems and capacity planning produce lasting value and are engineering work, not toil. Option C is wrong because novel debugging is not repetitive; it is engineering work even if unplanned. Option D is wrong because design work that yields enduring improvements is the opposite of toil ”” it is project work that often reduces toil.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: A service has an SLA of 99.5% monthly availability with a 10% billing credit if breached. The team is asked to justify why their internal SLO is set tighter at 99.9%. Which justification is INCORRECT and should NOT be used?
options: It provides a safety buffer so the team can detect reliability degradation and react before the contractual SLA is breached and customer credits become owed for the affected billing period
It allows the team to use error budget exhaustion as an internal signal to slow releases before any external customer-facing impact reaches the contractual threshold for credit issuance
It creates a gap between observed performance and the contractual line, giving room for incident response, postmortems, and progressive recovery without immediate financial exposure to the customer
It is mandated by Google Cloud's shared responsibility model, which requires every customer SLO to be at least 4x tighter than the SLA of the underlying infrastructure component hosting the workload
questions.explanation: The question asks which justification is INCORRECT. Option A is wrong as the answer because it IS a valid justification (safety buffer before SLA breach). Option B is wrong as the answer because it IS a valid justification (early signal for release decisions). Option C is wrong as the answer because it IS a valid justification (room for response without financial exposure). Option D is correct as the answer: no such 4x rule exists in the Google Cloud shared responsibility model; the SLO/SLA gap is a business decision, not a mandated multiplier. This is the incorrect justification.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: Your team must choose between symptom-based and cause-based alerting for a new service. Which statement best reflects the SRE-recommended approach in the Google SRE workbook?
options: Cause-based alerting is preferred because each alert points directly to a root cause, which lets on-call engineers skip triage and immediately apply the relevant runbook with no diagnosis required
Symptom-based alerting is preferred because it pages on user-visible impact tied to SLOs; cause-based alerts are useful supplementary signals but tend to be noisy and miss novel failures
Both should be avoided in favor of anomaly detection on Cloud Monitoring metrics, which uses machine learning to surface deviations without requiring SLOs or thresholds to be defined by the team
Cause-based alerting on every dependency's health is required so that the on-call rotation can react to upstream degradation before user-facing SLIs reflect the issue or trip an alert
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because cause-based alerts typically overlap, miss novel causes, and create alert fatigue; they do not eliminate triage. Option B is correct: symptom-based alerts tied to SLO/SLI ensure pages reflect user impact; cause-based alerts are useful for diagnosis but not the primary pager source. Option C is wrong because anomaly detection alone is noisy and not a substitute for SLO-driven alerting. Option D is wrong because alerting on every dependency creates excessive noise and pages for issues that may not affect users.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: You define an availability SLI as the ratio of HTTP 2xx/3xx responses to total requests at the external HTTPS load balancer. A regional outage causes 30 minutes where the load balancer returns 502 because the backend is unhealthy. How is this period reflected in the SLI?
options: It is not reflected because the load balancer itself remained healthy, and only requests that reach a backend instance count toward the SLI denominator under standard Google Cloud measurement
It is reflected only if Cloud Armor logging is enabled, since otherwise 5xx responses generated by the load balancer are excluded from the load balancer access log stream used by the SLI
It is reflected as a sharp drop in the SLI: every 502 counts as a bad event in the numerator's complement while still counting in the denominator, so the success ratio drops accordingly
It is reflected only after the load balancer's health check confirms backend unhealthiness, at which point Cloud Monitoring backfills the affected minute as unavailable in the SLO service
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because the load balancer logs and counts 502s as completed requests with bad status; they are part of the SLI. Option B is wrong because Cloud Armor is unrelated to load balancer access log generation. Option C is correct: 502s appear in load balancer logs, are counted in the denominator, and fail the 'good event' test, so the SLI drops during the outage. Option D is wrong because there is no backfill mechanism ”” the SLI is computed continuously from logged events as they occur.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: Your error budget policy states that when 50% of the monthly budget is consumed, the team must redirect 25% of feature work to reliability. The budget hits 55% on day 12 of 30. What is the correct application of the policy?
options: Wait until the end of the calendar month to reassess, since policies based on monthly windows are only evaluated at month boundaries and short-term consumption does not trigger reallocation actions

Trigger the reallocation only if a second condition fires, such as a high-severity incident or a regression in latency SLI, since single-threshold policies are advisory rather than enforceable in practice
Cancel all in-flight feature work for the remainder of the month and route 100% of engineering time into reliability initiatives until the next monthly window opens with a fresh budget allocation

Apply the reallocation now: 25% of remaining feature capacity for the rest of the window shifts to reliability work, and the team revisits at the next threshold or at window reset, whichever comes first
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because error budget policies are typically evaluated continuously, not just at window boundaries ”” that is the point of the budget. Option B is wrong because adding ad-hoc gating conditions undermines the policy; the threshold is itself the trigger. Option C is wrong because halting all feature work is an overreaction not specified by the 25% policy; it also burns trust between teams. Option D is correct: the policy says 25% reallocation at 50% consumption, and at 55% the team applies it immediately.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: Which of the following is the best Critical User Journey (CUJ) to define SLIs for an e-commerce site, according to SRE practice?
options: Browse-search-add-to-cart-checkout-pay: the end-to-end path a paying customer follows, since SLIs aligned to this journey best capture revenue-impacting failures across the dependent microservices
Backend database write latency for the orders table, since the orders table is the most critical persistence layer and slowdowns there will eventually surface as user-facing problems in checkout
CPU utilization of every Cloud Run service in the platform, normalized into a single composite score and tracked as the primary indicator of system health and customer experience over time
Number of internal admin actions performed per minute, since admin throughput correlates with the team's operational tempo and is easier to instrument than user-facing journeys end-to-end
questions.explanation: Option A is correct: SRE recommends defining SLIs around critical user journeys that reflect what users actually do and where revenue is generated. Option B is wrong because backend database latency is an internal signal, not a CUJ; it may or may not correlate with user impact. Option C is wrong because CPU is a resource metric, not a user-facing journey, and aggregating into a composite hides what users experience. Option D is wrong because admin throughput is not a customer-facing journey and does not measure customer experience.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: You want to compute an availability SLO directly inside Cloud Monitoring's SLO service for a service fronted by an external HTTP(S) load balancer. Which configuration matches the recommended setup?
options: Create a custom metric from Logs Explorer that counts only 200 responses, then express the SLO as the absolute count exceeding 99.9% of the previous month's request total over a rolling window
Create a Service in Cloud Monitoring and define a request-based SLI using the loadbalancing.googleapis.com/https/request_count metric filtered by response_code_class, with a 30-day rolling window
Enable VPC Flow Logs on the load balancer's serverless NEG, then build an SLO that consumes the flow log volume as a proxy for request success since flow logs are sampled and lightweight to query
Export load balancer logs to BigQuery, run a scheduled query every minute that computes success ratios, and write the result back to Cloud Monitoring via the custom metrics API for SLO evaluation
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because SLO targets are ratios, not absolute counts based on prior-month totals; the formulation is incorrect. Option B is correct: Cloud Monitoring's SLO service supports request-based SLIs that use the load balancer's built-in request_count metric split by response_code_class to compute good/total ratios. Option C is wrong because VPC Flow Logs measure network flows, not HTTP success, and are sampled ”” unsuitable as an availability SLI. Option D is wrong because while technically possible, it duplicates what the built-in SLO service does natively and adds latency and cost.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: A team is measuring latency SLI as 'p99 request latency below 500ms over a 5-minute window'. Why is this generally a weaker SLI than a request-based formulation?
options: Because percentile latency cannot be computed inside Cloud Monitoring without enabling distribution-valued metrics, which require manual exporter configuration in every Cloud Run revision deployed by the team

Because percentile-of-percentiles math breaks across longer windows, so the 5-minute p99 cannot be aggregated into an SLO over 30 days without storing the raw histogram and recomputing percentiles from buckets
Because the SLI does not produce a good/total event ratio that maps cleanly to an error budget; it is a threshold over a statistic, making error budget consumption hard to interpret per request
Because Cloud Monitoring caps percentile calculation at p95, so any SLI referencing p99 is approximated by extrapolation rather than measured directly from the underlying request distribution
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because Cloud Monitoring supports distribution metrics natively and Cloud Run emits request latency as a distribution. Option B is true on its own but is a side effect, not the primary weakness; request-based SLI also resolves it. Option C is correct: the recommended SLI form is good_events/total_events (e.g., requests served under 500ms / total requests), which maps directly to an error budget per event. Option D is wrong because Cloud Monitoring computes percentiles from histogram buckets up to and including p99.9.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: Your service has plenty of error budget remaining. The product team wants to roll out a risky experimental feature. Which application of error budget policy is most aligned with SRE principles?
options: Permit the rollout, ideally with progressive delivery techniques like canary or gradual percentage rollout, since available error budget signals the team can absorb the risk of an experimental change
Block the rollout pending a formal risk review by the SRE team, regardless of remaining budget, since experimental features always carry too much unknown risk to be released in production conditions
Permit the rollout only after consuming the entire remaining budget on planned chaos engineering exercises, since the budget exists to be spent and unused budget is wasted reliability capacity
Require a manual change advisory board approval for any new feature whenever budget is above 50% to ensure that available budget is reserved exclusively for unplanned incident recovery work
questions.explanation: Option A is correct: the error budget enables velocity. When budget exists, risky changes are permissible ”” ideally protected by progressive rollout to limit blast radius. Option B is wrong because gating regardless of budget defeats the budget mechanism. Option C is wrong because the budget is not 'wasted' if unused; deliberately spending it is an anti-pattern that introduces unnecessary risk. Option D is wrong because CAB-style gating contradicts the SRE principle of using budget data to govern velocity automatically.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: Which item from the following list is NOT one of the four golden signals defined in the Google SRE book for monitoring a user-facing service?
options: Latency ”” the time it takes to service a request, typically split by success and failure since a fast failure can mask a real problem when only successful requests are measured for latency
Traffic ”” a measure of how much demand is being placed on the service, such as HTTP requests per second, transactions per second, or session count depending on the service domain
Errors ”” the rate of requests that fail, either explicitly via 5xx, implicitly via wrong content, or by policy when responses violate latency thresholds that constitute user-visible failure
Cost efficiency ”” a measure of compute and storage spend per request served, normalized against the service's traffic and used to ensure that scaling decisions remain financially sustainable
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong (i.e., it IS a golden signal): latency is one of the four. Option B is wrong (it IS a golden signal): traffic is one of the four. Option C is wrong (it IS a golden signal): errors are one of the four. Option D is correct as the answer because cost efficiency is NOT a golden signal; the four are latency, traffic, errors, and saturation. Saturation ”” not cost ”” completes the set.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: A composite service depends on three internal microservices A, B, and C, each with a 99.9% availability SLO and called in series. Assuming independent failure modes, what is the upper-bound theoretical availability of the composite call path?
options: 99.9% ”” the composite availability is dominated by the highest individual SLO since callers fail over to alternate paths whenever a single backend dependency exceeds its allocated error budget
99.7% ”” multiplying 0.999 across three independent dependencies yields approximately 0.997, since serial dependencies compound error rates rather than averaging them across the call path
99.99% ”” caching and request retries between services raise the effective composite availability above any single SLO target by masking transient failures in the dependent backend systems
99.0% ”” three serial dependencies always degrade composite availability by at least one full nine because retries amplify load when any dependency degrades during normal production conditions
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because the composite is bounded by the product of dependencies, not the max; serial calls compound failure. Option B is correct: 0.999 × 0.999 × 0.999 â‰ˆ 0.997, giving a theoretical upper bound of 99.7%. Option C is wrong because caching/retries are mitigations that can help but the theoretical bound from the model is 99.7%, not 99.99%. Option D is wrong because the math gives 99.7%, not 99.0%; retries amplifying load is a separate concern from the baseline calculation.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: Your SLO uses a 30-day rolling window. A spike of failures on day 1 of last month has just rolled out of the window. What happens to the error budget status?
options: Nothing changes until the next calendar month rollover, since Cloud Monitoring's SLO service requires explicit window reset confirmation before older error events are removed from the SLI calculation
The window restarts from zero whenever any past spike rolls out, requiring the team to manually reauthorize the SLO target through the gcloud CLI or Cloud Monitoring console before continuing
Older bad events on the trailing edge of the rolling window fall out of the calculation, which can free up budget that the team can apply to upcoming releases without waiting for any reset
The historical spike remains counted permanently because Cloud Monitoring stores cumulative error counts and only the rolling-average view is recomputed on the dashboard for visualization
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because rolling windows continuously slide; no manual confirmation is required. Option B is wrong because rolling windows do not 'restart from zero'; the math is continuous and incremental. Option C is correct: in a rolling window, events leaving the trailing edge are no longer counted, which can restore budget over time as old failures age out. Option D is wrong because rolling SLOs do not retain past events forever ”” that would make the window meaningless.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: You inherit an on-call rotation where 70% of pages over the last quarter were caused by a flapping memory alert on a Cloud SQL instance that auto-recovered before the engineer logged in. What is the highest-leverage SRE response?
options: Increase the on-call rotation size from 4 to 8 engineers so the per-person page load drops and recovery time targets improve, distributing the existing alert burden more evenly across the team
Configure Cloud Monitoring to suppress this alert during business hours and re-enable it at night so daytime productivity is preserved while overnight on-call coverage continues to receive pages
Forward the flapping alerts to a dedicated chat channel without paging, since the engineer can review them asynchronously in the morning rather than waking up to acknowledge auto-recovered events

Tune the alert to fire only when memory pressure is sustained and correlated with a user-visible SLI degradation, and file a backlog item to right-size or autoscale the instance to remove the root cause
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because adding people to absorb noise does not fix the underlying problem; it dilutes pain and accumulates toil. Option B is wrong because suppression by time of day is arbitrary and masks real issues that may occur during business hours. Option C is wrong because silently routing a flapping alert to a channel still leaves the noise and does not address the cause. Option D is correct: tune to symptom-based, SLI-correlated firing and remove the root cause by right-sizing ”” the SRE approach of eliminating toil at the source.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: Which of the following is the most accurate description of an error budget burn rate?
options: The ratio of current error rate to the maximum error rate allowed by the SLO; a burn rate of 1 means the budget will be consumed exactly over the SLO window, and 10 means 10x faster than allowed
The total number of error events in the current window divided by the number of error events permitted; the value remains constant during the window and is only computed at window close for reports
The percentage of error budget remaining at any moment, normalized by the size of the SLO window so values are comparable across services with different window lengths and target values

The historical mean time between failures (MTBF) for the service divided by mean time to recovery (MTTR); a burn rate above 1 means recovery is slower than failure cadence over the past rolling window
questions.explanation: Option A is correct: burn rate is current_error_rate / allowed_error_rate. A burn rate of 1 consumes the budget exactly across the window; 10 means 10x faster consumption. Option B is wrong because that describes cumulative consumption, not burn rate, and burn rate is computed continuously. Option C is wrong because that is 'remaining budget', not burn rate. Option D is wrong because burn rate is not MTBF/MTTR; those are separate reliability metrics.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: You are reviewing a postmortem in which an engineer manually scaled a GKE node pool every Friday before peak traffic. The postmortem identifies this as toil. Which automation approach best eliminates the toil per SRE practice?
options: Add a recurring Cloud Scheduler job that triggers a Cloud Function to run gcloud container clusters resize at the appropriate time on Friday, then reverts the size on Sunday using a second scheduled call
Document the manual procedure in detail in the team runbook so that any on-call engineer can perform it consistently, and rotate ownership of the Friday scaling task across the SRE team weekly

Enable the GKE cluster autoscaler with appropriate min/max node bounds per pool so that capacity adjusts automatically based on pending pod resource requests, eliminating the recurring manual intervention
Move the workload to Cloud Run so that capacity is fully managed by the platform, redeploying every service and rewriting any Kubernetes-specific manifests during the migration process and validation
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because scheduled scaling guesses at demand and still requires maintenance of the schedule; it is automation but not the best fit. Option B is wrong because documentation perpetuates toil rather than eliminating it. Option C is correct: cluster autoscaler responds to actual demand and removes the manual step entirely while staying inside the existing GKE platform. Option D is wrong because rewriting the platform is a disproportionate response when a built-in autoscaler solves the toil directly.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: What is the recommended cap on toil for an SRE engineer's time, according to the Google SRE book?
options: Toil should be capped at 25% of an SRE's time on average, with the remaining 75% spent on operational tasks like ticket queues, capacity reviews, and on-call shadow rotations across the platform
Toil should be capped at 50% of an SRE's time, leaving the other 50% for engineering work; sustained toil above 50% is a signal that the team needs reinforcement or automation investment
Toil should be capped at 75% of an SRE's time when the team is on-call for multiple services, since rotation overlap inevitably consumes additional manual operational effort during peak quarters

Toil should be eliminated entirely within the first year of SRE engagement; any remaining toil after that point indicates a failure of the SRE team to deliver on its engineering charter for the service
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because 25% inverts the recommendation; SREs should spend at least 50% on engineering. Option B is correct: the SRE book sets a 50% cap on toil so that the remaining time is engineering work that reduces future toil. Option C is wrong because 75% would mean SREs are operating, not engineering ”” defeating the model. Option D is wrong because zero toil is unrealistic; toil is reduced over time, not eliminated overnight.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: Your error budget policy includes a 'release freeze' provision when the budget is exhausted. Which type of change should still be allowed during a freeze under the SRE-recommended freeze policy?
options: Reliability fixes, security patches, and changes that directly reduce error budget consumption, since the purpose of the freeze is to restore reliability rather than block all production deployment activity
Any change that has been pre-approved by the product owner, since freezes are an SRE-team recommendation rather than an absolute gate and product priorities can override the policy at any time
Feature flag toggles for unreleased experimental code paths, since they do not deploy new binaries and therefore cannot introduce new failure modes during the freeze window for the affected service

All changes are allowed if accompanied by a manual rollback plan, since the freeze is intended to slow change cadence rather than block it, and rollback plans mitigate the marginal risk of each change
questions.explanation: Option A is correct: freezes still permit changes that increase reliability (security patches, fixes, capacity adds). Option B is wrong because product overrides defeat the policy; the freeze must be enforced for it to be meaningful. Option C is wrong because feature flags do introduce risk ”” config changes have caused major outages ”” and freezes typically include them. Option D is wrong because allowing all changes with rollback plans is not a freeze; it is normal change management.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: Cloud Monitoring SLOs support both request-based and windows-based SLIs. When is a windows-based SLI most appropriate for a service?
options: When the service has uniformly high request volume and you want each individual request weighted equally in the SLI calculation regardless of the time of day it was served from the backend
When the service experiences highly variable request volume and you want each minute (or other unit) to weigh equally, so a low-traffic minute with errors is not drowned out by busy minutes
When the service is internal-only and does not receive enough traffic to compute statistically meaningful availability ratios within a 30-day window using request counts as the SLI input
When the service is fronted by Cloud CDN and request-based SLIs cannot be computed because cached responses do not appear in the origin load balancer logs that Cloud Monitoring consumes
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because uniform high volume is the ideal case for request-based SLIs, not windows-based. Option B is correct: when traffic is bursty, windows-based SLIs (e.g., 'minute is good if error rate < 1%') weight each time window equally and prevent dominant-traffic periods from masking low-traffic errors. Option C is wrong because low traffic also justifies windows-based, but the volume reason is more nuanced ”” variability is the textbook trigger. Option D is wrong because Cloud CDN does not prevent request-based SLIs; load balancer logs still capture origin requests.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: A service has SLOs for availability and latency, but customer complaints persist even when both SLOs are met. Which SRE concept best explains this gap?
options: The service is over-engineered for its SLO target and is wasting reliability headroom that should be redirected to faster feature delivery rather than continuing to outperform user-visible expectations
The SLO measurement window is too short, so transient regressions affecting individual users do not surface; widening the window to 90 days will smooth out customer-experienced anomalies for everyone
Customer complaints always lag SLO measurement by one full rolling window because Cloud Monitoring averages SLI signals before publishing them to the SLO service for evaluation and alerting purposes
There is a gap between the defined SLIs and the actual critical user journeys; the SLIs do not capture all user-facing failure modes, so meeting them does not guarantee meeting user expectations
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because complaints despite SLO compliance signal under-coverage, not over-engineering. Option B is wrong because lengthening the window hides issues further; the right move is better SLI coverage. Option C is wrong because Cloud Monitoring does not introduce a window-length delay between SLI and SLO. Option D is correct: persistent complaints despite SLO compliance typically indicate the SLIs do not cover all critical user journeys and there are gaps in measurement.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: How many minutes of downtime per 30 days are permitted by a 99.95% availability SLO, assuming a windows-based SLI where each downtime minute counts equally?
options: About 8.6 minutes per 30 days, because 0.05% of a 30-day window is roughly 22 minutes and SLO windows discount the lowest-traffic third for fairness across regional usage patterns over the month
About 43.8 minutes per 30 days, because 99.95% maps to 0.05% downtime, which in a 30-day window equals exactly 43.8 minutes of allowed outage time across the entire monitoring period
About 21.6 minutes per 30 days, computed as 30 × 24 × 60 × 0.0005 = 21.6, which is the standard 'three-and-a-half nines' downtime budget commonly cited in Google Cloud SLA documentation
About 4.4 minutes per 30 days, equivalent to 99.99% availability rounded to one decimal place, since 99.95% is conventionally rounded up to four nines in compliance reporting and SLO dashboards
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because there is no 'lowest-traffic third' discount; the math is straightforward. Option B is wrong because 43.8 minutes corresponds to 99.9%, not 99.95%. Option C is correct: 30 × 24 × 60 = 43,200 minutes total; 0.0005 × 43,200 = 21.6 minutes for 99.95%. Option D is wrong because 99.95% is not 99.99%; rounding up changes the budget significantly.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: Your team is implementing a slow-burn ticket alert in Cloud Monitoring to complement an existing fast-burn page. Which configuration aligns with the SRE workbook's recommended slow-burn signal for a 30-day SLO?
options: Burn rate above 1x sustained over a 24-hour window, with a 1-hour secondary window ”” this captures sustained budget consumption above plan without triggering pages during transient noise spikes
Burn rate above 14.4x sustained over 1 hour with a 5-minute secondary window, paging immediately so that on-call engineers can investigate before user-facing impact accumulates further during the day
Burn rate above 6x sustained over 6 hours with a 30-minute secondary window, paging on-call as a moderate-burn signal that complements both fast-burn pages and slow-burn ticket-based notifications
Burn rate above 0.1x sustained over 30 days, expressed as a long-term trend on the SLO dashboard rather than as an alerting policy, so that the team reviews it during weekly operational meetings
questions.explanation: Option A is correct: the SRE workbook recommends a slow-burn ticket alert around 1x burn over a long window (commonly 3-day or 24-hour) with a shorter secondary window, intended as a ticket rather than a page. Option B is wrong because 14.4x/1h is the fast-burn page configuration. Option C is wrong because 6x/6h is the medium-burn page configuration, not a slow-burn ticket. Option D is wrong because a 0.1x burn does not consume the budget meaningfully and would never alert.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: Which characteristic best distinguishes an SLO from an SLA in the SRE framework?
options: An SLO is computed in Cloud Monitoring while an SLA is computed in BigQuery; the difference is operational tooling rather than semantic, since both express the same reliability target for a given service

An SLO is reviewed monthly by leadership while an SLA is reviewed only annually with the customer; the cadence difference reflects the lower stakes of internal targets compared to contractual commitments
An SLO is an internal reliability target used to drive engineering decisions, while an SLA is a contractual commitment to customers with financial or business consequences if the target is missed
An SLO must always be stricter than the corresponding SLA by exactly 0.1 percentage points, and Cloud Monitoring enforces this gap when both are configured against the same service object
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because both can be computed in any tool; the distinction is conceptual, not tooling-based. Option B is wrong because cadence is a side effect, not the defining difference. Option C is correct: SLO is internal and drives engineering; SLA is external/contractual with consequences. Option D is wrong because there is no fixed gap; the gap depends on business judgment, and Cloud Monitoring does not enforce it.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: You're tracking toil hours per engineer per week. Which approach is most aligned with the SRE practice of toil reduction?
options: Hide toil hours from leadership dashboards to avoid management pressure that could reduce the team's autonomy in choosing which automation work to prioritize across the quarterly planning cycle
Track toil hours visibly per engineer and per service, set a per-team target below 50%, and use overages as a backlog input that funds engineering work to reduce the underlying toil generators

Track toil hours informally without a target, since toil naturally decreases as engineers gain experience and the team becomes familiar with the service's operational patterns over the engagement lifecycle
Track toil only at the level of the on-call rotation rather than per engineer, since variations between individuals reflect personal style rather than team-level operational burden across the service
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because hiding metrics from leadership prevents support for automation investment. Option B is correct: visible measurement, a target below 50%, and using overage as backlog input creates a feedback loop that drives toil reduction. Option C is wrong because passive tracking without a target does not drive improvement. Option D is wrong because per-engineer visibility surfaces hotspots that aggregate views hide.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: A service has separate availability and latency SLOs. Availability has been at 99.95% (target 99.9%) over the past 30 days, but only 92% of requests met the 300ms latency threshold (target 99% under 300ms). Which statement is INCORRECT in describing this situation?
options: The availability SLO is being met with about 0.05 percentage points of headroom, while the latency SLO is significantly missed ”” 92% under 300ms vs a 99% target means the latency budget is fully consumed
The service cannot be declared 'healthy' overall because meeting one SLO does not compensate for missing another; each SLO has its own error budget that the team's policy is bound to apply separately

The latency miss should trigger the team's error budget policy for latency, which may include freezing latency-impacting changes or reprioritizing performance work until the budget is restored to plan

The two SLIs are mathematically inconsistent: a service with 99.95% availability cannot have only 92% of requests meet a 300ms latency target, indicating a measurement pipeline error that requires audit
questions.explanation: The question asks which is INCORRECT. Option A is a correct statement (availability met, latency missed). Option B is a correct statement (each SLO has its own budget). Option C is a correct statement (latency budget triggers latency-specific policy). Option D is the incorrect statement and therefore the right answer: availability and latency are independent dimensions. A service can be highly available (low 5xx rate) while still being slow (high latency miss rate). The two SLIs are not mathematically linked in the way described, and no pipeline error is implied.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: Which item below is the best example of a leading indicator (as opposed to a lagging indicator) of reliability risk for a production service?
options: Sustained increase in error budget burn rate above plan during the past week, signaling that current change velocity or operational conditions are eroding reliability before the SLO is breached outright
Number of SLA credits issued to customers in the previous quarter, which directly reflects the financial impact of past incidents that have already affected the customer experience and contract terms
Mean time to recovery (MTTR) for incidents closed in the prior month, which describes how quickly the team responded to events that have already concluded and entered the postmortem process
Total downtime minutes attributed to the service in the previous rolling 30-day window, which is a direct measurement of customer-facing unavailability that has already been experienced and recorded
questions.explanation: Option A is correct: burn rate is a leading indicator ”” it warns of risk before the SLO is breached. Option B is wrong because SLA credits are lagging ”” they describe past impact. Option C is wrong because MTTR describes past incidents and is lagging. Option D is wrong because past downtime is by definition a lagging measurement of impact already experienced.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: You're designing an SLO for a batch data pipeline that runs every 6 hours on Dataflow. Which SLI formulation best fits a batch workload, per SRE guidance?
options: Percentage of HTTP 200 responses returned by the pipeline's gRPC control plane, since the pipeline orchestrator emits status calls that can be measured the same way as a user-facing API endpoint
Percentage of pipeline runs that complete successfully within the freshness target (e.g., output produced within 90 minutes of scheduled start) over the trailing measurement window of the service

Average CPU utilization of pipeline workers across the run, since saturation is the dominant reliability signal for batch jobs and tracks closely with the probability of partial output corruption events
Number of distinct error messages logged to Cloud Logging per pipeline run, since reducing the diversity of errors over time correlates with overall reliability improvements in the pipeline codebase
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because gRPC control-plane status is not the user-facing concern for a batch pipeline; data freshness is. Option B is correct: batch SLIs measure freshness/completeness ”” what fraction of scheduled runs delivered the expected output on time. Option C is wrong because CPU is a saturation signal, not a user-facing SLI. Option D is wrong because counting distinct error messages does not measure user-facing outcomes; it is an internal diagnostic at best.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: Your service has a strict 99.99% availability SLO. Engineers are reluctant to deploy because every release carries some risk. Which SRE practice would best reconcile this tension?
options: Lower the SLO to 99.9% so that the higher allowed error rate gives engineers more room to ship; reliability budgets are arbitrary internal targets and can be relaxed when they constrain delivery
Increase the deployment review board's authority to require sign-off from a director on every release, ensuring no risky change ships without senior approval and slowing change cadence overall
Adopt progressive delivery: canary releases, gradual percentage rollouts, and automated rollback gated on SLI signals, so that releases consume only the necessary fraction of the error budget
Move all deployments to a single fixed change window every two weeks so that incidents are concentrated in known time periods and the team can staff response capacity exclusively during them
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because relaxing the SLO without user-experience justification is not best practice; it solves the symptom by lowering the bar. Option B is wrong because additional gating slows delivery without reducing risk per release. Option C is correct: progressive delivery limits blast radius per release and lets the team ship more confidently within the budget. Option D is wrong because fixed change windows batch risk and remove the ability to deploy fixes quickly when needed.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: Which of the following is the most accurate statement about the relationship between SLOs and customer happiness in SRE practice?
options: Customer happiness is exactly proportional to SLO achievement: every additional nine of reliability translates linearly into measurable improvement in user satisfaction across the entire customer base

Customer happiness is uncorrelated with SLO achievement because users perceive reliability through interface quality and feature richness, not through underlying availability metrics that they cannot observe

Customer happiness is unaffected by reliability once a baseline is met; the SRE goal is therefore to reduce reliability investment to that baseline and redirect all freed engineering effort to feature work

Customer happiness is bounded above by a 'happy line' ”” beyond a certain SLO threshold, additional reliability yields diminishing returns and the cost of more nines exceeds the user-perceptible benefit
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because the relationship is not linear; users do not feel each nine equally. Option B is wrong because reliability does affect users ”” they notice outages and slowness. Option C is wrong because reducing to the baseline alone misses the point ”” the goal is to find the right balance, not to underinvest. Option D is correct: the SRE happy-line concept holds that beyond a threshold, more reliability yields diminishing user-perceived benefit relative to cost.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: You see a Cloud Monitoring alert configured to fire when CPU utilization on any GKE node exceeds 80% for 5 minutes. From an SRE perspective, which is the strongest criticism of this alert?
options: It is too narrow: the 5-minute window misses short spikes that could be diagnostic indicators of memory leak progression, which often manifests as brief CPU bursts before sustained high utilization eventually
It is a cause-based alert disconnected from user impact; high CPU is not user-facing and can be normal during peak traffic, so paging on it alone generates noise that does not reflect SLI degradation

It is missing a separate alert for memory utilization above 75%, which is the more reliable saturation signal because CPU autoscaling will absorb most CPU spikes before they translate to user-visible problems

It is incorrectly scoped to GKE nodes rather than pods; CPU on the node represents aggregate scheduler pressure, and the alert should instead track per-pod CPU limits to detect throttled application containers
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because shortening the window adds noise rather than fixing the core issue. Option B is correct: this is cause-based, not symptom-based; CPU high does not mean users are affected, and the alert may fire during expected peaks. Option C is wrong because adding more cause-based alerts compounds noise rather than improving signal. Option D is wrong because while pod-level granularity may be useful, the deeper problem is that CPU is not a user-facing SLI.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: Which best describes a 'blameless postmortem' as practiced in SRE?
options: A postmortem that focuses on systemic causes and contributing factors rather than individual blame, on the assumption that engineers act with good intent and that systems should be hardened against errors
A postmortem that omits the names of individuals involved in the incident response so the document can be shared externally with customers without risk of attribution to the engineers who paged in

A postmortem that proceeds only when no one is identified as responsible for the failure, since accountability conflicts with the psychological safety needed for honest discussion of contributing factors

A postmortem that uses a fixed template and avoids open-ended discussion, ensuring that legal review remains uncomplicated and that the record produced is acceptable to compliance auditors at any time
questions.explanation: Option A is correct: blameless postmortems focus on systemic causes and assume good intent, leading to system hardening rather than individual punishment. Option B is wrong because blamelessness is not about hiding names; it is about not punishing individuals. Option C is wrong because blameless â‰  no accountability; the focus is on systems, not absence of identification. Option D is wrong because rigid templates and legal-driven structure are not the point of blamelessness.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: Cloud Monitoring's SLO service exposes a burn rate metric and an SLI quality metric. Which scenario would benefit most from a multi-window, multi-burn-rate alerting policy rather than a single threshold alert?
options: A service with extremely low and uniform traffic where any single failed request is statistically meaningful, since multi-window alerting introduces unnecessary delay in pages for low-volume services
A service where the SLI is computed once per day in a scheduled BigQuery job, since multi-window logic requires sub-second SLI evaluation that is incompatible with daily batch computation pipelines

A service that experiences both transient bursts of errors and sustained degradations, where a single threshold would either page too often on bursts or miss sustained slow burns that erode budget over time

A service with a single critical dependency whose health alone determines availability, since the dependency's status check provides a cleaner signal than any SLO-derived burn rate computed downstream
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because low-volume services are actually a harder case; multi-window helps but is not the textbook fit. Option B is wrong because multi-window/multi-burn-rate alerting is supported by Cloud Monitoring's SLO service in real time, not via batch SQL. Option C is correct: multi-window alerting addresses exactly the case where the service has both fast spikes and slow drifts; it balances sensitivity and noise. Option D is wrong because pure dependency-health alerting is cause-based and ignores SLO framing.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: A team proposes adding an automated rollback in Cloud Deploy that triggers when the error budget burn rate exceeds 10x during the first hour after a release. Which SRE consideration most supports this design?
options: It guarantees no production change can ever consume more than 5% of the monthly budget, since the rollback fires before sustained burn rates exceed that threshold for any single release on the service

It ensures rollbacks happen only during business hours, since burn rate calculations require manual confirmation by the release manager before automation can take destructive action on the production deployment

It guarantees mean time to recovery (MTTR) of less than 60 seconds for every incident in the service, since rollback automation is faster than any human response could be during an active incident page

It limits the blast radius of any single release by tying rollback to SLI-driven consumption rather than human judgment, so risky changes that degrade users are reverted quickly without waiting for an on-call page
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because the 5% guarantee depends on traffic and timing; the policy provides containment, not a hard mathematical cap. Option B is wrong because automated rollback's value comes from running 24/7, not just business hours. Option C is wrong because MTTR depends on incident type; only release-induced regressions are caught here. Option D is correct: tying rollback to SLI burn limits release blast radius and reduces dependence on human alerting before action.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: Which is the best example of a 'symptom' to alert on, as opposed to a 'cause', for a public-facing web application?
options: Number of TCP retransmissions on the VPC interconnect link between the GKE cluster and the upstream Cloud SQL instance, since networking issues are the most common root cause of application latency problems

Increase in 5xx HTTP error rate above the SLO threshold, observed at the external load balancer where it represents user-facing failure regardless of which backend component is producing the error condition

CPU saturation on the busiest GKE node exceeding 85% for 10 minutes, since CPU pressure typically precedes user-visible degradation by 2-3 minutes and gives the team time to intervene before any impact

Number of failed liveness probes on application pods exceeding 5 per minute, since probe failures indicate that Kubernetes is restarting pods and that the application is in an unhealthy operational state
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because TCP retransmissions are a cause/internal signal ”” they may or may not correlate with user impact. Option B is correct: 5xx rate at the load balancer is a user-facing symptom that maps directly to availability SLI. Option C is wrong because CPU is a saturation cause; alerting on it generates noise. Option D is wrong because liveness probe failures are internal signals; they may not produce user-visible impact if other replicas are serving.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: Your team is debating whether to track availability as a calendar-month SLO (resets at month boundary) or a rolling 30-day SLO. Which is the strongest argument for the rolling window?
options: A rolling window avoids the perverse incentive of an end-of-month splurge: with a calendar reset, teams sometimes burn remaining budget aggressively in the last days; rolling windows keep behavior more consistent

A rolling window is the only configuration supported by Cloud Monitoring's SLO service, since the service stores window metadata internally and does not expose calendar-aligned boundaries for SLO objects

A rolling window aligns with billing cycles in Google Cloud Billing, since invoices are generated on a 30-day rolling basis and reliability reporting should match the same cadence for cost-aware reviews

A rolling window automatically excludes scheduled maintenance windows from the SLI computation, while calendar SLOs count maintenance as downtime, leading to artificially low availability for services under change
questions.explanation: Option A is correct: rolling windows avoid behavioral discontinuities at month boundaries (e.g., 'we have budget left, spend it'). Option B is wrong because Cloud Monitoring supports both calendar-aligned and rolling SLOs. Option C is wrong because Google Cloud Billing operates on calendar months, not rolling 30-day periods. Option D is wrong because neither window type automatically excludes maintenance ”” that requires explicit SLI definition logic.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: A high-traffic API has an availability SLO of 99.9% and a latency SLO of '95% of requests under 200ms'. Which composite scenario over the past 30 days indicates the service is meeting both SLOs?
options: Availability ratio 99.85% with 96% of requests under 200ms, since latency exceeds plan but availability remains within 0.05 percentage points of the target which is within typical measurement noise

Availability ratio 99.95% with 99% of requests under 500ms, since most users perceive a 500ms threshold as similarly responsive and the latency SLO can be reinterpreted under the same definition framework
Availability ratio 99.92% with 96% of requests under 200ms, since both numbers are above their respective SLO targets (99.9% and 95%) and the service is meeting both stated reliability commitments

Availability ratio 99.95% with 94% of requests under 200ms, since the latency miss is small (1 percentage point) and availability comfortably exceeds the SLO target by 0.05 percentage points in the window
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because 99.85% availability is below the 99.9% target ”” the service is missing availability. Option B is wrong because 99% under 500ms is not the SLI defined; you cannot reinterpret thresholds post hoc. Option C is correct: 99.92% > 99.9% and 96% > 95%, so both SLOs are met. Option D is wrong because 94% under 200ms is below the 95% target ”” the service is missing latency regardless of availability headroom.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: Your service handles 10,000 requests per minute on average. You want to detect a fast-burn event consuming 2% of the monthly budget in 1 hour. What burn-rate threshold corresponds to that, for a 30-day SLO window?
options: Approximately 2x burn rate, since consuming 2% of the budget in 1 hour represents twice the linear consumption pace required to spread the full budget evenly across all 720 hours in the window
Approximately 24x burn rate, computed as 2% × 30 days × 24 hours / 1 hour, which simplifies to 14.4 but is conventionally rounded up to 24 to allow for measurement margin within Cloud Monitoring
Approximately 4.8x burn rate, derived by dividing the 2% target by the 1-hour observation window expressed as a fraction of the 30-day SLO measurement period used by Cloud Monitoring's SLO service
Approximately 14.4x burn rate, derived from (2% × 30-day total minutes) / (1-hour minutes / 30-day total minutes), which is the canonical fast-burn threshold cited by the SRE workbook for this case
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because 2x would consume only ~0.28% of the budget per hour, not 2%. Option B is wrong because 24x is not the correct math; conventional rounding to 24 is not how the canonical figure is derived. Option C is wrong because 4.8x understates the rate. Option D is correct: the canonical SRE workbook figure for 2% budget consumption in 1 hour over a 30-day window is 14.4x burn rate.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: You are negotiating an SLO with a downstream team that depends on your service. They request 99.99% availability, but your current performance is 99.9%. What is the most SRE-aligned response?
options: Agree to the 99.99% target immediately, since SRE practice prioritizes downstream customer satisfaction over engineering cost; the team will figure out how to deliver the additional reliability over time
Discuss the business cost of additional nines: moving from 99.9% to 99.99% typically requires multi-region active-active design and 5-10x cost, then jointly decide whether the value justifies it
Refuse the request and require the downstream team to design their service to tolerate your current 99.9% availability, since their dependency on your service is their architectural choice to manage

Counter with 99.95% as a compromise without analysis, since splitting the difference between current performance and the requested target is a fair negotiation tactic in any SLO discussion with consumers
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because agreeing without analysis commits to expensive engineering work without justification. Option B is correct: SRE practice frames reliability as a business decision with cost; surfacing the cost lets both teams agree on the appropriate level. Option C is wrong because refusing without negotiation skips the joint decision; downstream teams often have legitimate needs. Option D is wrong because splitting the difference without analysis is arbitrary and not SRE practice.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: Which is the best example of toil reduction work that an SRE team would prioritize?
options: Building automation that rotates Cloud KMS keys for the team's services on a schedule, replacing a manual quarterly process that an engineer currently performs by following a 40-step runbook each cycle

Adding a new dashboard panel to Cloud Monitoring that shows request rates per region, since better observability is a general good and improves the team's situational awareness over time across services
Refactoring the team's main service codebase to use a newer Go version, since technical debt reduction is generally valuable and aligns with the team's long-term engineering goals across the platform
Migrating documentation from one wiki platform to another, since centralization will reduce the time engineers spend searching for runbooks and operational procedures across multiple tools each week
questions.explanation: Option A is correct: automating a manual, repetitive, scheduled procedure that follows a known runbook is canonical toil reduction. Option B is wrong because adding a dashboard panel is useful observability work but not directly toil reduction. Option C is wrong because Go version upgrades are technical debt reduction, not toil reduction. Option D is wrong because wiki migration is a one-time project, not ongoing toil elimination.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: How should an SRE team handle a service that is consistently exceeding its SLO target (e.g., delivering 99.99% when the SLO is 99.9%)?
options: Tighten the SLO to match observed performance, since the consumed budget is effectively zero and downstream teams have come to expect the higher reliability whether or not it is contractually committed

Maintain the SLO and continue investing the same engineering effort in reliability, since exceeding the SLO provides safety margin in case future conditions change and reliability degrades unexpectedly
Investigate whether engineering effort can be redirected to feature work, and consider intentionally injecting controlled risk (chaos) to validate that the budget is being meaningfully consumed

Reduce monitoring spend by disabling some SLI computations, since clearly the service is operating well above target and continuing to compute fine-grained SLI data is unnecessary operational overhead now
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because reflexively tightening the SLO penalizes the team and may create future trouble when conditions change. Option B is wrong because maintaining the status quo without analysis can mean over-investment in reliability at the expense of velocity. Option C is correct: consistent over-delivery suggests resources may be better deployed elsewhere; some teams introduce controlled fault injection (chaos) to validate the budget is meaningful. Option D is wrong because cutting observability is a false economy and reduces ability to react to future change.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: Which Cloud Monitoring construct lets you compute a single error budget across multiple SLOs for a service (for example, combining availability and latency into one budget for policy purposes)?
options: A composite uptime check that AND-combines multiple endpoint probes into one signal and reports the joint result, which Cloud Monitoring then exposes as a single derived SLI for the service object
A burn rate alerting policy that references multiple SLO objects with logical OR; the policy fires when any referenced SLO's burn rate exceeds threshold and treats the union as a combined budget
Cloud Monitoring's MQL aggregation feature with the join() operator, which combines latency and availability metrics into a single time series that can be exported to BigQuery for budget reporting

Multiple SLOs attached to a single Service object; budgets can be reviewed jointly via a Service-level dashboard, but each SLO still owns its own budget ”” there is no native single combined budget metric
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because uptime checks are probe-based, not SLI-based, and do not combine SLO budgets. Option B is wrong because a multi-SLO alerting policy can OR across SLOs, but it does not produce a combined budget; it just unifies alerting. Option C is wrong because MQL can join metrics but does not create a combined budget concept that Cloud Monitoring tracks natively. Option D is correct: Cloud Monitoring supports multiple SLOs per Service object, but each retains its own budget; review is joint but accounting is per SLO.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the role of a 'service level' in SRE: SLI, SLO, and SLA?
options: SLI is the measurement (e.g., 99.92% of requests succeeded), SLO is the internal target (e.g., 99.9% must succeed), and SLA is the contractual commitment with consequences if the target is missed

SLI is the alerting policy, SLO is the dashboard, and SLA is the service-level objective documented in the runbook; the three are aspects of the same Cloud Monitoring SLO object configured for any service

SLI is set by the customer, SLO is set by the SRE team, and SLA is the negotiated middle ground that both parties agree on at the start of every quarter during the operational review cycle for the service
SLI is computed from logs, SLO is computed from metrics, and SLA is computed from billing data; the three differ primarily by the data source they consume rather than what they represent semantically
questions.explanation: Option A is correct: SLI = the measurement, SLO = the internal target, SLA = the external contract with consequences. Option B is wrong because alerting, dashboards, and runbooks are tools, not the SLI/SLO/SLA hierarchy. Option C is wrong because customers do not set SLIs and the negotiation model described is inaccurate. Option D is wrong because the difference is semantic (measurement vs target vs contract), not the data source.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: A team is asked to choose an SLI window length: 7 days, 30 days, or 90 days. Which factor most strongly favors a longer window (e.g., 90 days)?
options: Frequent small incidents that consume budget gradually but never cause a single major outage, since long windows smooth out reactivity and reduce alert noise during normal day-to-day operational variance

Rare but high-impact incidents that have low probability per week but meaningful probability over a quarter, since a longer window better captures the long-tail risk profile of such infrequent failure modes
Highly elastic traffic patterns that produce 10x variation between weekdays and weekends, since longer windows weight every day equally and remove distortion from short-window peak-trough comparisons

Strict regulatory reporting requirements that demand 7-day reporting cadence, since 90-day windows align reporting periods naturally and ensure consistent results between auditor reviews and team dashboards
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because frequent small incidents are well-handled by shorter windows; long windows could mask them. Option B is correct: rare high-impact events have low per-week but meaningful per-quarter probability; longer windows better capture their effect on the SLO. Option C is wrong because traffic variation is addressed by SLI design (windows-based vs request-based), not window length. Option D is wrong because 90-day windows do not align with 7-day reporting; they conflict.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: Which of the following SLI types is best suited for measuring data correctness in a pipeline that processes financial transactions, where occasional duplicate or malformed records are the primary user concern?
options: Availability SLI as the ratio of processing pipeline minutes where at least one record was ingested successfully, since financial pipelines must keep moving and total stalls are the primary risk to address

Latency SLI as the median time between transaction creation and downstream availability in the data warehouse, since faster delivery is the strongest user-facing concern in financial transaction workloads
Saturation SLI as CPU and memory utilization of the pipeline workers, since financial pipelines fail under saturation and capacity planning is the dominant reliability concern for the workload type

Correctness/quality SLI as the ratio of records that pass downstream validation (no duplicates, schema-valid, balanced totals) to total records processed in the rolling measurement window for the pipeline
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because availability of processing does not measure correctness; a pipeline can run while producing bad data. Option B is wrong because latency is not the stated user concern; duplicate/malformed records are. Option C is wrong because saturation is an internal signal, not a user-facing SLI for correctness. Option D is correct: the SLI should directly measure correctness ”” fraction of valid records produced ”” because that maps to the stated user concern.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: Your team wants to alert when 5% of the monthly error budget is consumed in 6 hours. For a 30-day SLO window, what burn-rate threshold matches this configuration in the SRE workbook?
options: Approximately 14.4x burn rate, since the SRE workbook standardizes fast-burn pages at this threshold regardless of the specific percentage and window combination being chosen for the alerting policy
Approximately 1x burn rate, since 5% of the budget over 6 hours is roughly the linear consumption pace; alerting at 1x would catch any deviation from the expected pace of budget consumption over time
Approximately 6x burn rate, derived from (5% × 30 days × 24 hours) / 6 hours = 6, which is the canonical medium-burn-rate page configuration in the SRE workbook for 30-day rolling SLO windows

Approximately 30x burn rate, since 5% in 6 hours is a steep slope; setting the alert at 30x ensures sensitivity to even faster degradation modes while preserving headroom under the standard fast-burn threshold
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because 14.4x is the 2%/1h threshold, not 5%/6h. Option B is wrong because 1x is linear consumption (full budget over full window), much slower than 5% in 6 hours. Option C is correct: 5% × (30 × 24) / 6 = 6x burn rate is the canonical medium-burn page. Option D is wrong because 30x overstates the rate; the math gives 6x.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: Which of the following is the most appropriate response when a postmortem reveals that an outage was caused by a configuration push that bypassed the canary stage?
options: Mandate that the engineer who pushed the change attend a one-on-one with the SRE manager and complete a remedial training course on Cloud Deploy before performing any future production deployments

Add a Cloud Deploy gate that blocks promotion to production unless the canary stage has completed with success metrics within configured tolerances; treat the gap as a system weakness, not an individual mistake

Disable all configuration pushes outside of business hours and require a second engineer to approve each push, ensuring that pair approval prevents bypass of the canary stage in any future deployment scenario

Update the runbook to remind engineers to always use the canary stage and circulate the postmortem broadly so the engineering organization is aware that bypassing canary is discouraged in future releases
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because individual remediation is not blameless and does not fix the system; the next engineer could repeat the mistake. Option B is correct: treat the bypass as a system weakness and engineer a gate that makes the bad path impossible ”” canonical SRE response. Option C is wrong because business-hours restrictions and pair approval do not fix the bypass possibility; they layer process around the gap. Option D is wrong because runbook reminders are a weak control compared to a deployment-system gate.
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: What is the standard relationship between availability percentages and downtime per year (sometimes called 'nines of reliability')?
options: 99.9% (three nines) allows about 8.76 hours of downtime per year, 99.99% allows about 52.6 minutes, and 99.999% allows about 5.26 minutes per year ”” each additional nine reduces downtime by ~10x
99.9% allows about 1 hour of downtime per year, 99.99% allows about 6 minutes, and 99.999% allows about 36 seconds ”” each nine reduces downtime by ~10x but the baseline is lower than commonly cited
99.9% allows about 24 hours per year, 99.99% allows about 2.4 hours, and 99.999% allows about 14 minutes ”” the higher baseline reflects realistic downtime patterns observed across cloud providers
99.9% allows about 4.38 hours per year, 99.99% allows about 26 minutes, and 99.999% allows about 2.6 minutes ”” figures are derived from a 6-month operating year rather than the full calendar year
questions.explanation: Option A is correct: 0.1% of 8,760 hours = 8.76 hours/year; 0.01% × 8,760 hours × 60 = 52.56 minutes; 0.001% × 8,760 × 60 = 5.26 minutes. Option B is wrong because 1 hour per year corresponds to ~99.989%, not 99.9%. Option C is wrong because 24 hours/year would be ~99.726% availability, not 99.9%. Option D is wrong because a 6-month operating year is not the standard; the canonical figures use 8,760 hours (full year).
questions.category: Applying site reliability engineering principles to a service

questions.question: You are designing an SLI for a streaming workload where users hold long-lived WebSocket connections. The team proposes several SLI shapes. Which approach is the WORST choice for measuring user-facing reliability of an active session?
