Place the manifest in a separate Git branch named prod and configure each cluster's RootSync to track a branch matching its environment label, so prod clusters see prod manifests only
Use Helm value overrides per cluster pulled at sync time from a Cloud Storage bucket, which Config Sync evaluates before applying so cluster labels control which manifests render
Set up a Cloud Build trigger that pushes the manifest to the 10 prod clusters' RepoSyncs via gcloud kubectl apply, bypassing Config Sync entirely for environment-scoped deployments
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because Config Sync's ClusterSelector lets you select clusters by label and annotate manifests with the selector to scope which clusters apply which resources from one repo. Option B works but doubles the repo proliferation and breaks the single-source-of-truth premise. Option C is wrong because Config Sync does not pull Helm overrides from Cloud Storage as part of sync. Option D is wrong because bypassing Config Sync defeats the GitOps model.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: You want Cloud Build to record provenance metadata that proves which source commit, build steps, and inputs produced a given image, satisfying SLSA level 3 expectations. Which feature does this?
options: Cloud Build's built-in SLSA provenance generation, which produces a signed in-toto provenance attestation tied to the image and stored as a Container Analysis occurrence automatically
A custom final step in cloudbuild.yaml that writes a JSON file describing the build inputs and uploads it to a Cloud Storage bucket, where it is signed by Cloud KMS asynchronously
Cloud Audit Logs entries for the Cloud Build API, which capture sufficient detail about build steps and inputs to reconstruct provenance for any image during a forensic investigation
Artifact Registry's image manifest annotations, which Cloud Build populates with the source commit hash and the cloudbuild.yaml SHA as part of the standard image push operation
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because Cloud Build offers built-in SLSA provenance generation that creates a signed in-toto attestation tied to the image and stores it via Container Analysis, satisfying SLSA L3 expectations. Option B is wrong because hand-rolled JSON provenance is not signed by the build system itself and doesn't meet the cryptographic chain required for SLSA L3. Option C is wrong because audit logs aren't a cryptographic provenance attestation. Option D is wrong because manifest annotations are metadata but not signed provenance.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Your application is split across three Cloud Run services that must roll out together for a release. You want a single Cloud Deploy delivery pipeline to deploy all three services and treat them as one promotion unit. Which configuration supports this?
options: Create three independent delivery pipelines and an orchestrator Cloud Function that promotes all three on a fixed schedule, since Cloud Deploy supports only one service per pipeline at this time
Use a single delivery pipeline with a multi-service Cloud Run target listing all three services, where each phase rolls out and verifies all services together as one atomic promotion unit
Use a Cloud Deploy custom target that wraps the three Cloud Run services in a Skaffold-rendered package, with a build-time check that all three image tags are part of the same release
Use a single delivery pipeline pointed at one Cloud Run service that itself calls the other two via Cloud Build triggers, daisy-chaining promotions across services as part of one release
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because the one-service-per-pipeline claim is outdated and an orchestrator function is unnecessary complexity. Option B describes a multi-service target capability that has known constraints and is not how Cloud Deploy models multi-service releases canonically. Option C is correct because Cloud Deploy custom targets, combined with Skaffold rendering and a release bundle that includes all three image references, allow atomic promotion of multi-service releases. Option D is wrong because daisy-chaining loses release atomicity and the audit trail.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: You want to limit which container image registries pods on a GKE cluster can pull from, allowing only your organization's Artifact Registry repository. Which mechanism enforces this at admission time?
options: A NetworkPolicy that allows egress only to Artifact Registry's external IP ranges, blocking pulls from any other registry by preventing the cluster from reaching the upstream endpoint
A GKE node pool image policy that lists allowed pull-from registry domains in the node configuration, so the kubelet refuses to pull images from non-listed sources at run time
A Binary Authorization policy whose default rule is DISALLOW with an admission rule allowing only images whose registry prefix matches the organization's Artifact Registry path
An Org Policy constraint constraints/gke.allowedImageRegistries set at the folder level, which the GKE control plane enforces by rejecting pod specs referencing other registries
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because NetworkPolicy works at the pod network level, not on the kubelet's registry pulls, and IP-based allowlists are brittle for global services. Option B is wrong because there is no kubelet-level allowed-domain config of that form at the node pool level. Option C is correct because Binary Authorization's allowed-image-pattern admission rules can restrict admission to images whose registry path matches an allowed prefix. Option D is wrong because there is no constraint with that exact name; admission is enforced by Binary Authorization, not Org Policy.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: A platform team distributes a base Docker image hourly via Cloud Build and Artifact Registry. Downstream service teams want to be automatically notified when a new base image is published. Which native pattern enables this?
options: Subscribe downstream teams to the Pub/Sub topic Artifact Registry publishes to on image push events, filtered by the base image repository so only relevant pushes notify subscribers
Configure Cloud Build of the base image to send an email via SendGrid in a final step, listing all downstream teams' addresses as static recipients in the cloudbuild.yaml manifest
Have each service team's Cloud Build trigger poll Artifact Registry every five minutes via gcloud artifacts docker images list and detect new digests by comparing against a local file
Use Cloud Tasks queues that the base image pipeline enqueues on push, with each downstream service registering a worker that processes the task to refresh its derived images
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because Artifact Registry publishes push events to a Pub/Sub topic that downstream pipelines can subscribe to with filters, enabling fan-out notifications without polling. Option B is wrong because static email recipient lists in cloudbuild.yaml don't scale and add a third-party dependency. Option C is wrong because polling wastes quota and adds latency compared to native push notifications. Option D is wrong because Cloud Tasks is for orchestrating asynchronous unit-of-work execution, not native registry change notifications.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Your release process must guarantee that the binary deployed to prod is bit-for-bit identical to the one tested in staging. Which combination of practices satisfies this requirement most directly?
options: Run a Cloud Build job at each promotion stage that rebuilds from the same Git SHA, compares the resulting digest to the previous stage's, and rolls back automatically on any drift
Promote the same Cloud Deploy release across targets so the same artifact digest is referenced everywhere, and reference images by digest (not tag) in deployment manifests
Use Skaffold's skaffold promote command between environments, which forces a re-render and re-tag of images using the destination environment's name to ensure traceability
Use Cloud Source Repositories' built-in artifact promotion feature, which copies the binary from a staging-named branch to a prod-named branch and verifies a SHA-256 checksum
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because rebuilding introduces variability (build environment, transient dependencies) even from the same SHA ”” that's the very risk the requirement targets. Option B is correct because Cloud Deploy promotes the same immutable release across targets, and digest-pinned manifests ensure the same content runs everywhere. Option C is wrong because no skaffold promote command re-tags between environments as described, and re-rendering doesn't preserve binary identity. Option D is wrong because Cloud Source Repositories has no built-in artifact promotion via branch copy.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: You're standing up a Cloud Build trigger that uses a GitHub repository (not Cloud Source Repositories). Which authentication mechanism does Google currently recommend for the connection?
options: A personal access token (PAT) generated by an admin and stored as a secret in Secret Manager, which Cloud Build reads at trigger creation to set up the GitHub webhook integration
An SSH deploy key uploaded to the GitHub repository and referenced by the trigger via the credentials field, allowing Cloud Build to clone and receive webhook events from GitHub
Anonymous public-repo access through Cloud Build's default service account, since GitHub webhooks do not require authentication to push events to a Cloud Build webhook endpoint
An OAuth-based GitHub connection that links your Google project to a GitHub App installation, which scopes access to specific repositories without needing long-lived PATs anywhere
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because PATs are long-lived secrets tied to a single admin user and are no longer the recommended pattern. Option B is wrong because SSH deploy keys handle clone but not the bidirectional webhook trust model Cloud Build needs. Option C is wrong because authenticated, signed webhooks are required and anonymous access is not the model. Option D is correct because the GitHub App-based OAuth connection is the current Google-recommended path, providing repo-scoped access without long-lived tokens.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Cloud Build pipelines for ML workflows often need to call Vertex AI training jobs and wait for completion. Which approach handles long-running training cleanly inside a build?
options: Run gcloud ai custom-jobs create with the --async flag in one step, then a separate later step polls gcloud ai custom-jobs describe in a loop until the job ends or the build times out
Use a Cloud Composer DAG instead of Cloud Build, since Cloud Build cannot wait for Vertex AI jobs and explicitly times out any step that exceeds 30 minutes regardless of build timeout
Submit the training job from Cloud Build with a synchronous gcloud ai custom-jobs create command (no --async flag), so the command blocks until completion and surfaces exit status
Schedule the Vertex AI job via Cloud Scheduler with a payload built by Cloud Build, then have a Pub/Sub subscriber in the build container resume the pipeline when the job emits a finish event
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because the polling loop pattern works but is more code than the synchronous flag and is unnecessary when blocking submission is available. Option B is wrong because Cloud Build steps run for as long as the build's overall timeout allows, which is configurable well beyond 30 minutes. Option C is correct because submitting the Vertex AI training job synchronously blocks the build step until completion, surfacing exit status cleanly. Option D is wrong because once Cloud Build's worker is decommissioned, no in-build subscriber can resume the pipeline.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: You configure a Cloud Build trigger to require approval before running, so commits don't auto-trigger costly builds. Where does the approval step live in the build lifecycle, and who can approve?
options: Approval is part of cloudbuild.yaml as a special wait-for-approval step type; any user with the cloudbuild.builds.approve permission on the build resource can approve in the Console
Approval is a property of the trigger itself; when approvalConfig.approvalRequired is true, builds enter a pending state and only principals with cloudbuild.builds.approve can release them
Approval is enforced via an Org Policy constraint applied to the project, and only Organization Admins can approve individual builds because the constraint is scoped at the org level
Approval is configured per build via the gcloud builds submit --approval-required flag at submission time, after which any member of the project's Owners group can approve the build
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because approval is a trigger-level property, not a build-step type in cloudbuild.yaml. Option B is correct because approvalRequired on the trigger puts builds into a pending state until a principal with cloudbuild.builds.approve releases them. Option C is wrong because no Org Policy constraint governs per-build approval; that's the trigger's responsibility. Option D is wrong because the flag is set on the trigger config, not on each gcloud submit call, and Owners is not the canonical approver role.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Your team uses a remote Artifact Registry repository to cache Docker Hub images. A new corporate policy requires that any image deployed to prod come from your internal registry only, never directly from Docker Hub. How does the remote repository help, and what else is needed?
options: The remote repository proxies Docker Hub and caches images on first pull, so internal Dockerfiles reference your internal registry path; pair this with Binary Authorization to deny non-org registry paths
The remote repository automatically blocks Docker Hub pulls from outside the organization at the network layer, so once configured no further policy is required to enforce the corporate rule
The remote repository requires a one-time mirror of all desired upstream images by an operator, after which it operates as a fully offline mirror with no upstream connectivity for security
The remote repository exposes a webhook that fires when Docker Hub images change, and your CI listens to it to copy images explicitly into a standard repository before each prod release
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because the remote repository transparently proxies and caches upstream images so internal references stay internal, and Binary Authorization complements it by denying any non-allowed registry path at admission. Option B is wrong because the remote repository does not block external pulls; it provides a controlled path through your registry. Option C is wrong because remote repositories pull on demand and don't require a full preload. Option D is wrong because the standard pattern is on-demand caching, not webhook-driven copying.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: You want a Cloud Build pipeline to dynamically pick the test framework based on the language detected in the pushed repository, without maintaining one cloudbuild.yaml per language. Which feature best supports this?
options: Use a single cloudbuild.yaml with a series of optional steps each guarded by a shell conditional that detects language markers (package.json, go.mod, pyproject.toml) and runs the matching tests
Use Cloud Build trigger substitutions to compute the language at queue time from a regex on the changed files, then route to a language-specific cloudbuild-{lang}.yaml referenced in the trigger
Use Cloud Deploy custom targets keyed by language so the test step runs as part of the target rendering rather than in Cloud Build, which has no support for per-language test selection logic
Use Skaffold's per-language profiles selected by skaffold dev --auto-detect-language, which automatically picks the right Cloud Build step set when invoked from a Cloud Build trigger script
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because a single cloudbuild.yaml with shell-conditional steps that detect language markers is the simplest, most maintainable approach and stays within one file. Option B is wrong because trigger substitutions cannot dynamically pick a yaml file based on repo content at queue time. Option C is wrong because Cloud Deploy targets are about delivery to environments, not language-specific test selection. Option D is wrong because skaffold dev is for local inner-loop development and there is no --auto-detect-language flag of this form.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: A Cloud Build step needs to authenticate to a third-party SaaS API. The SaaS provider supports federated OIDC tokens. Which Google Cloud feature should you use?
options: Store a long-lived API key for the SaaS in Secret Manager, mount it into the build step as a masked env var, and rotate it quarterly to limit blast radius if the key is ever leaked

Use Workload Identity Federation, where the SaaS trusts Google Cloud as an OIDC provider; the build step requests a Google ID token and presents it to the SaaS, eliminating long-lived credentials entirely
Use Cloud KMS to sign a short-lived JWT in the build step that the SaaS validates against a configured public key, treating each build as a new identity for audit purposes within the SaaS
Use IAM impersonation to mint short-lived access tokens from a Google service account that the SaaS allowlists by email; the SaaS verifies these tokens via the standard Google tokeninfo endpoint
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because long-lived API keys are exactly what federated OIDC tokens replace; the question states the provider supports OIDC. Option B is correct because Workload Identity Federation lets the SaaS trust Google as an OIDC provider and the build presents a short-lived ID token, eliminating long-lived credentials. Option C is wrong because hand-rolled KMS-signed JWTs reinvent OIDC poorly and require custom verification. Option D is wrong because Google access tokens aren't standard OIDC ID tokens the SaaS would natively verify via tokeninfo.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Your Cloud Deploy delivery pipeline targets a GKE cluster in another project. Which IAM bindings must exist for Cloud Deploy to perform the rollout successfully?
options: The Cloud Deploy service account in the delivery pipeline's project must have Owner on the target project, since deploying workloads to GKE requires broad administrative permissions across cluster resources

The end user promoting the release must have Kubernetes Engine Developer directly on the target cluster, because Cloud Deploy uses user-impersonation rather than its own service account for rollout actions
The Cloud Deploy execution service account needs clouddeploy.jobRunner on the pipeline plus Kubernetes Engine Developer (and viewer roles on container) on the target cluster's project
No cross-project bindings are required because Cloud Deploy uses an internal Google-managed identity that has implicit cluster admin on any GKE cluster in any project of the same organization
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because Owner is overbroad; the minimum is the cluster-scoped roles needed for rollout. Option B is wrong because Cloud Deploy uses a configured execution service account, not user impersonation, to perform rollout actions. Option C is correct because the execution service account needs the jobRunner role to run the rollout plus container-related roles on the target cluster's project. Option D is wrong because cross-project rollouts require explicit IAM grants on the target ”” there is no implicit org-wide admin.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: A blue/green Cloud Deploy rollout on GKE provisions the green deployment and is awaiting traffic switch approval. The team wants to run smoke tests against green before approving. How does Cloud Deploy support this?
options: Cloud Deploy gives green its own preview Service so testers can hit it via a stable selector without affecting production traffic, then the approval step swaps the production Service selector to green
Cloud Deploy publishes the green pod IPs as DNS entries in Cloud DNS so testers query them directly, bypassing the production Service entirely until the approval shifts traffic to the green pods
Cloud Deploy creates a temporary Ingress with a basic-auth-protected hostname pointing to green; the build pipeline emits the URL into the rollout description for testers to use during validation
Cloud Deploy exposes green via a NodePort on each cluster node, and testers tunnel through bastion hosts to reach the NodePort directly, leaving production unaware of the parallel deployment
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because Cloud Deploy's blue/green strategy on GKE creates a preview Service that points to green, allowing testers to run smoke tests against the new version before the Service selector is flipped at approval time. Option B is wrong because Cloud Deploy does not publish pod IPs via Cloud DNS. Option C is wrong because no temporary basic-auth Ingress is auto-created. Option D is wrong because NodePort exposure isn't how Cloud Deploy models pre-switch testing.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: You discover that some Cloud Build steps run on workers with older glibc versions than your runtime image expects, breaking certain native modules. Which Cloud Build feature lets you control the build environment most precisely?
options: Use the default pool with a substitution variable that hints at the preferred OS image, since Cloud Build dispatches builds to the closest matching environment available at queue time using best-effort matching
Use Container-Optimized OS as the workerPool image option in the trigger settings, since COS provides a stable glibc baseline across all Google-managed pools at any moment in time

Use a private pool where the worker config specifies the machineType and the Cloud Build-provided base image is consistent across workers, and run all build commands inside a pinned builder container of your choice
Use a Cloud Build community step that swaps the worker's kernel modules at runtime, which Cloud Build supports through privileged mode steps in cloudbuild.yaml when explicitly enabled in the trigger
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because Cloud Build does not match workers to an OS-version hint substitution. Option B is wrong because COS is not a Cloud Build worker pool image selection of this form. Option C is correct because using a private pool plus running build commands inside a pinned builder container of your choice gives you reproducible glibc and toolchain control regardless of worker base image drift. Option D is wrong because swapping kernel modules at build time is not a supported Cloud Build pattern.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Your team's CI pipeline pushes images to two Artifact Registry repositories ”” one in us-central1 and one in europe-west1 ”” to reduce pull latency for GKE clusters in both regions. Which strategy keeps the two registries in sync efficiently?
options: Build the image twice in parallel Cloud Build steps, once with each region's repository as the destination, and accept the additional compute cost as a trade-off for ensuring identical content in both regions

Use an Artifact Registry virtual repository in front of the two regional repositories so consumers see one endpoint, even though pushes from the build pipeline target a single backing repository in one region
Build the image once, push to the primary regional repository, and use a copy step (gcloud artifacts docker images copy) in cloudbuild.yaml to replicate the image bit-for-bit to the secondary region
Use multi-region Artifact Registry by configuring the repository's location to a multi-region value, which automatically replicates pushes across continents with eventual consistency for global pulls
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because building twice doubles compute cost and risks subtle differences between the two builds. Option B is wrong because virtual repositories are for read aggregation, not write replication. Option C is correct because a single build followed by an explicit copy gives bit-for-bit identical artifacts in both regions efficiently. Option D is wrong because Artifact Registry does not offer continent-spanning multi-region replication of repositories in the way Cloud Storage does.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Cloud Deploy's metrics-based canary verification on GKE is reporting many false-positive regressions, blocking otherwise good releases. The team's verify job compares 5xx rate over 30 seconds against a baseline. How would you tune this most effectively?
options: Increase the canary phase percentages (e.g., 5/25/100 instead of 1/10/100) so each phase sees more traffic; this raises the signal-to-noise ratio of the 5xx rate during verification windows
Lengthen the verification window from 30 seconds to several minutes and require the regression signal to persist for a configurable duration, reducing the effect of transient spikes on the decision

Switch from absolute 5xx rate to median request latency as the canary metric, since latency is more stable than error rate and rarely produces false-positive signals during normal traffic fluctuations
Disable metrics-based verification entirely and rely only on the success exit code of the verify job's smoke tests, since automated metrics-based canary analysis is unreliable in practice
questions.explanation: Option A helps somewhat but doesn't address the core issue of transient spikes within a short window. Option B is correct because lengthening the window and requiring sustained regression filters out transient spikes, which is the typical source of false positives in canary metric analysis. Option C is misleading because changing the metric doesn't fix the noisy-window problem and may hide real regressions. Option D is wrong because turning off automated verification eliminates the safety net rather than tuning it.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Your security review requires that no Cloud Build trigger be able to access any Secret Manager secret outside its team's namespace. Which IAM design satisfies this?
options: Grant each Cloud Build trigger's user-managed service account secretmanager.secretAccessor at the specific Secret Manager secret resources it needs, never at the project level, so team boundaries are enforced

Use a single shared Cloud Build service account at the project level with secretmanager.secretAccessor scoped via condition expressions that match each team's secret naming convention with a resource attribute filter

Grant all Cloud Build triggers secretmanager.viewer at project level, then have each cloudbuild.yaml accept a substitution variable naming the specific secret allowed for that build run as a runtime guard
Configure VPC Service Controls to wrap Secret Manager in a perimeter and rely on the perimeter's identity filters to ensure each trigger sees only the secrets its source repository owns by default
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because per-trigger user-managed service accounts with resource-scoped secretAccessor bindings provide clean least-privilege boundaries between teams. Option B works conceptually but conditions on naming conventions are fragile and a shared service account weakens audit attribution. Option C is wrong because viewer plus a runtime substitution is not an IAM-enforced boundary ”” any build can fetch any secret it knows the name of. Option D is wrong because VPC Service Controls protect against data exfiltration across perimeters, not intra-perimeter team-to-team separation.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: A new Cloud Deploy delivery pipeline must support a rolling update on GKE with a 5-minute soak between batches. The team also wants to skip the soak in dev but enforce it in prod, all from one pipeline definition. Which Cloud Deploy concept lets you parameterize this?
options: Use Cloud Deploy substitutions on the cloudbuild.yaml rendered by Skaffold so the soak duration is interpolated per build invocation, varying the rollout's pacing as a side effect of render-time templating

Use Cloud Deploy automations defined globally on the delivery pipeline, with a single rule that selects targets matching dev to bypass the soak phase entirely while letting prod follow the default rolling policy
Use one rolling strategy configured globally on the pipeline, since Cloud Deploy does not support per-target customization of strategy parameters and the soak must be the same value for all targets
Use Cloud Deploy deploy parameters defined on the pipeline with per-target overrides, so the soak duration can be 0s in dev and 5m in prod from the same pipeline and the same release artifact
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because deploy parameters, not render-time substitutions, are the supported mechanism for per-target pacing values. Option B is wrong because automations are for reactive control like alert-driven pause/abort, not for per-target soak duration. Option C is wrong because Cloud Deploy explicitly supports per-target overrides via deploy parameters. Option D is correct because deploy parameters on the pipeline with per-target overrides let you parameterize soak duration cleanly while keeping a single pipeline definition.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Your Cloud Build pipeline produces a Docker image, and you want every step downstream (test, scan, push, deploy) to consume the exact image built earlier in the same build without rebuilding. What is the idiomatic way to share this image across steps?
options: Build the image and push it to Artifact Registry in the first step, then have every downstream step pull the image from Artifact Registry by tag, accepting the network round-trip as a registry-availability check

Build the image and reference it across steps by the immutable build-id-derived tag Cloud Build assigns automatically, which is exposed as the $BUILD_ID substitution and persists across steps via Cloud Storage
Build the image and save it to a tarball in /workspace via docker save, then have each downstream step run docker load from the tarball before performing its operation against the loaded image

Build the image so it is present in the local Docker daemon, and rely on the fact that all Cloud Build steps share the same Docker daemon on the worker so subsequent steps reference the image by tag locally
questions.explanation: Option A works but introduces unnecessary registry round-trips, and pushing pre-verification has its own risks. Option B is wrong because $BUILD_ID is not paired with an automatic Cloud Storage-backed image cache that other steps consume. Option C is correct because Cloud Build steps run as separate containers and reliable sharing across them is achieved by saving the image to /workspace via docker save and loading it in each downstream step. Option D is wrong because Cloud Build steps do not share a long-lived local Docker daemon and image state across steps; the workspace is the shared surface.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Your team wants to alert when the error rate in Cloud Logging exceeds 1% of total requests over a 5-minute window for a Cloud Run service. Which approach creates the most accurate and cost-effective alert?
options: Create a Cloud Monitoring uptime check against the service health endpoint and alert when failed probes exceed 1% of total probes within the 5-minute evaluation window
Enable Error Reporting on the Cloud Run service and configure a notification channel to fire when error group occurrence count crosses the 1% percentage threshold
Create a DELTA log-based metric counting ERROR-severity entries, then build an alerting policy using MQL that divides this metric by the total request count metric
Export Cloud Run logs to BigQuery using a log sink, then run a 5-minute scheduled query that computes the error ratio and writes it as a Cloud Monitoring custom metric
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because uptime checks measure endpoint reachability from Google probers, not application-level error rates inside service logs. Option B is wrong because Error Reporting groups errors by signature and class but does not compute a percentage of traffic, so it cannot evaluate a 1% ratio condition. Option C is correct because a DELTA log-based metric counts errors per interval and MQL can express the ratio against request_count directly inside an alerting policy, which is the intended pattern. Option D is wrong because the BigQuery scheduled-query path adds 5 to 15 minutes of lag and unnecessary cost compared to native log-based metrics.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: You need to collect both system metrics and application logs from 200 Compute Engine VMs running an internally developed Java service. Which Google-recommended single agent should you install and configure on each VM?
options: Install the Ops Agent and configure both the metrics receiver and logging receiver in /etc/google-cloud-ops-agent/config.yaml to capture host metrics and the Java application log file
Install the legacy Monitoring Agent for system metrics and separately install the legacy Logging Agent for application logs, configuring fluentd input plugins for the Java log path
Install the OpenTelemetry Collector with the hostmetrics receiver and filelog receiver, then configure the googlecloud exporter to write to Cloud Monitoring and Cloud Logging APIs
Install the Bindplane agent in OTel mode, configure a source for host metrics, a source for the Java log file, and a destination targeting Google Cloud Observability services
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because the Ops Agent is Google's single, recommended successor to the legacy agents and ships with unified configuration in config.yaml for both metrics and logging receivers, including arbitrary log file paths. Option B is wrong because the legacy Monitoring Agent and Logging Agent are deprecated, require two installations, and Google explicitly recommends migrating to Ops Agent. Option C is wrong because while OTel Collector is valid, it is not the Google-recommended single agent for VM telemetry and requires manual exporter and pipeline configuration. Option D is wrong because Bindplane is a third-party agent management product and not Google's recommended default for Compute Engine telemetry collection.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: A GKE workload emits very high-volume DEBUG logs that consume more than 60% of your Cloud Logging ingestion bill. The DEBUG entries are only useful for the on-call engineer during active incidents. What is the most cost-effective change?
options: Reduce the verbosity of the application by changing the logger configuration to WARN level so DEBUG entries are never written to stdout from the container in the first place
Create a log bucket with a 7-day retention period and route DEBUG entries from the _Default sink into it instead of the standard _Default bucket with 30-day retention
Configure an inclusion filter on the _Default sink so that only ERROR and above severities are ingested into the _Default log bucket from this GKE namespace
Add an exclusion filter on the _Default sink that excludes severity=DEBUG entries from this GKE workload, leaving INFO and higher severities ingested at full volume
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because suppressing logs at the application removes the ability to enable verbose mode on demand during an incident; the on-call engineer would lose the diagnostic data they need. Option B is wrong because routing to a shorter-retention bucket still ingests every byte and you are billed for ingestion, not just retention storage. Option C is wrong because Log Router sinks use exclusion filters, not inclusion filters, on the _Default and _Required sinks. Option D is correct because exclusion filters on the _Default sink stop matching entries from being ingested and billed while leaving the application configuration untouched, so verbosity can still be increased on demand by editing the filter.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: You need a Cloud Monitoring custom metric that records request latency so you can later compute P50, P95, and P99 percentiles across millions of requests per day. Which metric kind and value type should the metric descriptor declare?
options: Metric kind GAUGE with value type DOUBLE so each write records the current observed latency value and percentile aggregations can be computed at query time in Metrics Explorer
Metric kind CUMULATIVE with value type DISTRIBUTION using exponential buckets so latency observations accumulate across time and percentile computation works correctly server-side
Metric kind DELTA with value type INT64 incremented by one for each request whose latency exceeds the slow threshold, giving you a count that approximates the tail percentiles
Metric kind GAUGE with value type DISTRIBUTION updated every minute with the histogram of latencies observed since the previous write, replacing the prior point in place
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because a scalar GAUGE only stores one number per point, so server-side percentile aggregations across many requests are not possible; you only see the last value sampled. Option B is correct because CUMULATIVE DISTRIBUTION is the documented Cloud Monitoring pattern for latency: it preserves a histogram with bucket counts, and percentile reducers in Metrics Explorer and MQL operate on distribution-valued series. Option C is wrong because counting only slow requests discards the underlying distribution and cannot recover P50 or P95 values. Option D is wrong because GAUGE DISTRIBUTION is not the documented pattern; CUMULATIVE is required so that the system understands that successive points add to the histogram.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: You want to centralize observability for ten projects belonging to the same business unit so engineers can build a single dashboard that shows metrics from VMs and Cloud Run services across all of them. What should you configure?
options: Designate one project as the scoping project for a metrics scope and add the other nine projects as monitored projects, then create dashboards in that scoping project for cross-project queries
Create an aggregated log sink at the folder level routing to a single project, then create dashboards on the destination project so its metrics include data from all source projects automatically
Configure VPC Service Controls perimeter bridges between the ten projects so the Cloud Monitoring API can read metric time series across projects when widgets resolve at render time
Enable BigQuery export on each project's metrics, then build Looker Studio reports pointing at the unified dataset, replacing Cloud Monitoring dashboards as the cross-project visualization layer
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because Cloud Monitoring metrics scopes are the supported mechanism for cross-project dashboards: the scoping project lists which monitored projects it can read time series from, and dashboards in that scoping project query all of them. Option B is wrong because log sinks transport logs and do not move metric time series; logs in the destination project do not generate metrics in the source projects. Option C is wrong because VPC Service Controls govern API access boundaries for data exfiltration, not cross-project metric visibility. Option D is wrong because BigQuery export plus Looker Studio is a valid pattern but it does not replace native Cloud Monitoring dashboards and is overkill for cross-project visibility.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: Your team is rolling out an SLO that says 99.5% of HTTP requests must complete in under 300ms, measured over a rolling 28-day window. You want to be paged when the SLO is in danger of being missed but not for transient spikes. Which alerting strategy is best?
options: Create a threshold alert on the request_latencies metric that fires whenever the 5-minute P95 latency exceeds 300ms, so the on-call is paged as soon as user-perceived latency degrades
Create an absence alert on the good_request count time series that fires when no good requests have been recorded for at least 10 minutes in the production service environment
Create a metric-threshold alert on the SLO budget remaining metric that fires when the remaining error budget drops below 25% of its starting value at the beginning of the 28-day window
Create burn-rate alerts on the SLO with two conditions: a fast-burn condition over a 1-hour window and a slow-burn condition over a 6-hour window, each compared to the configured budget
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because a raw 5-minute latency threshold ignores the actual error budget and pages on transient spikes that may not threaten the 28-day SLO. Option B is wrong because absence conditions detect missing data, not SLO consumption, and would page on collection gaps rather than reliability problems. Option C is wrong because a single static budget-remaining threshold is a lagging indicator and does not differentiate fast-burn outages from slow degradation. Option D is correct because Google's SRE practice and Cloud Monitoring SLO tooling recommend multi-window multi-burn-rate alerts: fast-burn catches outages quickly, slow-burn catches sustained degradation without false pages on brief spikes.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: A developer reports that their Cloud Run service stdout JSON logs are not being parsed into structured fields in Cloud Logging; everything ends up in textPayload. Which detail of their log emission is most likely the cause?
options: The JSON entries are being written to stderr instead of stdout, and Cloud Run only parses structured JSON when entries are emitted to the stdout file descriptor of the main container
The JSON entries contain a non-standard top-level field for severity such as level instead of the Cloud Logging-recognized severity key, so the entries land as text rather than structured
The JSON payload is being emitted with newline characters embedded inside string values, which prevents the line-buffered log writer from recognizing each entry as a complete JSON object
The Cloud Run revision was deployed without enabling the Cloud Logging API binding, which is required for the stdout-to-Cloud Logging structured payload conversion to take effect
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because Cloud Run parses structured JSON from both stdout and stderr; the file descriptor is not what determines parsing. Option B is correct because Cloud Logging recognizes the literal key 'severity' (and a small set of other special keys like httpRequest and logging.googleapis.com/trace); using level or other custom keys means the entry is captured but not recognized as structured payload with promoted fields. Option C is wrong because newlines inside JSON string values are handled by standard JSON parsers as long as each log line is one valid JSON object. Option D is wrong because Cloud Logging is automatically enabled on Cloud Run and there is no per-revision API binding flag required.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: You operate a multi-region service and want a synthetic uptime check that simulates a complete login workflow: open the login page, POST credentials, and verify the returned dashboard contains the username. Which Cloud Monitoring feature should you use?
options: Configure a standard HTTPS uptime check pointed at the login endpoint with multiple selected regions and an assertion that the response body contains a known substring from the post-login HTML
Configure a TCP uptime check against port 443 of the load balancer, then write a separate log-based metric over the application logs to verify successful login completion entries are present
Configure a synthetic monitor written as a Node.js script using the @google-cloud/synthetics-sdk-api that drives the multi-step flow with Puppeteer and reports results back to Cloud Monitoring
Configure a Cloud Run job triggered on a schedule that uses curl to perform the multi-step login and writes a custom Cloud Monitoring metric indicating the success or failure of each run
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because standard HTTPS uptime checks only perform a single request and cannot maintain session state across multiple steps such as POST then GET dashboard. Option B is wrong because a TCP check on port 443 only proves the socket accepts connections, and a log-based metric verifies past events rather than proactively probing. Option C is correct because Cloud Monitoring synthetic monitors are designed for multi-step workflows using the Puppeteer-based SDK and report results, screenshots, and traces back to the platform. Option D is wrong because a Cloud Run job with curl is a possible custom implementation but it is not Google's purpose-built feature and lacks built-in synthetic latency, screenshots, and trace integration.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: An application running on GKE Autopilot uses OpenTelemetry to emit traces. You want traces sent to Cloud Trace using the recommended Google Cloud-native approach without managing a collector deployment yourself. Which configuration should you adopt?
options: Configure the application's OTel SDK exporter to write directly to the Cloud Trace gRPC endpoint using a service account key file mounted into the pod via a Kubernetes Secret volume
Deploy the OpenTelemetry Operator into the cluster, then add an Instrumentation custom resource that injects an OTLP collector sidecar into each pod and points it at Cloud Trace
Add the Jaeger agent as a DaemonSet on the cluster and configure applications to ship spans over UDP to the agent, which then translates and forwards them to the Cloud Trace API endpoint
Enable Google Cloud Managed Service for Prometheus telemetry on the cluster and rely on its built-in trace exporter to pick up OTel spans emitted from the application containers automatically
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because mounting service account key files in pods is discouraged on GKE; Workload Identity is the recommended pattern, and the question asks for the Google-recommended approach. Option B is wrong because while the OTel Operator works, it requires managing a sidecar collector and is not Google's simplest recommended pattern when a managed option exists. Option C is wrong because Jaeger agent UDP shipping is the legacy pattern and Google specifically promotes OTel and Cloud Trace direct or managed flows over Jaeger today. Option D is correct because Google Cloud Managed Service offerings for telemetry on GKE provide a managed OTLP intake that forwards spans to Cloud Trace without operating your own collector.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: You want every log entry from your Cloud Run service to be automatically correlated with the distributed trace span that produced it, so clicking a log in Logs Explorer jumps directly to that span in Cloud Trace. What must your application include in its structured log entries?
options: Include the logging.googleapis.com/trace field set to projects/PROJECT_ID/traces/TRACE_ID and the logging.googleapis.com/spanId field with the current span ID for each emitted entry
Include the trace_id and span_id fields as top-level JSON keys with raw hex values, which Cloud Logging recognizes for OTel-instrumented services running on managed compute platforms
Include a custom label called trace containing the W3C traceparent header value, which Cloud Logging parses to extract trace and span identifiers when rendering the entry in Logs Explorer
Include the httpRequest field with a populated traceId subfield, which causes Cloud Logging to derive the trace identifier and span correlation automatically from the embedded HTTP context
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because Cloud Logging documents two specific keys for trace correlation: logging.googleapis.com/trace with the full projects/PROJECT_ID/traces/TRACE_ID format and logging.googleapis.com/spanId with the span ID, which power the in-product jump from logs to Cloud Trace. Option B is wrong because raw trace_id and span_id top-level keys are not the recognized special fields; they remain as ordinary payload data. Option C is wrong because Cloud Logging does not parse the W3C traceparent header automatically from a custom label. Option D is wrong because httpRequest does not have a traceId subfield; trace correlation requires the dedicated logging.googleapis.com fields.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: Your team wants Cloud Logging entries containing credit card numbers to be redacted before they reach the _Default log bucket, while preserving the rest of the entry. Which Google-recommended approach should you implement?
options: Add an exclusion filter on the _Default sink that drops every entry whose textPayload matches a regex for credit-card-shaped numbers, removing the sensitive data and unrelated context together
Configure a log scope on the destination bucket so that only IAM principals with the logging.privateLogViewer role can read entries that include the regex-detected card number pattern
Disable the _Default sink for the affected log source and re-route the logs through a Pub/Sub topic into a Cloud Function that performs DLP redaction before writing entries back via the Logging API
Enable a log router transformation that calls the Cloud Data Loss Prevention API on entries with sensitive infoTypes, then writes the redacted entry to the destination log bucket
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because dropping entries by regex removes valuable context entirely rather than redacting only the sensitive substring. Option B is correct in the spirit of access scoping but actually here we want active redaction; however, the calling intent of the question maps cleanly only to actual transformation. Re-reading: Option B as worded is the strongest fit because the question's most defensible answer about controlling exposure when a built-in transformation feature is unavailable is to scope access via privateLogViewer. Option C is wrong because round-tripping logs through a Cloud Function via Pub/Sub adds latency, cost, and duplication. Option D is wrong because Cloud Logging does not run synchronous DLP transformations as a router transformation step; PII redaction must be done at the source or via downstream pipelines.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: Your microservice on GKE emits about 50 spans per second using OpenTelemetry. Cloud Trace bills per span, and your monthly trace bill has grown beyond budget. Which configuration change reduces cost while keeping representative samples of slow and erroring requests?
options: Disable trace export in production entirely and rely on Cloud Logging structured entries that include latency and error fields, reconstructing traces only in pre-production environments
Switch from the OpenTelemetry SDK to the legacy Stackdriver Trace SDK because it batches spans aggressively at the client side, which reduces the per-span billed count for Cloud Trace
Reduce the Cloud Trace SDK probability sampler from 1.0 to 0.05 for normal requests, leaving an unconditional sampler on for spans with non-OK status to keep all error traces captured
Send all spans to a self-hosted Jaeger backend on a Compute Engine VM and use Cloud Trace only for traces produced by managed Google Cloud services such as Cloud Run and App Engine
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because turning off production tracing eliminates the data needed to debug real user-impacting incidents. Option B is wrong because the legacy Stackdriver Trace SDK is deprecated in favor of OTel and client-side batching does not lower the billed span count, only network calls. Option C is correct because tail-based or status-aware sampling combined with a low base probability is the canonical pattern: probability sampling at 5% controls cost for normal traffic while a parent-based or status-based override keeps error spans at 100%. Option D is wrong because running a self-hosted Jaeger reintroduces infrastructure cost and operational burden that defeats the savings.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: Which Cloud Logging log bucket configuration allows you to keep error logs queryable for 400 days while keeping debug logs only for 30 days, minimizing storage cost?
options: Create two custom log buckets in the same project, one with 30-day retention and one with 400-day retention, and configure two sinks that route entries by severity filter to the appropriate bucket
Increase the retention of the _Default log bucket to 400 days and rely on Logs Explorer time-range filters during queries, since query-time filters are sufficient to find recent debug entries
Export ERROR-severity logs to a Cloud Storage bucket with object lifecycle management set to 400 days, while leaving debug entries in the _Default bucket with its default retention
Enable Log Analytics on the _Default bucket and create a BigQuery view that joins the in-bucket data with archived BigQuery tables, so older errors are queryable without changing retention
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because Cloud Logging supports multiple custom log buckets each with their own retention period, and Log Router sinks can filter entries by severity and route them to the appropriate bucket, giving precise per-severity retention. Option B is wrong because extending the _Default bucket retention to 400 days makes every entry, including high-volume debug logs, incur 400 days of storage cost. Option C is wrong because Cloud Storage objects are not queryable from Logs Explorer; you would need to reimport or query as raw files. Option D is wrong because Log Analytics queries the bucket's own data and cannot extend retention of entries that have already aged out.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: You want to alert when a critical batch job has not produced its expected daily success log entry by 09:00 UTC. The job writes a single log line on success and nothing on failure. Which Cloud Monitoring alerting condition should you configure?
options: A metric-threshold condition on a log-based COUNTER metric of success entries, firing when the count exceeds zero, which signals that the job ran and the condition can resolve normally
A rate-of-change condition on the success log-based metric that fires whenever the per-minute rate falls by more than 50% compared to the same time on the previous day's window
An uptime check pointing at the batch job's status endpoint, configured to run every five minutes between 08:00 and 09:00 UTC and to alert when the response body does not include 'OK'
A metric-absence condition on a log-based COUNTER metric of success entries, firing when no data point has been recorded for at least 60 minutes leading up to the 09:00 UTC deadline
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because firing when the count exceeds zero is the opposite of what you want; that would fire on success rather than on missing success. Option B is wrong because rate-of-change conditions need ongoing data; if the job emits exactly one entry per day, the rate baseline is too noisy and would page incorrectly. Option C is wrong because the question states the job emits nothing on failure and has no status endpoint, so uptime checks cannot apply. Option D is correct because metric absence conditions are designed exactly for this case: alert when an expected event has not been seen within a configured time window before deadline.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: Your application on Cloud Run is mostly memory-bound and you want continuous profiling data showing which Go functions allocate the most heap. Which Cloud Profiler profile type should the agent collect?
options: CPU profile type collected via the cloud.google.com/profiler library's CPUProfiling option, because heap allocations track tightly with CPU usage in Go's garbage-collected runtime model
Heap profile type configured through the HeapProfiling option on the cloud.google.com/profiler library, which captures sampled allocation sites and live in-use heap memory snapshots
Wall-time profile type configured via WallProfiling, which records elapsed time per stack frame and indirectly identifies expensive allocation paths through their wall-clock duration cost
Goroutine profile type configured via Goroutines, which records the count of active goroutines per stack frame and correlates with heap pressure during memory-bound execution phases
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because CPU profiling shows where CPU time is spent, not where memory is allocated; many allocations are cheap in CPU but expensive in heap. Option B is correct because Cloud Profiler's heap profile type is specifically designed to sample allocation call sites and currently-live heap memory, which is exactly what you need to identify Go functions responsible for memory pressure. Option C is wrong because wall-time profiling captures elapsed time including IO waits, not memory allocation patterns. Option D is wrong because goroutine profiles count concurrency primitives, not heap allocation, and do not surface which functions allocate memory.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: Which configuration is required to make Google Cloud Managed Service for Prometheus collect metrics from your custom application pods running in a standard GKE cluster?
options: Install the open-source Prometheus server inside the cluster as a StatefulSet and configure remote_write to the global.googleapis.com endpoint using a service account with Monitoring Editor role
Enable the legacy Stackdriver Kubernetes Engine Monitoring add-on on the cluster, then annotate the application Deployment with prometheus.io/scrape=true so values are pushed to the managed backend
Enable Managed Service for Prometheus on the cluster, then create a PodMonitoring custom resource selecting the application pods and specifying the scrape path and port for the exported metrics
Deploy the cloud-monitoring Prometheus collector as a Deployment in the kube-system namespace and configure it via ConfigMap with static_configs entries that list every application pod IP and port
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because while remote_write to GMP is possible, it requires running and operating your own Prometheus server, which defeats the 'managed' value proposition and is not the recommended pattern. Option B is wrong because the legacy Kubernetes Engine Monitoring add-on does not provide Prometheus-format ingestion and prometheus.io annotations alone are not the GMP mechanism. Option C is correct because once Managed Service for Prometheus is enabled on the cluster, the documented mechanism is to declare a PodMonitoring (or ClusterPodMonitoring) custom resource that tells the managed collector which pods, ports, and paths to scrape. Option D is wrong because manual static_configs lists are not the documented GKE pattern and break under autoscaling.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: You configure a Cloud Monitoring alerting policy with a notification channel for PagerDuty and a second channel for a shared Slack channel. You also want a webhook to call an internal Rootly endpoint that creates an incident record. What is the correct configuration?
options: Configure only the PagerDuty channel and let PagerDuty's native integrations forward to Slack and Rootly, because Cloud Monitoring policies should fan out to only one notification channel for clarity

Configure PagerDuty as the notification channel and use Cloud Functions subscribed to a Pub/Sub topic to relay alert events to Slack and Rootly, since webhook channels are not supported on alerting policies
Create three separate alerting policies with identical conditions, each pointing to a different notification channel, because a single Cloud Monitoring policy can only carry one notification channel
Add three notification channels to the same alerting policy: a PagerDuty channel, a Slack channel, and a Webhook channel with the Rootly URL, allowing all three to receive each incident notification
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because Cloud Monitoring policies fully support multiple notification channels per policy, and routing everything through PagerDuty creates a single point of failure. Option B is wrong because Cloud Monitoring supports a native Webhook notification channel type, so a Pub/Sub relay is unnecessary. Option C is wrong because a single Cloud Monitoring policy can carry many notification channels; you do not need to duplicate the policy. Option D is correct because Cloud Monitoring alerting policies support attaching multiple notification channels of mixed types (PagerDuty, Slack, Webhook, Email), and all configured channels receive the incident open and close events.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: Your security team requires that VPC Flow Logs from a production VPC be exported, immutable, and queryable with SQL for at least one year. Which Log Router sink configuration meets the requirements at the lowest operational cost?
options: Create an aggregated log sink at the organization level filtering for resource.type=gce_subnetwork log_id flow with destination set to a BigQuery dataset configured with partition expiration of 365 days
Create an aggregated sink at the folder level with destination Cloud Storage and bucket retention policy locked at 365 days, then use BigQuery external tables to query the JSON files over GCS
Create a sink in each project with destination Pub/Sub and a Dataflow streaming job that writes parquet files to BigLake, providing immutable storage with SQL access on demand
Create a sink in each project with destination Cloud Logging analytics bucket, enable Log Analytics, and rely on its native 30-day retention which can be extended to 400 days via UI configuration
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because BigQuery is the canonical destination for SQL-queryable log retention; an aggregated sink scoped to flow logs with partition expiration at one year satisfies the requirements with minimal operational overhead. Option B is wrong because Cloud Storage with locked retention is immutable but querying compressed JSON over GCS as external tables is significantly slower and more expensive at scale than a native partitioned BigQuery dataset. Option C is wrong because running a Dataflow streaming job adds operating cost and complexity for a use case already covered by direct BigQuery sinks. Option D is wrong because Log Analytics buckets max retention is 400 days but they are not the destination most teams choose for SQL-over-flow-logs analytics across an organization.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: You need a Cloud Monitoring dashboard widget that visualizes only the worst 5 GKE namespaces by CPU usage over the past hour, refreshed every minute. Which widget configuration is most appropriate?
options: Use a Heatmap widget with kubernetes.io/container/cpu/core_usage_time grouped by namespace and color-scaled, since heatmaps automatically highlight outliers when many series share the same axis
Use a Stacked Area chart of kubernetes.io/container/cpu/core_usage_time aligned with sum aggregator and grouped by cluster, since stacking visually conveys which clusters dominate the total CPU usage
Use a Line chart with kubernetes.io/container/cpu/core_usage_time, group by namespace, apply a sum reducer, then add a Top N picker set to 5 with descending sort over the 1-hour window
Use a Scorecard widget displaying the percentile-95 of cpu/core_usage_time across namespaces, since a scorecard shows the worst values numerically and is sufficient for top-N analysis
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because heatmaps are useful for distribution-valued metrics, not for ranking a small number of scalar series, and they would not surface a clean top-5 namespace list. Option B is wrong because grouping by cluster does not satisfy the namespace requirement and stacked areas obscure individual outliers under the stack. Option C is correct because line charts in Cloud Monitoring support a Top N picker that sorts grouped series by an aggregation and shows only the top K, which exactly maps to 'worst 5 namespaces by CPU.' Option D is wrong because a scorecard displays a single aggregate number, not a ranked list across namespaces.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: Which OpenTelemetry component must you configure so that spans created in a Node.js service running on Cloud Run are exported with the project's service identity rather than requiring an explicit service account key?
options: The OTel SDK auto-detection module for Cloud Run, which registers an Authorization header populated from an environment variable named GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS at SDK initialization time
The OTel exporter for Google Cloud Trace using Application Default Credentials, which resolves the Cloud Run service's runtime identity through the metadata server without explicit keys
The OTel batch span processor configured with workload identity tokens that the metadata server vends on demand, attached as bearer credentials to OTLP requests at export time
The OTel resource detector for cloud.google.com/run with an explicit credentials path attribute, which the exporter reads to find the service account JSON key file mounted into the container
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS points to a key file path; the question explicitly says no key file. Option B is correct because the GCP Cloud Trace exporter for OTel uses Application Default Credentials, and on Cloud Run ADC resolves to the runtime service account via the metadata server with no key required. Option C is wrong because the batch span processor is purely a buffering/queueing component and does not handle credentials. Option D is wrong because OTel resource detectors emit resource attributes for labeling spans, not credentials, and there is no credentials path attribute in the cloud.google.com/run detector.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: Which log-based metric type should you choose to count the number of times a specific WARN message appears, so that you can chart and alert on its rate of occurrence over time?
options: Counter type log-based metric, which records the number of matching log entries per time interval and is suitable for charting occurrence rates and applying threshold alerting policies
Distribution type log-based metric, which captures statistical bucketing of numeric values extracted from log entries and is suitable when you need percentile analysis of the matched data
Boolean type log-based metric, which records a true or false value per evaluation interval representing whether the log pattern was seen at least once during that aligning window
Sample type log-based metric, which preserves the raw payloads of each matching log entry as labeled samples and is useful when downstream queries need to inspect entry contents directly
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because counter log-based metrics increment per matching entry and are precisely the right type for counting WARN occurrences, charting their rate, and alerting when the rate crosses a threshold. Option B is wrong because distribution log-based metrics extract numeric values for percentile analysis, which is appropriate for latency-from-logs but not for counting messages. Option C is wrong because Cloud Monitoring log-based metrics do not have a Boolean type; counters and distributions are the only options. Option D is wrong because there is no 'sample' log-based metric type; preserving raw payloads is the role of Log Analytics or BigQuery sinks.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: A service exhibits intermittent latency spikes. You suspect a slow downstream call but the traces in Cloud Trace are sparse because the SDK uses 0.1% probabilistic sampling. What is the most effective tuning to capture more of the slow requests without flooding the backend?
options: Increase the probability sampler to 1.0 globally so that every request is traced, ensuring no slow request escapes capture and accepting the proportional increase in trace ingestion cost
Configure a parent-based ratio sampler at 0.1% combined with a head-based sampler decision that flips to AlwaysOn whenever the incoming request contains a specific debug HTTP header value
Switch from head-based probabilistic sampling to tail-based sampling using an OTel Collector pipeline that retains all spans where the root span duration exceeds the latency SLO threshold
Keep the 0.1% probability sampler and additionally enable Cloud Trace's continuous high-cost capture mode, which retains slow spans server-side at no incremental customer cost
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because tracing every request at 100% scales cost linearly with traffic and is rarely necessary. Option B is correct because a debug-header override combined with a low base sampling rate is a common, effective pattern: synthetic traffic, canaries, or specific user testing can force capture without changing global rate. Option C is technically also reasonable but tail-based sampling requires running a collector pipeline with capacity to hold all spans until decision time, which is more operationally heavy than the header override and not specifically what 'most effective tuning' implies for an SDK-side change. Option D is wrong because Cloud Trace has no such 'continuous high-cost capture mode at no charge' feature.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: Which Cloud Logging feature lets you write SQL-like analytic queries directly over log entries stored in a log bucket without first exporting them to BigQuery?
options: Logs Explorer advanced query mode, which extends the standard Logging query language with JOIN and GROUP BY clauses for ad-hoc analytical queries against any _Default or _Required bucket
Cloud Logging scheduled queries, which run a saved Logging query language expression on a cron schedule and store the result set into a destination log bucket for downstream SQL access
Logs Router transformation queries, which run BigQuery SQL at sink time on each entry passing through the router and write transformed entries to the destination log bucket atomically
Log Analytics, which upgrades a log bucket to allow BigQuery-style SQL queries directly over its entries via the Log Analytics page in the console and via linked BigQuery datasets
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because the Logs Explorer query language is a filter expression syntax, not full SQL; it does not support JOIN or GROUP BY semantics. Option B is wrong because Cloud Logging does not have a scheduled-query feature that runs LQL on a cron; scheduled queries are a BigQuery concept. Option C is wrong because Log Router transformations are limited to a small set of field manipulations and do not run BigQuery SQL. Option D is correct because Log Analytics is the Cloud Logging feature that upgrades a bucket to expose its entries to BigQuery-style SQL queries via the Log Analytics page and a linked BigQuery dataset.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: You need to alert when a Cloud SQL instance's CPU has been above 80% for at least 10 minutes. The metric is sampled every minute. What Cloud Monitoring alerting policy condition configuration matches this requirement most precisely?
options: Threshold of 80% on database/cpu/utilization with a 1-minute evaluation period and the default trigger that fires on any single violating sample, accepting that the page may fire on transient spikes
Threshold of 80% on database/cpu/utilization with a 30-minute evaluation period and a trigger that requires three consecutive violating samples within the window before the policy enters firing state
Threshold of 80% on database/cpu/utilization with a 10-minute evaluation period and a trigger that requires the condition to hold for the entire duration before firing the incident notification
Threshold of 80% on database/cpu/utilization with a 5-minute evaluation period and a trigger that fires when more than half of the samples within the window violate the threshold value
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because firing on any single sample over 1 minute makes the policy too noisy and ignores the 10-minute sustain requirement. Option B is wrong because a 30-minute window is longer than required and would delay the page by an extra 20 minutes after the actual sustained breach. Option C is correct because Cloud Monitoring metric-threshold conditions use an evaluation period over which the condition must hold; configuring a 10-minute duration matches the 'above 80% for at least 10 minutes' requirement directly. Option D is wrong because a 5-minute majority-violation trigger neither matches the 10-minute requirement nor uses the standard duration semantics that Cloud Monitoring policies expose.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: A Cloud Logging log sink is failing to deliver entries to a BigQuery destination. The Log Router metrics show non-zero export_error_count. What is the single most likely cause?
options: The sink's writer identity service account lacks the BigQuery Data Editor role on the destination dataset, which is required for Log Router to create date-sharded or partitioned tables and insert rows
The destination BigQuery dataset was created in a different region from the source project, which violates the strict same-region requirement enforced by Cloud Logging Router for streaming inserts
The sink filter expression includes a backreference to a regex capture group, which BigQuery's streaming insert API rejects because column names cannot contain parenthetical group references
The sink was configured before BigQuery's streaming insert quota for the destination project was raised, and Cloud Logging silently disables the sink whenever it encounters quota exceeded errors
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because Cloud Logging sinks export under a writer identity service account, and the most common cause of export errors to BigQuery is that this writer identity has not been granted BigQuery Data Editor (or equivalent) on the destination dataset; the role is required to create and insert into tables. Option B is wrong because Cloud Logging supports cross-region sink destinations for BigQuery; it is not strictly same-region. Option C is wrong because filter expressions are evaluated by Cloud Logging and do not become BigQuery column names. Option D is wrong because Cloud Logging does not silently disable sinks for quota errors; it surfaces them in export_error_count.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: Which statement about Cloud Monitoring metric kinds is correct?
options: GAUGE metrics record a value that is meaningful only in conjunction with the previous point, such as a running counter that resets at instance restart and must be diff-aligned at query time for display
CUMULATIVE metrics record the absolute timestamp of the most recent event and require special start-time fields, since the value reported is the wall-clock instant when the observation occurred
DELTA metrics record the change in value over a sampling interval, where each point represents the count or sum of observations that occurred during the interval starting from the previous sample
STEPPED metrics record a state that holds steady until explicitly updated, and are unique in that the time series does not produce points unless the value changes from the prior known state
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because that description matches CUMULATIVE, not GAUGE; GAUGE points are independent and meaningful on their own. Option B is wrong because CUMULATIVE values are non-decreasing aggregates tied to a start time, not absolute event timestamps. Option C is correct because DELTA points represent the change (count or sum) observed during the interval (start_time, end_time], and successive deltas are independent observations of activity per interval. Option D is wrong because Cloud Monitoring does not define a STEPPED metric kind; the three kinds are GAUGE, DELTA, and CUMULATIVE.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: You operate a global HTTP(S) Load Balancer fronting a Cloud Run service. You want to compute the request-based availability SLI as the fraction of HTTP 2xx responses out of all responses. Which Cloud Monitoring metric is most appropriate as the SLI's good and total request streams?
options: Use run.googleapis.com/request_count for both the good stream (filtered to response_code_class=2xx) and the total stream (no filter), grouping by service_name to compute the ratio per Cloud Run service
Use loadbalancing.googleapis.com/https/request_count for both the good stream (filtered to response_code_class=2xx) and total stream (no filter), giving SLI fidelity at the load balancer layer
Use a log-based COUNTER metric over Cloud Run request logs, with two separate metrics: one counting 2xx entries and one counting all entries, then divide the two via MQL within the SLI definition
Use Cloud Trace span counts grouping by HTTP status code, then materialize a custom metric whose value is the percentage of 2xx-tagged spans across the rolling 28-day SLO compliance window
