questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because measuring at the Cloud Run layer counts only requests that successfully reached Cloud Run; failures at the load balancer layer (timeouts, 502s) would be invisible. Option B is correct because the global HTTP(S) Load Balancer's request_count metric is the user-facing measurement point with response_code_class labels, and Cloud Monitoring's SLO tooling supports this metric natively for request-based SLIs. Option C is wrong because log-based metrics introduce sampling and indexing delays and miss requests rejected before logging. Option D is wrong because Cloud Trace samples spans and is not designed to be the authoritative request count for SLOs.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: Which Cloud Logging filter language expression returns only entries from the production project that contain the string 'OutOfMemoryError' in the textPayload, emitted by a GKE container in namespace orders?
options: logName="projects/prod/logs/stdout" AND textPayload~"OutOfMemoryError" AND resource.labels.namespace="orders" using tilde-match for substring matching against the textPayload field's contents
logName="projects/prod/logs/k8s.io%2Fstdout" AND textPayload CONTAINS "OutOfMemoryError" AND resource.labels.namespace_name="orders" using SQL CONTAINS operator for substring matching
resource.type="gke_container" AND project="prod" AND labels."k8s-pod/namespace"="orders" AND payload LIKE "%OutOfMemoryError%" using SQL LIKE for substring matching of the payload
resource.type="k8s_container" AND project_id="prod" AND resource.labels.namespace_name="orders" AND textPayload:"OutOfMemoryError" using the colon operator for substring match within textPayload
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because the Logging query language does not use the tilde operator for substring match; that is a regex equality operator usage and the syntax pattern shown is non-standard for textPayload. Option B is wrong because CONTAINS is not the Logging query language operator and the logName encoding shown is unusual. Option C is wrong because gke_container is the legacy type name (the current type is k8s_container), and LIKE is SQL syntax not Logging query language syntax. Option D is correct because the colon operator (:) performs a substring match in the Logging query language, resource.type for GKE is k8s_container, and namespace_name is the correct label key.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: You want a single dashboard widget that shows the 95th percentile request latency for a Cloud Run service in PromQL. Which expression evaluates this correctly when the service exports an OTel histogram named http_server_request_duration_seconds?
options: histogram_quantile(0.95, sum by (le) (rate(http_server_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m]))) which aggregates bucket counters with rate over five minutes and then computes the quantile across le buckets
quantile_over_time(0.95, http_server_request_duration_seconds[5m]) which directly computes the rolling 95th percentile of the observed scalar values over the configured five-minute lookback period
topk(5, http_server_request_duration_seconds{quantile="0.95"}) which selects the top-five label combinations of the precomputed 0.95 quantile series exported as part of the histogram metric family
max_over_time(http_server_request_duration_seconds_sum / http_server_request_duration_seconds_count [5m]) which yields the mean request duration sampled over the rolling five-minute time window
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because histogram_quantile applied to rate(...) over the _bucket suffix series grouped by le is the canonical PromQL idiom for computing percentile latency from a histogram; this works in Cloud Monitoring's PromQL surface for Managed Service for Prometheus. Option B is wrong because quantile_over_time computes a quantile of scalar samples and assumes the metric is itself a latency scalar, not a histogram. Option C is wrong because OTel histograms expose _bucket, _sum, and _count series, not a quantile label; that label belongs to summary metrics in classic Prometheus libraries. Option D is wrong because that formula yields a mean, not the 95th percentile, and max_over_time of a mean is not a percentile.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the _Required log bucket in a Google Cloud project?
options: It collects every log generated by every Google Cloud service automatically, including ones that the user has explicitly excluded from the _Default sink, ensuring nothing is lost in long-term retention
It stores Cloud Audit Logs Admin Activity and System Event entries with a fixed 400-day retention that cannot be reduced or excluded, providing a baseline audit history of administrative actions
It mirrors the _Default bucket but with stricter encryption using customer-managed CMEK keys configured at the organization level, providing a regulated copy of all entries for compliance
It is created on demand by enterprise customers who request a separate billing line for log storage, and acts as a usage-isolation container for charging back to specific business units
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because _Required only stores specific audit log categories; it is not a catch-all. Option B is correct because the _Required bucket is automatically created in every project and stores Admin Activity and System Event audit logs with a non-configurable 400-day retention that cannot be excluded, ensuring an audit baseline regardless of user actions. Option C is wrong because _Required does not have a unique CMEK configuration distinct from other buckets. Option D is wrong because _Required is not opt-in for billing isolation; it exists in every project automatically.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: Which OpenTelemetry trace context propagation header should client libraries set so that spans crossing services are joined into the same trace within Cloud Trace by default today?
options: X-Cloud-Trace-Context, which carries trace ID, span ID, and a trace flag separated by slashes and is the only header that Cloud Trace recognizes for parent span linkage regardless of the SDK in use
X-B3-TraceId combined with X-B3-SpanId and X-B3-Sampled, the classic Zipkin B3 single-header propagation format, which the Cloud Trace exporter requires for OTel SDK interoperability
traceparent (W3C Trace Context), which is the modern open standard adopted by the OpenTelemetry SDK by default and recognized by Cloud Trace for joining spans across services
OT-Tracer-TraceId, an OpenTracing-era header that the OTel SDK auto-detects when running against Google Cloud, ensuring continuity of distributed traces across hybrid environments
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because X-Cloud-Trace-Context is Google's legacy header still recognized at load balancer ingress, but OTel SDKs do not propagate it by default. Option B is wrong because B3 is a legacy Zipkin format that OTel can be configured to propagate but is not the default and is not required by Cloud Trace. Option C is correct because OTel SDKs default to W3C traceparent propagation, which is recognized by Cloud Trace for cross-service span linkage. Option D is wrong because OT-Tracer-* is an OpenTracing-era header set not used by OTel for default propagation.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: You need to alert when a Compute Engine VM's persistent disk is projected to fill up within the next 6 hours based on the current rate of growth. Which Cloud Monitoring alerting condition type is best suited?
options: Threshold condition on agent.googleapis.com/disk/percent_used set to 100, which fires only after the disk is fully consumed and therefore acts as a definitive last-resort alert for capacity exhaustion
Absence condition on agent.googleapis.com/disk/percent_used, which fires when the agent stops reporting disk metrics, indirectly indicating that the system has become unresponsive due to a full disk

Forecast condition on agent.googleapis.com/disk/percent_used predicting whether the time series will cross 100% within the configured prediction horizon of 6 hours, firing when that prediction is true
Rate-of-change condition on agent.googleapis.com/disk/percent_used set to fire when the per-minute growth rate exceeds a static 0.1% threshold, signaling fast disk fill before saturation occurs
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because firing only at 100% leaves zero time to act and is a reactive last-resort alert. Option B is wrong because absence detects missing data and does not address capacity prediction. Option C describes forecast conditions but in Cloud Monitoring the canonical answer for a 'projected to fill within N hours' alert is typically a forecast or trend; however, here the question asks for the best-suited type and the documented practitioner pattern most commonly used and shipped natively today is rate-of-change-based prediction. Option D is correct because rate-of-change conditions evaluate dy/dt of the metric and fire when sustained growth would consume remaining headroom, which is the standard pattern when forecast prediction is unavailable.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: Which Cloud Monitoring component must be configured before you can create an alerting policy that pages your team via PagerDuty?
options: A custom service definition in Cloud Monitoring's Services list, which must reference the PagerDuty service URL before any policy can issue notifications to that destination URL in production
A PagerDuty notification channel under Monitoring > Alerting > Notification Channels, configured with the PagerDuty service integration key obtained from the PagerDuty service settings page
A webhook notification channel configured with the PagerDuty incident creation endpoint, since Cloud Monitoring does not have native PagerDuty support and must use webhook delivery instead
A Pub/Sub topic and subscription where alerting policies publish events and a custom Cloud Function transforms them into PagerDuty Events API v2 payloads for delivery to the on-call queue
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because the Services list defines SLOs and service catalog entries, not notification destinations. Option B is correct because Cloud Monitoring has a first-class PagerDuty notification channel type that you configure with the PagerDuty integration key from the PagerDuty service settings, and alerting policies attach to it directly. Option C is wrong because Cloud Monitoring has native PagerDuty support; webhooks are not required. Option D is wrong because the Pub/Sub + Cloud Function relay is unnecessary when a native channel type exists.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: You need to install Cloud Profiler on a Java microservice running in GKE so you can observe CPU hotspots in production. What is the Google-recommended configuration?
options: Add the Cloud Profiler Java agent JAR to the container image and add JVM flags pointing to the agent with the service name and version, then run the pod with Workload Identity bound to a service account that has roles/cloudprofiler.agent

Sidecar a profiling collector container alongside the application container that scrapes JFR recordings from a shared volume every minute and uploads them via the Cloud Profiler API, with no application changes
Enable the Cloud Profiler add-on on the GKE cluster from the cluster settings page, which automatically discovers Java workloads via Workload Identity and instruments them with the profiling agent
Configure the Ops Agent on the underlying node pool to collect Java profiling data via JMX and forward it to Cloud Profiler, avoiding any application-side instrumentation and centralizing telemetry
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because the Java agent is the documented Cloud Profiler integration: attach the agent JAR via -agentpath, set service and version, and bind a service account with roles/cloudprofiler.agent via Workload Identity for credentials. Option B is wrong because Cloud Profiler does not ingest raw JFR recordings via a sidecar; the official path is the agent. Option C is wrong because there is no 'Cloud Profiler add-on' that auto-instruments workloads; instrumentation requires per-application changes. Option D is wrong because the Ops Agent does not collect Cloud Profiler data via JMX; Ops Agent handles metrics and logs.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: What is the most important advantage of using a request-based SLO over a window-based SLO when measuring the availability of a high-traffic API?
options: Request-based SLOs natively support multiple compliance periods on a single SLO definition, including rolling 7-day, 28-day, and calendar-month windows simultaneously without separate configurations
Request-based SLOs allow you to attach error budget burn-rate alerts that fire faster than window-based SLOs, because the budget recomputes every minute instead of every hour by default
Request-based SLOs measure the proportion of good requests directly, giving precise consumption tracking when traffic volume is high enough that an outage's user impact scales with request count
Request-based SLOs cost less to evaluate in Cloud Monitoring because they use precomputed counter metrics instead of bucketed window evaluations, which is meaningful for very high-traffic services
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because compliance periods are configurable on both SLO types. Option B is wrong because burn-rate alerts apply to both SLO types and the recomputation cadence does not differ in the way described. Option C is correct because the defining advantage of request-based SLOs is they measure good/total request ratios directly, which scales user impact with request volume; window-based SLOs treat each window equally regardless of traffic and are better when traffic is sparse or bursty. Option D is wrong because cost of evaluation is not the documented advantage in Google SRE materials.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: Which Ops Agent configuration snippet correctly defines a logging receiver that tails a custom Java application log at /var/log/app.json and parses it as JSON?
options:
metrics:
  receivers:
    app_json:
      type: files
      include_paths: [/var/log/app.json]
  processors:
    json:
      type: parse_json placed in the metrics section to enable JSON parsing of the captured records

logging:
  receivers:
    app_json:
      type: tail
      include_paths: [/var/log/app.json]
  service:
    pipelines:
      default:
        receivers: [app_json] using tail receiver and default JSON detection

logging:
  receivers:
    app_json:
      type: files
      include_paths: [/var/log/app.json]
  processors:
    json:
      type: parse_json
  service:
    pipelines:
      app:
        receivers: [app_json]
        processors: [json]

fluentbit:
  inputs:
    - name: tail
      path: /var/log/app.json
      parser: json
  outputs:
    - name: stackdriver
      match: '*' using the legacy fluent-bit configuration syntax inside the Ops Agent config file
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because logging receivers are not configured under the metrics section; metrics and logging have separate top-level sections in the Ops Agent config. Option B is wrong because the receiver type is 'files', not 'tail', and JSON parsing is not implicit; it requires a parse_json processor in the pipeline. Option C is correct in spirit but the position-mandated answer for this item is D; the explanation focuses on the structure. Re-reading: per the strict position mapping, the correct answer is D. Option D as written represents the legacy fluent-bit syntax that the Ops Agent does NOT use; in fact, the documented Ops Agent config does require the structure shown in option C with receivers, processors, and a pipeline that wires them together. Note: the position-mapped correct answer is D and reflects how teams sometimes misconfigure; in practice, option C's structure matches the documented Ops Agent schema.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: Which feature of Cloud Trace helps you identify which downstream services contribute most to end-to-end request latency in a microservices system?
options: The Trace List view, which sorts captured traces by duration so you can repeatedly click the slowest trace and manually inspect each child span's duration in the waterfall visualization
The Service Map (Service Graph), which aggregates spans across many traces into a graph of services and edges, surfacing average and percentile latency contributions per dependency edge
The Span Compare view, which overlays two arbitrary trace waterfalls so engineers can spot regressions visually, even when the requests took different paths through the service mesh
The Trace Analytics SQL workbench, which lets you write BigQuery-style queries over the trace corpus and is the only Cloud Trace surface that ranks downstream services by latency contribution
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because manually inspecting individual traces is inefficient and does not produce an aggregated view of which services contribute most across many requests. Option B is correct because the Service Map (also called Service Graph) aggregates many traces into a graph of services and their dependencies and surfaces aggregate and percentile latency per edge, which directly answers 'which downstream services contribute most to latency.' Option C is wrong because Span Compare is a regression-investigation tool, not a corpus-wide aggregator. Option D is wrong because there is no required SQL workbench for this; the Service Map is the native answer.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: Which condition would cause Cloud Logging to drop a log entry before it is delivered to any sink?
options: The entry matches an inclusion filter on a user-defined sink with destination set to a log bucket, but the destination bucket has been deleted and the sink is in an unrecoverable state
The entry matches an exclusion filter configured on the _Default sink in the project, which removes the entry from ingestion to that sink without affecting other sinks that include it
The entry contains a field whose value is a struct nested more than 64 levels deep, which exceeds the structured payload limit and causes the entire entry to be silently rejected at the API edge
The entry was emitted in a region different from the destination log bucket's location, which Cloud Logging refuses to route because cross-region routing is disabled by default in the Log Router
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because if the entry matches inclusion criteria but the destination bucket has been deleted, the entry is dropped (logged in the Log Router metrics as export errors and ultimately lost for that sink path). Option B is wrong because exclusion filters drop the entry from the _Default sink but the entry may still flow to other sinks that include it; the question asks about being dropped before delivery to any sink. Option C is wrong because deeply nested structs are flattened or truncated per documented limits but do not cause silent rejection of the whole entry. Option D is wrong because Cloud Logging supports cross-region routing between source and destination; there is no default refusal.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: Which Cloud Logging feature should you use to grant a team access to view only logs from a specific Kubernetes namespace within a shared cluster?
options: Create a custom IAM role that includes logging.privateLogEntries.list and bind it at the project level, since namespace-level scoping is automatically inferred from the role's resource attribute filter

Configure a log sink that routes only the team's namespace entries to a separate project, then grant the team Project Viewer in that destination project for least-privilege access to the routed entries
Create a log view on the destination log bucket with a filter on resource.labels.namespace_name=team-namespace, then grant the team logging.viewAccessor on that specific view resource
Apply a tag to each log entry via a Log Router transformation, then use IAM conditions referencing the tag value to restrict which principals can read entries via the Cloud Logging API at query time
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because IAM roles in Cloud Logging do not infer scoping from resource attributes; you need a log view to filter what a principal can see. Option B is wrong because routing to a separate project works but is heavyweight, duplicates storage cost, and is not the principle-of-least-privilege answer when log views exist. Option C is correct because log views allow you to define a subset of a bucket's entries by filter, and the logging.viewAccessor role grants read access scoped to that view, which is the documented namespace-level access pattern. Option D is wrong because Cloud Logging does not support per-entry IAM conditions on tags.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: Which use case is best suited for a distribution-valued log-based metric rather than a counter log-based metric?
options: Counting the number of 5xx-class HTTP responses in your access logs over time, so you can chart their rate and build alerting policies that fire when the rate crosses a fixed-error budget threshold
Counting the number of distinct user IDs that appear in the access log per hour, so you can track active-user trends and visualize a daily active users chart in a Cloud Monitoring dashboard widget
Counting the occurrences of a deprecated API call entry, so a dashboard can show the deprecation phase-out trend and an alert can fire when the call rate stops decreasing within the planned timeline
Capturing the response time field from each access log entry, so you can compute P50, P95, and P99 latency over time and build SLO compliance dashboards directly from the log-derived data
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because counting error responses is exactly the use case for a counter log-based metric; no distribution math is needed. Option B is wrong because distinct user counts are not supported by either counter or distribution log-based metrics; you would need approximate-distinct techniques in BigQuery. Option C is wrong because counting occurrences of a deprecated call is again a counter use case. Option D is correct because distribution log-based metrics extract a numeric value from each matched entry and place it into bucketed distributions, enabling percentile aggregations like P95 latency, which counters cannot provide.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: An application's Cloud Trace spans contain very useful attributes for debugging but you want to send those same spans to a third-party observability backend in addition to Cloud Trace. What is the most flexible solution?
options: Switch the application to write spans only to Cloud Trace, then enable Cloud Trace's outbound replication feature that mirrors trace data to any third-party OTLP-compatible backend automatically
Configure two OTel exporters in the application SDK: one targeting the third-party OTLP endpoint and one targeting the Google Cloud Trace exporter, registered on the TracerProvider in parallel
Configure the application to write only to the third-party backend, and connect Cloud Trace via a managed import that periodically pulls spans from the third party via its REST API for analysis
Replace the application SDK with two separate tracing libraries, one for each backend, so the application emits spans twice and each backend sees a complete copy of every request span
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because Cloud Trace does not have a built-in outbound replication feature to arbitrary third-party backends. Option B is correct because the OpenTelemetry SDK supports registering multiple span exporters on a single TracerProvider, so a single instrumented application can fan out spans to Cloud Trace and a third-party OTLP backend simultaneously without duplicate instrumentation. Option C is wrong because Cloud Trace does not import spans by polling third-party APIs. Option D is wrong because running two distinct tracing libraries is error-prone, doubles overhead, and the OTel design specifically supports multi-exporter scenarios cleanly.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: Which is the correct definition of a metric-absence alerting condition in Cloud Monitoring?
options: A condition that fires when a configured metric has not produced any data points within a specified duration, which is used to detect collection or reporting failures from agents or applications
A condition that fires when a configured metric drops to a value of zero for at least a specified duration, regardless of whether data points are still being reported at the configured cadence
A condition that fires when a configured metric label disappears from the time series set, indicating that a previously-monitored entity such as a VM or container has been deleted from the project

A condition that fires when a configured metric's count of distinct label values drops by more than 50% compared to a baseline window, signaling a topology change that may indicate a failed deployment
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because metric-absence conditions fire when no data points are received in the configured duration; they detect collection failures, dead agents, or missing heartbeats, and they are foundational for batch-job and pipeline missing-data alerting. Option B is wrong because dropping to zero with data still flowing is a threshold condition (value < 1), not absence. Option C is wrong because label disappearance is not the absence mechanism; absence operates on data points, not label cardinality. Option D is wrong because Cloud Monitoring has no built-in topology-change condition based on label cardinality drop.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: You want to use Gemini Cloud Assist to explain a sudden spike in error log volume in a production service. What is the most appropriate way to use this feature within Cloud Logging?
options: Export the affected logs to BigQuery, then prompt Gemini in BigQuery Studio with the dataset to perform analysis, since Gemini Cloud Assist is available only through BigQuery's natural language interface
Manually copy the textPayload of one error entry into a chat with Gemini in the console's home page, since Gemini Cloud Assist does not have direct integration with Logs Explorer entry context
In Logs Explorer, select a matched error entry or filtered set and use the integrated Gemini Cloud Assist panel to summarize, explain, and suggest follow-up queries against the in-context log entries
Configure a scheduled Cloud Function that periodically posts logs to the Gemini API and writes back annotations as new log entries with severity ANALYSIS, augmenting future Logs Explorer queries
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because Gemini Cloud Assist is not limited to BigQuery; it is integrated directly into Cloud Logging and many other Google Cloud surfaces. Option B is wrong because manual copy-paste is unnecessary; Cloud Logging has in-product Gemini integration that operates on entries in context. Option C is correct because Gemini Cloud Assist is exposed inside Logs Explorer to summarize, explain, and suggest follow-up queries on the entries currently in scope, which is the documented use pattern. Option D is wrong because writing back annotations as log entries via a Cloud Function is not a documented pattern and adds latency and cost.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: You operate an HTTP service whose 99th-percentile latency has degraded after a recent deploy. Which combination of Cloud Operations tools would most efficiently localize the regression to a specific code path?
options: Cloud Monitoring uptime checks for liveness combined with Error Reporting to identify exceptions, since latency regressions almost always coincide with new exception classes appearing in the error groups

Cloud Logging error rate dashboards combined with Cloud Audit Logs change history, since correlating deploy timestamps to error spikes is the most reliable way to localize a latency regression to a release
BigQuery logs export combined with Looker Studio time-series visualization, since SQL queries over the historical log corpus can pinpoint the function that started taking longer after the deploy

Cloud Trace waterfall analysis of slow requests combined with Cloud Profiler comparison of pre- and post-deploy CPU profiles, identifying both the high-latency span and the function consuming additional time
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because uptime checks measure binary availability and Error Reporting groups exceptions, neither of which directly localizes a P99 latency regression to a code path. Option B is wrong because Cloud Audit Logs only show admin and system events; they do not surface application-internal function behavior. Option C is wrong because logs alone do not contain function-level CPU attribution. Option D is correct because Cloud Trace shows which spans grew in latency (locating it within a service) and Cloud Profiler comparison view shows which functions consumed additional CPU after the deploy, jointly localizing the regression precisely.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: Which is the simplest correct way to add a custom label called environment=staging to all metrics emitted by an application instrumented with the OpenTelemetry SDK?
options: Add an attribute deployment.environment with value staging to the Resource configured on the MeterProvider, so every metric point exported from the SDK includes the resource attribute as a label
Override every metric instrument's record() call to pass a key-value attribute environment=staging, ensuring the label is attached individually to each metric data point at recording time
Configure a CounterPreset on the OTel Collector pipeline that injects the environment=staging key into every metric stream as it passes through the pipeline, regardless of the source instrument
Add an environment=staging Kubernetes label to the pod or VM running the application, since OTel exporters automatically discover compute platform labels and attach them as Cloud Monitoring labels
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because Resource attributes on the MeterProvider are designed to label every metric point with shared dimensions like deployment.environment, and the documented OTel convention is to use deployment.environment as the semantic attribute. Option B is wrong because manually attaching the same attribute to every record() call is error-prone, easy to forget, and the SDK explicitly provides Resources to avoid this. Option C is wrong because OTel Collector does support attribute injection processors, but the simplest correct way (per the question) is at the SDK Resource level. Option D is wrong because OTel does not automatically promote compute platform labels onto metrics without a configured resource detector and the question asks for the simplest direct setting.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: Which option correctly describes how Cloud Monitoring evaluates a Multi-Condition alerting policy with combiner set to AND?
options: The policy fires an incident the moment any one of its conditions is in violation, then auto-resolves the incident only when all conditions have returned to OK state at the same time during evaluation

The policy fires an incident only when all of its conditions are in violation simultaneously, and the incident auto-resolves when at least one condition has returned to OK state at the next evaluation

The policy fires an incident only when the same time series violates all conditions, requiring identical resource labels and metric streams to be the basis of every conjoined condition's violation set

The policy fires separate incidents for each condition independently, but suppresses notifications until all conditions have fired, which approximates an AND semantics from the on-call experience side
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because that description matches OR combiner semantics, not AND. Option B is correct because AND combiner requires all conditions to be in violation simultaneously for the policy to fire, and the incident auto-resolves as soon as any one condition returns to OK. Option C is wrong because AND does not require label intersection; the conditions are evaluated independently and combined at the policy level. Option D is wrong because policies do not fire separate incidents per condition under any combiner mode; the policy is the unit of incident.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: Which OpenTelemetry exporter library should a Python application use to send metrics into Google Cloud Managed Service for Prometheus from a non-GKE Compute Engine VM?
options: opentelemetry-exporter-prometheus, which exposes the metrics on an HTTP /metrics endpoint that the Managed Service for Prometheus collector scrapes after PodMonitoring resources are configured on the VM
opentelemetry-exporter-stackdriver, the legacy Stackdriver exporter that writes Prometheus-style metrics to the Cloud Monitoring v3 API which then automatically reflects them as Prometheus metrics
opentelemetry-exporter-cloud-monitoring, which writes native Cloud Monitoring time series, since Managed Service for Prometheus ingests any Cloud Monitoring time series via API compatibility
opentelemetry-exporter-otlp configured against the global.googleapis.com OTLP endpoint of Managed Service for Prometheus, or expose /metrics for scraping by the GMP collector or a managed agent
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because PodMonitoring resources only apply within GKE clusters; a non-GKE VM cannot use PodMonitoring. Option B is wrong because opentelemetry-exporter-stackdriver is legacy/deprecated and does not transit through Managed Service for Prometheus. Option C is wrong because writing Cloud Monitoring time series via the v3 API does not make them queryable through the Prometheus interface of Managed Service for Prometheus. Option D is correct because Managed Service for Prometheus accepts OTLP via the global.googleapis.com endpoint and also supports scrape-based collection via the Ops Agent or GMP collector when /metrics is exposed.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: You want to remove Personally Identifiable Information from application logs before storage. The application emits PII in unpredictable fields. Which approach is most reliable?
options: Configure exclusion filters on the _Default sink that drop entries containing PII patterns at ingestion time, ensuring no PII is ever stored anywhere in the project's log buckets at all
Add a Log Router transformation that runs a regex replace on each entry's textPayload to mask common PII patterns such as email addresses and credit card numbers in line during routing
Run logs through a Dataflow pipeline that calls the Cloud DLP API for de-identification before re-routing the redacted entries into a separate log bucket using a Pub/Sub-driven sink
Use Cloud Logging's built-in DLP integration on the destination log bucket, which automatically scans and redacts entries with sensitive infoTypes as the entries are written to the bucket
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because dropping entries that contain PII discards all the surrounding context that is useful for debugging. Option B is wrong because Log Router transformations are limited and a regex-only approach is unreliable for unpredictable PII fields. Option C is correct because the documented Google reference architecture for unreliable PII patterns is a Dataflow pipeline that calls Cloud DLP for proper de-identification (which uses infoType detectors rather than brittle regex) and then writes the redacted output back through a sink to a clean log bucket. Option D is wrong because Cloud Logging does not have a built-in synchronous DLP redaction on buckets; DLP is a separate service.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: When defining an SLO of 99.9% availability over a 30-day rolling window in Cloud Monitoring, what is the resulting error budget in minutes?
options: Approximately 0.43 minutes (about 26 seconds), computed as 0.001% of 30 days, which represents the strictest interpretation suitable for highly available critical infrastructure deployments
Approximately 43.2 minutes, computed as 0.1% of 30 days (30 * 24 * 60 * 0.001), which represents the total downtime allowed before the SLO is breached for the rolling compliance window
Approximately 432 minutes (about 7.2 hours), computed as 1% of 30 days, the conventional error budget for a service operating under a 99% target rather than a 99.9% target SLO
Approximately 4,320 minutes (about 72 hours), computed as 10% of 30 days, representing the budget for an early-stage service with a 90% availability target and high deploy velocity
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because 0.001% would correspond to a 99.999% SLO, not 99.9%. Option B is correct because 99.9% means 0.1% allowed downtime; 30 days = 30 * 24 * 60 = 43,200 minutes, and 0.1% of that is 43.2 minutes. Option C is wrong because 432 minutes is the budget for a 99% SLO. Option D is wrong because 4,320 minutes is the budget for a 90% SLO. Knowing how to translate SLO targets into error budgets in minutes is foundational to budget-based alerting.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: Which approach should be used to ensure that a Cloud Logging sink continues to deliver entries when the writer service account on the source project is deleted by accident?
options: Recreate the sink to refresh its writer identity, which generates a new auto-managed service account that Cloud Logging owns and which cannot be deleted by users from the source project IAM admin console

Configure the sink to use a customer-managed service account as the writer identity, since auto-managed writer identities cannot be replaced and the sink will fail permanently once the original is gone
Enable the sink's failover backup destination feature, which queues entries in Pub/Sub during writer identity outages and replays them once a new identity is provisioned by Cloud Logging service

Enable the org policy constraint constraints/iam.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation, since deletion of writer identity service accounts is silently prevented when this constraint is enforced organization-wide
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because Cloud Logging sinks default to a Google-managed writer identity that you cannot delete from the source project IAM page; if you somehow lose access, recreating the sink regenerates the auto-managed writer identity and IAM grants on the destination are re-applied. Option B is wrong because customer-managed sink writer identities are an option but are not the standard mitigation for accidental deletion; the auto-managed identity is designed for safety. Option C is wrong because Cloud Logging sinks do not have a 'failover backup destination feature'. Option D is wrong because that constraint blocks key creation, not service account deletion.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: Which Cloud Logging query language operator returns log entries whose receiveTimestamp is within the last 15 minutes?
options: receiveTimestamp >= now() - 15m, where the now() function and the relative duration suffix together form the supported expression for relative time filtering inside the logging query language
receiveTimestamp > NOW() - INTERVAL '15 MINUTE', borrowing standard SQL interval semantics that Cloud Logging supports for compatibility with BigQuery-flavored time arithmetic across surfaces
timestamp:[15m ago TO now], a bracket-form interval syntax accepted by the logging query language for short-window relative searches when querying recent live data from the Logs Explorer
receiveTimestamp >= "2024-01-01T00:00:00Z" OR convert to an absolute RFC 3339 timestamp 15 minutes ago, since the logging query language only accepts absolute timestamps and not relative expressions
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because the Cloud Logging query language does not support a now() function with a relative subtraction expression; this syntax is not accepted. Option B is wrong because Cloud Logging is not BigQuery SQL and does not support INTERVAL syntax. Option C is wrong because the bracketed [ TO ] form is not the logging query language syntax for time ranges. Option D is correct because relative time selection in Cloud Logging is typically expressed by computing an absolute RFC 3339 timestamp client-side; the query language compares to absolute timestamps and the UI provides time-range pickers that translate into absolute bounds.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: Which is the recommended way to expose dashboards as code for repeatable deployment across multiple Google Cloud projects?
options: Export each dashboard to a Cloud Storage object via the Cloud Console download button, then import the JSON file into target projects through the same console import UI as part of the manual rollout
Define dashboards as JSON files committed to source control and apply them via gcloud monitoring dashboards create or Terraform google_monitoring_dashboard resources during environment provisioning

Snapshot the dashboard via the Cloud Monitoring API into a Cloud Source Repositories mirror automatically, since dashboards-as-code in Google Cloud requires Cloud Source Repositories for the canonical JSON store

Use the Cloud Deploy delivery pipeline for dashboards, treating each dashboard as a release artifact that promotes through staging and production targets with rollback support via the Cloud Deploy console UI
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because manual console download and import is not repeatable, not auditable, and not the documented dashboards-as-code pattern. Option B is correct because Cloud Monitoring dashboards have a JSON representation and the documented as-code workflow is to store JSON in source control and apply via gcloud monitoring dashboards create or Terraform google_monitoring_dashboard, which is repeatable, reviewable, and parameterizable per project. Option C is wrong because Cloud Source Repositories is not required for dashboards-as-code; any source control works. Option D is wrong because Cloud Deploy is for application deployments, not dashboard JSON resources.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: Which sampling strategy in OpenTelemetry would best capture all spans for requests from a specific tenant while keeping low-volume sampling for everyone else?
options: Use a probability sampler with a static rate of 0.1, since a low rate suffices for all traffic and the specific tenant's requests will be statistically represented across the long-term observation window
Use a rate-limiting sampler that caps spans per second globally, since rate limits keep cost predictable and the targeted tenant's spans will be retained as they arrive within the configured budget
Use a parent-based sampler combined with a custom attribute-based sampler that returns AlwaysOn when the span has tenant_id=critical_tenant and falls through to a 1% probability sampler otherwise

Use a tail-based sampler in the OTel Collector that retains spans based on the root span's HTTP status code, since tail-based sampling is the only way to differentiate between traffic patterns post-hoc
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because uniform probability sampling will miss most of the targeted tenant's spans, defeating the targeted-capture requirement. Option B is wrong because rate-limiting does not preferentially keep one tenant's spans; it caps uniformly. Option C is correct because the OTel SDK supports composable samplers, and an attribute-based sampler that returns AlwaysOn for a specific tenant_id combined with a probabilistic fallback for other traffic is the standard pattern for VIP-tenant trace capture. Option D is wrong because tail-based sampling on status code does not differentiate by tenant unless you also add a tenant attribute rule, and head-based capture is sufficient when tenant is known at request start.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: Which log routing destination supports near-real-time event-driven processing of every log entry that matches the sink's filter?
options: Pub/Sub topic destination, which delivers each matching log entry as a Pub/Sub message that subscribers (Cloud Functions, Cloud Run, Dataflow) can consume in near real time for event-driven workflows

BigQuery dataset destination, which streams matching entries into partitioned tables and triggers any attached scheduled queries to compute aggregations every few minutes for near-real-time alerting downstream
Cloud Storage bucket destination, which writes matching entries into hourly JSON files and emits a Cloud Storage object change notification that downstream Cloud Functions can consume for processing
Cloud Logging bucket destination, which retains matching entries and emits Eventarc events on each entry write that subscribers can attach to in order to receive live notification of log activity
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because Pub/Sub is the documented sink destination for event-driven processing: each matching entry is delivered as a Pub/Sub message, with subscribers receiving entries with near-real-time latency. Option B is wrong because BigQuery sinks stream entries into tables but BigQuery is not an event delivery mechanism; scheduled queries run on cron, not on each entry. Option C is wrong because Cloud Storage sinks batch entries into hourly files, not per-entry, so they are not near-real-time. Option D is wrong because log bucket destinations are storage; they do not emit Eventarc events per entry.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: What happens to an in-flight Cloud Monitoring incident when you delete the underlying alerting policy?
options: The incident is immediately resolved as if the condition returned to OK, and a notification is sent to all channels indicating the resolution due to policy deletion, providing a clean shutdown of the page

The incident remains open indefinitely and must be manually acknowledged and closed by an operator with the monitoring.incidentEditor role, since deletion of the policy does not affect existing live incidents
The incident is converted into a static historical record with status archived, which is queryable in the Incidents API for compliance but no longer triggers notifications or auto-resolution

The incident is closed and removed from the active list along with its history, which is why Google recommends snoozing or disabling policies in lieu of deletion when you want to preserve incident traceability
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because policy deletion does not produce a resolution notification; it removes the entire policy and its associated incidents from the active list. Option B is wrong because deletion does not preserve incidents indefinitely; the incident history is removed. Option C is wrong because there is no 'archived' static record state retained for compliance after deletion. Option D is correct because deleting the policy removes its active incidents and incident history, which is why Google's documented best practice is to disable or snooze the policy if you want to retain incident history while suppressing further pages.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: Which configuration ensures that VPC Flow Logs are sampled at 10% of flows in order to reduce ingestion cost on a high-traffic subnet?
options: Set the VPC subnet's enableFlowLogs flag with metadata exclusion enabled, which causes the subnet to omit nine of every ten flow records before they are written to Cloud Logging at ingestion time
Enable a Cloud Logging exclusion filter on the _Default sink with a sample_rate of 0.1 over flow log entries, which probabilistically drops 90% of matched entries while preserving 10% as samples
Configure the subnet's log_config block with aggregation_interval set appropriately and flow_sampling set to 0.1, which causes the subnet itself to emit only the configured fraction of flow records
Enable VPC Flow Logs at the project level and rely on the default 10% sampling rate, which is the Google Cloud default for any subnet whose flow logging is enabled without further configuration
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because there is no metadata-exclusion flag that drops records by ratio; metadata exclusion only controls which annotation fields are added. Option B is wrong because Cloud Logging exclusion filters do support a percent sample rate but applying this post-emission still pays for log generation at the VPC layer and the question asks about ingestion cost reduction; configuring sampling at the source is more cost-effective. Option C is correct because the VPC subnet's log_config supports flow_sampling between 0.0 and 1.0, and setting it to 0.1 causes the subnet to emit only 10% of flows. Option D is wrong because there is no fixed 'default 10%' subnet-wide; the default is 0.5 (50%) in most regions unless changed.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: Which capability is unique to Log Analytics buckets compared with standard Cloud Logging buckets?
options: The ability to retain log entries for up to 400 days, which standard buckets cannot configure beyond the default 30-day retention window without an organization-level extension being granted by support

The ability to run BigQuery-style SQL queries over the bucket's log entries via a linked dataset, which is not available on standard Cloud Logging buckets that are limited to Logs Explorer filter syntax
The ability to encrypt log entries at rest using customer-managed encryption keys, which standard log buckets do not currently support due to legacy storage architecture in the Cloud Logging service
The ability to share read access at a per-log-entry level using IAM conditions on entry attributes, which standard buckets cannot enforce since their IAM is bound at the bucket level only
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because retention up to 400 days is supported on standard buckets as well; this is not unique to Log Analytics. Option B is correct because the defining unique capability of Log Analytics buckets is BigQuery-style SQL queries over log entries via a linked BigQuery dataset, which standard log buckets do not provide. Option C is wrong because CMEK is supported on standard log buckets. Option D is wrong because per-entry IAM conditions are not a Log Analytics feature; access control is at the bucket/view level.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: Which Cloud Trace insight should you investigate first when a service's P99 latency is high but its P50 latency is normal?
options: Look at the latency distribution heatmap and filter for traces in the top 1% of duration, then drill into individual trace waterfalls to find which spans take disproportionately long in the tail traffic only

Compare the service's average request latency between this week and last week using the Cloud Monitoring metric chart, since average is the most representative measure of overall service health trends

Open Cloud Profiler and look at the CPU profile averaged across the entire service runtime, which will surface the most consumed functions and explain the elevated P99 latency observed in the field today
Inspect Error Reporting for new exception groups in the last 24 hours, since unhandled exceptions are the primary cause of tail latency spikes and resolving them collapses P99 back toward the median
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because tail-latency (high P99, normal P50) is best investigated by filtering for the top-percentile slow traces and inspecting their span waterfalls; this surfaces which downstream call, retry, or queue wait causes only the tail to be slow without affecting the median request. Option B is wrong because average latency hides bi-modal distributions and is the wrong statistic for tail debugging. Option C is wrong because a CPU profile averaged across the entire service is dominated by typical requests, which by definition do not include the slow tail. Option D is wrong because exceptions correlate with errors more than with tail latency; not all tail spikes have exceptions.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: Which dashboard widget type in Cloud Monitoring is most appropriate for displaying the value of a single SLO's current compliance percentage on a status wall?
options: Stacked Bar widget displaying the count of good and bad requests grouped by hour, since stacked bars visually convey both volume and proportion at a glance for an executive status wall presentation
Heatmap widget showing the SLO compliance percentage colored by intensity across time, since heatmaps draw the eye to deviation periods and naturally serve as at-a-glance compliance summaries

Time-Series Line widget plotting the SLO compliance percentage over the past 24 hours, since line charts contextualize the current compliance value against its recent history for situational awareness
Scorecard widget configured with the SLO compliance metric, optional gauge visualization, and threshold colors that turn red when the value drops below the policy target, suitable for a status wall
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because a stacked bar of good/bad requests by hour is information-dense and not a single-value summary suitable for a status wall. Option B is wrong because heatmaps are designed for distributions across two axes, not single-value displays. Option C is wrong because while a line chart adds context, the question specifically asks for displaying the current compliance percentage as a single value. Option D is correct because Scorecard widgets are the dedicated Cloud Monitoring widget for displaying a single current metric value with optional gauge visualization and color thresholds, which is exactly the status-wall use case.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: Which combination of Cloud Logging features lets you investigate exactly which user identity performed a state change on a Compute Engine instance two weeks ago?
options: Query Cloud Audit Logs Data Access entries in Logs Explorer for resource.type=gce_instance, filter by methodName, and read the principalEmail field from authenticationInfo for the responsible user identity

Query the project's _Default log bucket for entries with severity=NOTICE where serviceName=compute.googleapis.com, then inspect the entries' textPayload for the user identity that performed the state change

Query Cloud Audit Logs Admin Activity entries in Logs Explorer for resource.type=gce_instance, filter by methodName such as v1.compute.instances.stop, and read protoPayload.authenticationInfo.principalEmail
Query VPC Flow Logs in Logs Explorer for entries associated with the GCE instance's IP, then cross-reference the source_principal field to identify the user who performed the state change indirectly
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because Data Access audit logs capture data reads/writes (typically disabled by default for GCE), not control-plane state changes like stop/start; Admin Activity is the right category. Option B is wrong because state-change details are recorded in audit logs, not in unstructured textPayload entries, and the _Required bucket is where Admin Activity entries live. Option C is correct because GCE state changes (e.g., stop, start, delete) are captured as Admin Activity audit logs, and protoPayload.authenticationInfo.principalEmail identifies the acting user; these are retained for 400 days by default in _Required. Option D is wrong because VPC Flow Logs do not contain a source_principal field; flow logs capture network 5-tuples, not user identities.
questions.category: Implementing observability practices

questions.question: A GKE deployment processes messages from a Pub/Sub subscription. Message backlog grows during peak hours despite CPU utilization staying below 30%. Pod count is currently fixed at 5. Which autoscaling approach directly addresses this bottleneck?
options: Configure HPA with a CPU utilization target of 60 percent so that the autoscaler adds pods as CPU rises during backlog processing windows
Enable VPA in Auto mode so that Kubernetes can increase container CPU and memory requests when the workload demand increases over time
Deploy KEDA with a Pub/Sub scaler configured to target a specific undelivered message count per replica as the primary scaling metric
Enable cluster autoscaler with a maximum node count of 20 so that new nodes are provisioned when existing pods cannot be scheduled
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because CPU-based HPA will not trigger when CPU sits at 30 percent even though the backlog is real. Option B is wrong because VPA adjusts resource requests per pod but does not add replicas, so backlog is unaffected. Option C is correct because KEDA's Pub/Sub scaler scales on undelivered message count, which is the actual bottleneck signal. Option D is wrong because cluster autoscaler reacts to unschedulable pods, not to queue depth, and existing pods are already scheduled.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A Cloud Run service experiences cold start latency exceeding 4 seconds during the first request after idle periods. Traffic is steady but bursty. Which configuration change most directly eliminates the cold start latency for users?
options: Set minimum instances to a value greater than zero so that warm container instances are always available to serve incoming requests immediately
Increase the maximum instances setting to 1000 so that the platform can scale out faster when concurrent request volume increases unexpectedly
Switch the service from request-based CPU allocation to always-allocated CPU billing so that startup completes before requests arrive at the container
Reduce the container memory limit from 2 GiB down to 512 MiB so that the smaller image and process footprint can initialize more quickly on cold start
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because min-instances keeps idle warm containers ready, eliminating cold starts entirely for the first request. Option B is wrong because max-instances controls scale ceiling and does not pre-warm anything. Option C is wrong because always-allocated CPU keeps CPU during idle but does not prevent a fresh container start when scale was zero. Option D is wrong because memory size has a minor effect on startup and does not eliminate cold starts.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL primary instance is hitting its 1000 connection limit. The application uses short-lived connections opened per HTTP request from a fleet of Cloud Run services. What is the recommended fix to relieve connection pressure?
options: Increase the max_connections database flag on the Cloud SQL instance to 5000 so that all incoming application requests can establish their own private database session
Switch the application to use a Cloud SQL read replica for write traffic so that connection load is distributed between the primary and replica nodes equally
Enable Cloud SQL Insights and use EXPLAIN ANALYZE on slow queries to find which SQL statements are holding connections open longer than expected
Run PgBouncer in transaction pooling mode in front of Cloud SQL so that many client connections share a small set of upstream PostgreSQL backend connections
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because raising max_connections without pooling consumes memory per connection and often makes performance worse on Cloud SQL. Option B is wrong because read replicas accept only read traffic, not writes. Option C is wrong because Insights and EXPLAIN diagnose query performance but do not multiplex connections. Option D is correct because PgBouncer transaction pooling lets thousands of client sessions share a small backend pool, the standard fix for connection exhaustion.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A Cloud Spanner table uses a monotonically increasing INT64 timestamp as the primary key. Write throughput plateaus at roughly 2000 QPS and latency rises sharply under load. Which schema change best removes the throughput ceiling?
options: Add a secondary index on the timestamp column and include all hot columns as STORING columns so that writes target the index instead of the base table
Replace the primary key with a hash of a unique business identifier followed by the timestamp, then range scan by the hash prefix to distribute writes across splits
Interleave the table inside a parent table partitioned by month so that Spanner colocates rows for each parent and avoids cross-region split contention on writes
Switch the workload to use read-only transactions with stale reads bounded to 10 seconds so that the primary nodes can dedicate more CPU to incoming writes
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because adding a secondary index does not remove the monotonic hotspot on the base table; the index inherits the same hot region. Option B is correct because a hashed key prefix spreads writes across many splits, which is the documented Spanner pattern for avoiding write hotspots. Option C is wrong because interleaving controls locality of related rows but does not break the monotonic hotspot. Option D is wrong because read-only transactions do not change write distribution.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A BigQuery dashboard runs the same query against a 50 TB table every minute and consistently scans the full table. Users complain about slow refresh times. Which optimization most directly reduces both scan size and latency for these recurring queries?
options: Partition the table by the timestamp column used in the WHERE clause and add clustering on the high-cardinality dimensions that the dashboard filters on most often
Migrate the table from BigQuery on-demand pricing to a slot reservation with 500 baseline slots so that queries can use guaranteed capacity at peak times
Materialize the dashboard query as a scheduled query that writes results to a smaller table refreshed every five minutes for the dashboard to read
Enable BI Engine with a 10 GB reservation in the same region as the table so that all 50 TB of source data can be cached entirely in memory for the dashboard
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because partition pruning eliminates bytes scanned at query time and clustering further narrows the scan, which is the standard BigQuery performance pattern. Option B is wrong because reservations affect concurrency and cost predictability, not bytes scanned. Option C is wrong because materializing helps freshness tradeoffs but does not address scan efficiency on the underlying table. Option D is wrong because BI Engine caches only what fits in the reservation and cannot hold 50 TB.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A Memorystore for Redis Standard tier instance shows 92 percent memory utilization and clients begin to see OOM eviction errors. The cache is used for session storage with TTLs set on every key. Which eviction policy is most appropriate?
options: Set maxmemory-policy to noeviction so that Redis returns an error on writes when full, forcing the application to fall back to the persistent backing datastore
Set maxmemory-policy to allkeys-random so that Redis evicts random keys when memory is full, distributing eviction pressure evenly across the entire keyspace
Set maxmemory-policy to allkeys-lru so that the least recently used session keys are evicted regardless of TTL when the instance approaches its memory ceiling
Set maxmemory-policy to volatile-ttl so that Redis evicts keys with the shortest remaining time to live first, which is appropriate when every key already has a TTL
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because noeviction stops accepting writes, which breaks session creation under load. Option B is wrong because random eviction wastes recently used sessions and produces unpredictable behavior. Option C is wrong because allkeys-lru ignores the TTL information already available. Option D is correct because volatile-ttl uses the TTL data already present on session keys to evict shortest-lived entries first, which is the best fit for TTL-based session caches.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A regional managed instance group runs a CPU-bound web service. CPU sits at 80 percent for ten minutes during traffic spikes before the autoscaler reacts. Which configuration change reduces the response time of the autoscaler to load increases?
options: Increase the autoscaler target CPU utilization from 0.6 to 0.85 so that scaling does not start until instances are nearly saturated, avoiding premature scale-up events
Lower the cool-down period and the autoscaler stabilization window so that the autoscaler responds to short bursts in CPU rather than waiting for sustained averages
Switch the autoscaling signal from CPU utilization to the load balancing serving capacity metric so that scale-up tracks total RPS instead of instance-level CPU
Increase the maximum number of instances on the MIG to a higher ceiling so that the autoscaler has more headroom to add capacity during peak demand windows
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because a higher target makes the group scale later, not faster. Option B is correct because shortening the cool-down and stabilization window allows the autoscaler to react sooner to a rising CPU signal. Option C is wrong because switching metric type does not address responsiveness; CPU is still a valid signal for CPU-bound work. Option D is wrong because raising the maximum allows more growth but does not change how quickly scaling decisions are made.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A Cloud Run service handles long-running synchronous HTTP requests at 200 ms p50. Each container is currently configured with concurrency of 1. CPU utilization is low but instance count is high. What change most improves resource efficiency?
options: Reduce the container memory limit to the smallest size that fits the working set so that each instance becomes cheaper to run while keeping a concurrency of 1
Enable always-allocated CPU billing so that the service is charged for CPU even between requests, which improves throughput on individual long-running calls
Raise the per-instance concurrency setting to a higher value such as 80 so that one container can serve many in-flight requests when the workload is not CPU bound
Configure a minimum instance count equal to the average peak instance count so that scaling activity stops fluctuating during normal traffic patterns each day
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because shrinking memory does not address the root cause that each container is artificially limited to one request at a time. Option B is wrong because always-allocated CPU changes billing model and idle behavior but does not raise per-instance throughput. Option C is correct because raising concurrency lets a single container serve multiple in-flight requests, which is exactly what Cloud Run is designed for when work is I/O bound. Option D is wrong because increasing min-instances raises cost without improving per-instance efficiency.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: Cloud CDN is enabled on an HTTPS load balancer fronting a backend bucket of static assets. The cache hit ratio reported in Cloud Monitoring is only 35 percent despite the content being immutable. What is the most likely cause?
options: The backend bucket is missing a uniform bucket-level access policy, which causes Cloud CDN to bypass cache on every request to validate the IAM permissions of the caller
Cloud CDN is configured in USE_ORIGIN_HEADERS mode and the origin is returning Cache-Control headers with no-store or short max-age values for the static assets
The load balancer is using a global external IP address rather than a regional internal address, which prevents Cloud CDN from caching content close to end users
Cloud CDN signed URLs are enabled on the backend, which by design disables caching of any object request that includes a signature parameter in the query string
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because IAM does not run on every CDN edge request and does not change cacheability. Option B is correct because in USE_ORIGIN_HEADERS mode the origin's Cache-Control headers determine caching, and weak headers from the bucket directly cause low hit ratios. Option C is wrong because Cloud CDN requires a global external HTTPS load balancer; regional addresses do not apply here. Option D is wrong because signed URLs are still cached by Cloud CDN; signing does not prevent caching.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A GKE cluster has cluster autoscaler enabled. After a temporary spike, nodes remain at peak size for over an hour even though pod count has dropped. Which setting most directly controls how quickly the cluster releases unused nodes after the spike?
options: Lower the value of the --scale-down-unneeded-time flag so that a node only needs to be unneeded for a short period before being considered safe to remove
Lower the value of the --scale-down-delay-after-add flag so that scale-down can occur sooner after the most recent scale-up event has been recorded by the autoscaler
Switch the cluster autoscaler profile from balanced to optimize-utilization so that the autoscaler more aggressively packs pods and removes underutilized nodes faster
Enable node auto-provisioning so that the autoscaler can both add and remove node pools dynamically based on the workload's CPU, memory, and GPU resource requests
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because scale-down-unneeded-time is the dwell period a node must be idle before removal, and lowering it directly speeds reclaim. Option B is wrong because scale-down-delay-after-add is a cooldown after scale-up; it can also help but is secondary to the unneeded-time timer. Option C is wrong because the optimize-utilization profile reshapes packing decisions, not the explicit dwell timer for unneeded nodes. Option D is wrong because node auto-provisioning manages node pool creation, not removal speed.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: An HTTPS Cloud Load Balancer serves a global single page application. Mobile users on lossy networks report long page load times despite TLS handshake metrics looking healthy at the edge. Which protocol change should yield the largest improvement?
options: Enable HTTP Strict Transport Security so that compliant browsers skip the initial plaintext redirect and connect directly over HTTPS to the load balancer frontend
Enable HTTP/3 QUIC on the load balancer frontend so that connection establishment uses UDP and is resilient to packet loss and head-of-line blocking on mobile networks
Enable TLS 1.3 and disable older cipher suites on the SSL policy so that handshake round trips are reduced and the negotiated ciphers are more efficient on mobile CPUs
Enable mutual TLS authentication on the load balancer frontend so that clients present certificates and skip session re-establishment overhead on subsequent requests
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because HSTS removes a redirect but does not affect packet loss or head-of-line blocking. Option B is correct because HTTP/3 over QUIC removes TCP head-of-line blocking and recovers faster on lossy mobile links, which directly addresses the symptom. Option C is wrong because TLS 1.3 is already common and saves at most one round trip; it does not solve packet loss issues. Option D is wrong because mTLS adds handshake cost; it is a security feature, not a latency optimization for mobile clients.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A BigQuery analytics workload uses on-demand pricing. Costs are predictable but a critical executive dashboard sometimes waits 30 seconds for slots during business hours. Which approach guarantees sub-second responsiveness for that dashboard?
options: Apply table-level clustering on every column referenced by the dashboard so that BigQuery can serve all panel queries from clustered data without waiting for slot availability
Rewrite the dashboard queries to use approximate aggregation functions such as APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT so that fewer slots are required to return acceptable results
Create a BI Engine reservation in the same region as the dataset and ensure the dashboard queries match BI Engine acceleration criteria for the reservation's memory capacity
Migrate the dataset to Bigtable with row keys aligned to the dashboard filters so that low-latency lookups replace expensive analytical scans of the BigQuery storage layer
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because clustering reduces bytes scanned but does not bypass slot scheduling for guaranteed sub-second response. Option B is wrong because approximate functions reduce work but still depend on available slots. Option C is correct because BI Engine accelerates supported queries in-memory and is the documented option for sub-second dashboards on BigQuery. Option D is wrong because Bigtable is for key-based access patterns, not ad hoc analytic dashboards.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: An application running on Compute Engine reads a 50 GB working dataset from a Persistent Disk on every cold boot. Boot times exceed 90 seconds. Which storage change most reduces cold boot latency?
options: Migrate the working dataset to a Local SSD attached to the instance template so that boot-time reads achieve high IOPS and low latency from on-host NVMe storage
Increase the size of the boot Persistent Disk to 2 TB so that the pd-balanced disk earns higher baseline IOPS and throughput proportional to its provisioned capacity
Switch the boot disk type from pd-balanced to pd-standard so that sequential read throughput is increased on the large working set during the boot read phase
Mount the working dataset as a read-only Cloud Storage FUSE filesystem so that bytes are streamed lazily from a regional bucket only when the application requests them
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because Local SSD delivers very high IOPS and low latency for large reads, dramatically reducing dataset read time on boot. Option B is wrong because larger PD does scale IOPS but typically cannot reach Local SSD performance for this size of read. Option C is wrong because pd-standard is slower than pd-balanced, not faster. Option D is wrong because GCSFuse adds latency and is not suitable for fast cold reads of a 50 GB working set.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: Cloud Profiler is enabled on a Go service running in GKE. After a new release the team suspects a memory leak. Which Cloud Profiler workflow most directly confirms or rules out a leak compared to the previous release?
options: Switch Cloud Profiler to capture wall-clock time profiles for 24 hours and look for stack frames that block on system calls more than they did in the prior release
Capture CPU profiles for both the old and new release and compare hot functions, focusing on functions that show increased self time after the new release was deployed
Enable the Cloud Monitoring container memory dashboard and alert on container_memory_working_set_bytes growth that exceeds a fixed percentage per hour over a baseline
Capture heap profiles before and after the new release, use the Cloud Profiler diff view, and look for allocation sites that show increased retained bytes in the new build
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because wall-clock profiles measure blocking, not memory allocation or retention. Option B is wrong because CPU profiles measure compute, not heap retention. Option C is wrong because raw memory metrics show growth but do not attribute it to allocation sites or code paths. Option D is correct because heap profile diffs in Cloud Profiler attribute retained bytes to specific allocation sites, which is the established workflow for confirming leaks.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A Cloud Run service is fronted by an external HTTPS load balancer. Latency p99 from clients is high while latency measured inside the container is low. The load balancer backend health checks pass. What is the most likely cause to investigate first?
options: The Cloud Run service has a minimum instance count of zero, which causes cold start latency to dominate the load balancer's measured tail latency during quiet traffic periods
Network egress is using the Standard network service tier rather than Premium tier, which routes traffic over the public internet for more of the path between client and Google
The load balancer is configured with HTTP/1.1 only on the frontend, which prevents browsers from multiplexing requests and increases tail latency on pages with many assets
The serverless NEG backend region is not the closest region to the majority of users, which adds cross-region network latency on every request even when warm capacity exists
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because the question says container latency is low, which excludes cold starts. Option B is correct because Standard tier routes egress over the public internet for more of the path, which is a common cause of high client-side p99 while in-region container latency stays low. Option C is wrong because HTTP/1.1 vs HTTP/2 affects multiplexing but does not produce the in-vs-out gap described. Option D is also plausible but Standard tier is the more direct match for client-vs-server latency divergence.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A team needs to run a nightly batch ML training job that can tolerate interruption. Cost is the primary concern. Performance must complete within an 8-hour window. Which Compute Engine option best balances cost and reliability for this workload?
options: Use a managed instance group of N2D on-demand instances with a commitment-based discount applied at the project level for predictable workloads running every single night
