Use a managed instance group of E2 standard instances and rely on sustained-use discounts to reduce cost without changing the configuration of the underlying workload at all
Use Spot VMs with an instance template that includes a shutdown handler so that checkpointing flushes state before preemption and the next instance resumes from the checkpoint
Use a single large N2 VM with a 3-year committed-use discount so that the per-hour rate is the lowest possible while keeping the workload on a single high-memory machine type
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because on-demand with general commitments is cheaper than pure on-demand but still costlier than Spot for interruptible batch. Option B is wrong because sustained use discounts apply automatically and modestly; they do not approach Spot pricing. Option C is correct because Spot VMs offer the deepest discount, the workload tolerates interruption, and shutdown handlers preserve progress on preemption. Option D is wrong because a 3-year commit for a nightly job wastes capacity for 16 hours each day.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A microservice deployed on GKE sees periodic 1-second latency spikes during traffic increases. Cloud Profiler shows GC pauses growing during these windows. The container has a memory request of 256 Mi and limit of 512 Mi. Which mitigation is most effective?
options: Disable VPA for the workload so that the recommended memory request is not changed by the controller while the team validates the new garbage collection tuning settings
Move the workload to a node pool of preemptible n2-highmem instances so that nodes have a higher memory-to-CPU ratio available for GC overhead during traffic increases
Configure the HPA to scale on memory utilization instead of CPU so that the controller adds replicas when the working memory of the pod approaches the configured limit
Increase the container memory request and limit and tune the language runtime heap parameters so that garbage collection can run with more headroom and shorter pause times
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because disabling VPA does not address the underlying GC pressure caused by a small heap. Option B is wrong because preemptible nodes introduce eviction risk and do not solve the per-pod heap sizing issue. Option C is wrong because memory-based HPA adds pods but does not relieve GC pressure inside any one pod. Option D is correct because raising the request and limit and tuning the runtime gives GC more headroom, which is the documented response to GC-bound latency spikes.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A Cloud SQL for MySQL workload runs heavy analytical reporting queries against the same instance that serves transactional writes. Reports cause CPU saturation and slow down OLTP. Which architectural change provides immediate relief without rewriting the application?
options: Provision one or more Cloud SQL read replicas and route the reporting traffic to the replica connection name so that OLTP traffic keeps the primary's CPU and IOPS budget
Enable Cloud SQL high availability and configure the failover replica to receive reporting workloads when the primary CPU exceeds an alert threshold during business hours
Migrate the workload to a larger Cloud SQL machine type with double the vCPUs and memory so that the primary has enough headroom to absorb both reporting and OLTP
Enable Cloud SQL query insights and use the recommendations to add indexes that cover the slow reporting queries, reducing per-query CPU cost on the primary instance
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because read replicas absorb read-heavy reporting and isolate the primary from CPU saturation, which is the standard Cloud SQL pattern. Option B is wrong because the failover replica is for HA and is not intended for routine read serving. Option C is wrong because vertical scaling helps temporarily but does not isolate workloads. Option D is wrong because better indexes help, but indexing alone usually does not eliminate CPU contention between OLTP and large analytics.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: An internal HTTP(S) load balancer fronts a backend service running on GKE. Throughput tests show that a single client connection plateaus at 100 RPS while many clients in parallel achieve thousands of RPS. Which protocol change should be evaluated?
options: Enable backend service-based session affinity by client IP so that one client's requests are pinned to one backend instance and avoid the cost of new TLS handshakes per request
Increase the load balancer's max RPS per endpoint setting so that the load balancer permits higher per-backend throughput before it begins to consider the backend overloaded
Enable HTTP/2 between client and load balancer so that a single connection can multiplex many concurrent streams instead of being limited by request serialization on HTTP/1.1
Switch backend protocol to HTTPS with TLS 1.3 so that handshake costs are minimized and the load balancer can establish connections to backends more quickly when needed
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because session affinity helps cache locality, not single-connection throughput. Option B is wrong because per-endpoint max RPS is a capacity threshold, not a protocol-level throughput limiter for one client. Option C is correct because HTTP/2 multiplexing on one connection removes the head-of-line serialization that caps a single client at low RPS on HTTP/1.1. Option D is wrong because TLS version changes do not multiplex requests over one connection.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A Cloud Run service serves a public API and traffic is highly variable. The team wants to keep cold starts below 1 percent of requests but also keep idle cost low. Which configuration best supports both goals?
options: Set the max instances ceiling high and disable startup CPU boost so that scaling can grow widely while individual instances start with normal CPU allocation to save spend
Set min instances to a small value such as 2 and enable startup CPU boost so that the small warm pool absorbs new traffic and instance startup completes faster under load
Set min instances equal to peak traffic capacity so that scaling never has to add new instances during business hours, which guarantees that no user ever experiences a cold start
Disable min instances entirely and rely on Cloud Run's default request queueing for very short bursts so that the platform absorbs cold starts inside the queue before scale-out
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because no min instances means cold starts dominate after idle periods. Option B is correct because a small warm pool plus startup CPU boost meets the cold start target while keeping spend low compared to provisioning for peak. Option C is wrong because provisioning for peak wastes money outside peak hours. Option D is wrong because Cloud Run's queue is short and does not reliably mask cold starts for variable traffic.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A BigQuery query that joins a 10 TB fact table with a 50 MB dimension table is slow because BigQuery is broadcasting the dimension to every slot. The slot count is healthy. Which technique most reliably improves the join's performance?
options: Cluster both tables on the join key and rewrite the query to use a JOIN hint that forces BigQuery to use a hash join with the small table broadcast to all the slots
Materialize the dimension table inside the query as a WITH clause and rely on the BigQuery optimizer to pick the most efficient join strategy from the inlined subquery
Migrate the dimension table to BigQuery BI Engine so that the in-memory copy of the dimension is used for the join and the broadcast over network is avoided entirely
Convert the join into a denormalized table built by a scheduled query so that the fact already includes the dimension columns and no runtime broadcast join is required
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because clustering the fact table on the join key reduces shuffle and combining that with a broadcast hash join is the documented best pattern for small-to-large joins. Option B is wrong because a WITH clause is just syntax sugar and does not improve join cost. Option C is wrong because BI Engine accelerates dashboard queries, not arbitrary join optimization on large fact tables. Option D is wrong because denormalization is a heavy refactor and is not the most direct optimization here.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A Cloud Spanner workload reports high CPU on read transactions despite a small data set. Cloud Spanner Query Insights flags many scans on a frequently filtered column. What is the most direct fix?
options: Run ANALYZE on the affected table so that the optimizer rebuilds the table's column statistics and chooses a more selective execution plan for the filtered queries
Create a secondary index on the frequently filtered column and include the commonly returned columns as STORING columns so that the queries become covered index lookups
Increase the number of nodes in the Spanner instance so that CPU is distributed across more compute and the existing query plan completes faster on each individual replica
Rewrite the read transactions to use stale reads with a maximum staleness of 10 seconds so that they bypass the leader and execute against any replica without consensus overhead
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because Spanner does not require manual ANALYZE for this kind of index improvement. Option B is correct because a secondary index with STORING columns turns scans into covered lookups, eliminating the CPU cost of scans. Option C is wrong because scaling masks the symptom and increases cost without fixing the bad scan pattern. Option D is wrong because stale reads can help for read-only workloads but do not reduce the CPU cost of a poorly indexed scan.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: Cloud CDN is enabled on a global external HTTPS load balancer. The team wants every static image under /assets/* cached for one week regardless of what the origin returns. Which configuration achieves this with the least operational overhead?
options: Set the Cloud CDN cache mode for the backend to USE_ORIGIN_HEADERS and reconfigure the origin to return Cache-Control max-age headers of 604800 for all responses under /assets/
Apply a Cloud Armor security policy to add a custom Cache-Control header to all responses under /assets/ before Cloud CDN sees them on the path back to the client browser
Enable Cloud CDN signed URLs for the /assets/* prefix and set a 7-day signature lifetime so that the load balancer treats every signed asset request as a cache-friendly response
Set the Cloud CDN cache mode for the backend to FORCE_CACHE_ALL and configure a default TTL of 604800 seconds on the backend service so that all matching responses are cached
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because USE_ORIGIN_HEADERS depends on the origin sending correct headers, which is the very situation the question wants to avoid. Option B is wrong because Cloud Armor is for security policy enforcement, not response header rewriting for caching. Option C is wrong because signed URLs control access, not caching. Option D is correct because FORCE_CACHE_ALL with a default TTL ignores origin headers and caches per the configured lifetime, which matches the requirement exactly.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A team running stateless workloads on GKE wants to take advantage of low-cost capacity for non-critical jobs while preserving production SLOs. Which deployment pattern best uses GKE features to achieve this safely?
options: Deploy all workloads to a single default node pool of Spot VMs so that the cluster benefits from Spot pricing on every workload and absorbs preemption with replica counts
Run all workloads on a node pool of on-demand instances but apply a PodDisruptionBudget that blocks voluntary evictions so that production pods never lose more than one replica
Create a Spot-only node pool with a taint and run only non-critical batch workloads that tolerate the taint while production runs on a separate on-demand node pool with no taint
Enable node auto-provisioning on the cluster and allow it to mix Spot and on-demand machine types automatically so that the autoscaler decides where each pod lands at scheduling time
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because putting production on Spot risks SLO violation on preemption. Option B is wrong because a PDB does not address cost optimization. Option C is correct because taints and tolerations isolate Spot capacity to opt-in workloads, which is the documented GKE pattern for mixing Spot with on-demand safely. Option D is wrong because node auto-provisioning helps capacity decisions but should not silently place production pods on Spot.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: Cloud Run jobs run a nightly ETL that processes data in parallel. The team wants to maximize throughput per dollar by tuning parallelism. Which configuration knob most directly controls how many tasks run at the same time?
options: Set the parallelism setting on the Cloud Run job to the number of parallel tasks that should run concurrently, bounded above by the configured task count for the job
Set the CPU allocation for the Cloud Run job to always allocated so that tasks start more quickly and run in parallel without being throttled by request-based CPU billing
Set the maximum number of retries on each task to a high value so that the job tolerates transient failures and uses the additional retry capacity to process more tasks at once
Set the task timeout to a long value so that each parallel task has time to finish without being killed by the platform, which allows the job to scale out to more concurrent tasks
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because the parallelism field on a Cloud Run job directly controls how many tasks execute concurrently, up to the configured task count. Option B is wrong because CPU allocation affects each task's execution model, not the count of concurrent tasks. Option C is wrong because retries handle failure, not concurrency. Option D is wrong because longer timeouts let tasks finish but do not raise parallelism.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: An application uses Cloud Spanner read-write transactions for read traffic that does not modify data. p50 read latency is acceptable but p99 is high under load. Which change reduces tail latency without sacrificing data consistency for those reads?
options: Move all read traffic to a separate Spanner instance configured with multi-region replication so that the read traffic does not compete with write traffic for leader CPU at peak
Add the FORCE_INDEX query hint to every read query so that the Spanner optimizer is required to use a specific secondary index path for the read instead of scanning the table
Convert the read traffic to use Spanner single-use read-only transactions with strong reads so that they bypass the read-write transaction coordinator and consume fewer locks
Configure the client library to use stale reads with a maximum staleness of 100 milliseconds so that reads can be served by any replica without paying for paxos coordination cost
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because a separate instance is a heavyweight architectural change and the question asks about a configuration change. Option B is wrong because index hints help specific queries but do not address transaction overhead. Option C is correct because Spanner read-only transactions are lock-free and lighter than read-write transactions while still offering strong consistency. Option D is wrong because stale reads sacrifice strong consistency, which the question forbids.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A Memorystore for Redis Standard tier instance is reaching its connection limit because of a fanout from hundreds of pods. The team cannot reduce pod count. What configuration change provides the most headroom on connections?
options: Increase the maxmemory of the Redis instance to a larger tier so that more memory is available, which indirectly raises the connection limit because the limit scales with capacity
Switch the Memorystore instance from Standard tier to a Cluster tier so that connections are spread across multiple primary shards instead of one single primary endpoint
Switch all client libraries to use Redis Pub/Sub instead of normal key-value operations so that fewer persistent TCP connections to the Memorystore primary are required at idle
Lower the TCP keepalive on the clients and shorten the idle timeout on Memorystore so that idle connections are reaped more aggressively, freeing room for new client connections
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because increasing instance memory tiers raises connection limits modestly but a single primary still becomes a bottleneck. Option B is correct because Memorystore Cluster mode shards data and connections across multiple primaries, which is the supported way to scale connection counts beyond a single primary's ceiling. Option C is wrong because Pub/Sub still uses connections. Option D is wrong because reaping idle connections only helps when the workload has many idle clients, not many active ones.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: An external HTTPS load balancer fronts a backend service running on a regional MIG. CPU utilization is uneven across instances. Which load balancer setting most directly addresses uneven distribution of requests across the backend?
options: Switch the load balancing scheme from EXTERNAL to EXTERNAL_MANAGED so that the load balancer uses the newer data plane that distributes traffic more aggressively across backends
Increase the autoscaler target CPU utilization on the MIG to 0.8 so that the load balancer has more headroom on each instance before being required to spread to other instances
Configure the backend service balancing mode to UTILIZATION with a target backend utilization that triggers redistribution before any one instance saturates compared to its peers
Configure the backend service balancing mode to RATE with an explicit max requests per second per instance so that the load balancer caps each instance and spreads load to others
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because the scheme controls capabilities, not distribution fairness. Option B is wrong because raising the autoscaler target increases CPU saturation rather than spreading load. Option C is partially correct because UTILIZATION mode does help, but rate-based balancing is more deterministic for evening distribution. Option D is correct because RATE balancing distributes per RPS, which directly addresses uneven CPU caused by uneven request count when requests are similar in cost.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A team uses Cloud Profiler on a Java service. They want to track the impact of a recent code change on CPU. Which Cloud Profiler workflow most directly attributes increased CPU to specific call paths after the deploy?
options: Capture CPU profiles before and after the deploy and use the Cloud Profiler comparison view to identify the call stacks whose self CPU time increased between the two builds
Enable Java Flight Recorder on every pod and download the JFR files to a central bucket so that engineers can analyze them offline with desktop tooling on their workstations
Use Cloud Monitoring CPU utilization charts grouped by Kubernetes deployment label so that the team can confirm whether the new release uses more CPU than the previous release
Use Cloud Trace and look at the latency waterfall for one slow request so that the engineers can see which downstream call introduced new latency since the most recent deploy
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because the Cloud Profiler comparison view is purpose-built to diff CPU profiles between builds and is the documented workflow for change attribution. Option B is wrong because JFR is independent of Cloud Profiler and adds operational overhead. Option C is wrong because aggregate CPU metrics confirm the symptom but do not attribute to call stacks. Option D is wrong because Cloud Trace attributes latency in distributed calls but not CPU within a process.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A Compute Engine VM serves a high-throughput gRPC backend behind an internal load balancer. The team observes that egress throughput plateaus around 2 Gbps even though the machine type lists higher capability. Which factor is most likely to be the cap?
options: The instance has a public external IP that routes traffic through Cloud NAT, which imposes per-flow bandwidth limits on outbound connections leaving the project's VPC network
The machine type's per-VM egress cap and the per-vCPU egress allocation together cap throughput for the configured shape, requiring a larger shape to raise the per-VM cap
The boot disk type is pd-standard rather than pd-balanced, which throttles the network stack because Persistent Disk I/O shares the same NIC bandwidth budget as egress traffic
The internal load balancer is configured with a session affinity of CLIENT_IP, which forces all flows from one client onto one backend NIC and saturates that NIC at 2 Gbps
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because Cloud NAT applies to egress to the internet, not to an internal load balancer path. Option B is correct because Compute Engine documents per-VM and per-vCPU egress caps, and moving to a larger shape lifts the ceiling. Option C is wrong because PD type does not throttle the NIC bandwidth budget directly. Option D is wrong because session affinity does not cap NIC bandwidth at 2 Gbps; the machine type cap is the documented control.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: An HPA on GKE is configured with both CPU and a custom Pub/Sub queue depth metric via the Stackdriver adapter. Pod count oscillates rapidly during traffic spikes. Which HPA setting most directly stabilizes the scaling behavior?
options: Reduce the target average value for the Pub/Sub queue depth so that the HPA scales up earlier on smaller queue depths and the queue never grows during traffic spikes at all
Remove the CPU metric from the HPA so that only Pub/Sub queue depth drives scaling decisions and the controller no longer has to reconcile two signals during traffic spikes
Configure the HPA scale-down stabilization window to a longer value so that the controller waits before reducing replica count after a temporary drop in metric values
Increase the HPA sync period in the kube-controller-manager so that the controller polls metrics less often and produces fewer reconciliation decisions during the same window
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because lowering the target makes scaling more aggressive, which tends to make oscillation worse. Option B is wrong because removing a useful signal does not stabilize behavior. Option C is correct because the scale-down stabilization window dampens oscillation by requiring the controller to wait before scaling down, which is the documented fix for thrashing. Option D is wrong because the controller-manager sync period is cluster-level and is not the right knob.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL Insights flags that a query plan does a sequential scan over a 10 GB table when filtering by a single column. The column has uniform distribution and high cardinality. What is the recommended first action?
options: Increase the work_mem flag on the Cloud SQL instance so that PostgreSQL can perform sorts and hash operations on the sequential scan output without spilling to disk during execution
Enable parallel query by raising max_parallel_workers_per_gather so that the sequential scan is divided across worker processes and finishes in less wall-clock time at higher CPU cost
Increase the Cloud SQL instance machine type by one tier so that the additional CPU and memory available on the database can make the sequential scan complete in less wall-clock time
Create a B-tree index on the filtered column so that the planner can switch from a sequential scan to an index scan or index-only scan for the queries that filter by that column
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because work_mem helps memory-bound operations but does not change the access path. Option B is wrong because parallel scan is still a scan and consumes more resources. Option C is wrong because scaling the instance hides the symptom and increases cost. Option D is correct because a B-tree index on a high-cardinality filtered column directly enables an index scan, which is the standard PostgreSQL optimization recommended by Cloud SQL Insights.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A Cloud Run service uses Cloud SQL via the Cloud SQL Auth Proxy sidecar. Latency on database calls is high during scale-out. Which combination of changes most directly reduces per-request database latency under load?
options: Increase the maximum instances setting on Cloud Run so that more proxy sidecars are available during scale-out, which improves connection reuse on busy database operations under load
Enable connection pooling at the application layer and right-size the per-instance pool so that each Cloud Run container reuses connections instead of opening one per HTTP request
Switch from the Cloud SQL Auth Proxy to a direct private IP connection from Cloud Run so that the proxy hop is removed entirely from the latency budget of every database call
Increase the Cloud SQL machine type by two tiers so that the underlying database can handle additional connections during Cloud Run scale-out without contention or queueing on locks
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because more proxies do not reuse connections per request; they just add more clients. Option B is correct because pooling reuses connections and is the most direct fix for per-request connection setup latency, which is the dominant cost in this pattern. Option C is wrong because private IP can help latency slightly but does not address connection setup overhead. Option D is wrong because scaling the database does not change the connection cost per request.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A team wants to estimate the cost of a BigQuery query before running it on a 100 TB partitioned table. Which approach gives the most accurate bytes-scanned estimate without paying for execution?
options: Run the query as a dry run using the BigQuery client library or bq command-line tool with the --dry_run flag so that the API returns the bytes that would be processed
Run the query against a smaller copy of the table created with TABLESAMPLE SYSTEM 1 PERCENT so that the cost extrapolation is based on a representative sample of the data
Run the query with maximum bytes billed set to a small value so that the query fails after a partial scan and returns a useful estimate of how many bytes it would have processed
Run the query against the table's INFORMATION_SCHEMA.PARTITIONS view so that you can compute bytes scanned from partition metadata and the planner's expected pruning behavior
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because dry-run requests against the BigQuery API return totalBytesProcessed without execution, which is the documented way to estimate cost. Option B is wrong because sampling executes a query and is approximate. Option C is wrong because maxBytesBilled stops a query early and is intended for cost guardrails, not estimation. Option D is wrong because INFORMATION_SCHEMA gives metadata but does not predict planner behavior on a specific query.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A GKE Autopilot cluster runs latency-sensitive microservices. The team wants to reduce p99 latency caused by pod evictions during node maintenance windows triggered by the cluster's release channel updates. Which GKE feature is most appropriate?
options: Add a node taint and toleration to keep the latency-sensitive pods on a dedicated node pool of stable-version nodes that are upgraded manually during planned maintenance windows
Configure pod priority and preemption so that the latency-sensitive pods have the highest priority class and are evicted last when node pressure forces voluntary pod removal events
Configure pod disruption budgets that allow at most one pod to be unavailable at a time so that voluntary disruptions during upgrades do not violate the latency budget for the service
Enable surge upgrades on the cluster with a high maxSurge value so that node upgrades proceed quickly and the total time spent in a degraded state during upgrades is minimized
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because Autopilot does not allow user-controlled node pools in the traditional sense and dedicated stable pools are not the right primitive here. Option B is wrong because priority controls scheduling order, not eviction during voluntary upgrades. Option C is correct because PDBs limit voluntary disruption during upgrades and are the documented way to protect latency budgets during maintenance. Option D is wrong because faster upgrades increase concurrent disruption rather than protecting latency.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: Cloud CDN is enabled but the team needs to invalidate a single object after an emergency content update. Which approach is the most correct for production use?
options: Disable Cloud CDN entirely on the backend service and re-enable it after the update propagates so that all stale entries are flushed and the new content is served immediately to users
Change the URL of the updated object by adding a version query string and update references in HTML so that future requests use the new URL and old cache entries become irrelevant
Use the gcloud compute url-maps invalidate-cdn-cache command targeting the specific object path so that Cloud CDN evicts the matching entries from its edge cache servers
Reduce the default TTL for the backend service to 60 seconds so that the cached object expires quickly and the next request after the TTL boundary fetches the new version from origin
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because disabling CDN is destructive and breaks unrelated objects. Option B is wrong because URL versioning is a good practice generally but does not invalidate the existing URL. Option C is correct in spirit but the specific gcloud command is for url-maps; the correct invocation is the Cloud CDN invalidation API on the URL map. Option D is correct because the invalidation API with a path target removes the specific entry from the edge cache, which is the documented way to invalidate Cloud CDN cache.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A Cloud Run service has CPU set to always allocated and a minimum of 1 instance. During traffic spikes p99 latency rises sharply while p50 stays low. Cloud Trace shows time spent in container before request handling. What is the most likely cause?
options: The Cloud Run service has CPU throttling enabled by accident, which prevents background work from running between requests and causes warm-up code to execute on request paths
The container image is large and the container layer pull on cold starts dominates new instance startup, which inflates p99 latency for the first requests routed to each new instance
The application is single-threaded and concurrency is set to 80, which forces all incoming requests to queue inside the container's HTTP server before the handler can process them
The container is hitting its memory limit and being recycled, so a fresh instance must start during the spike and the p99 represents the new instance's startup and warmup time
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because always-allocated CPU explicitly disables throttling between requests. Option B is correct because large image pulls inflate startup time on new instances created during spikes, and Cloud Trace would show that time as before-handler latency. Option C is wrong because high concurrency on a single-threaded server affects all percentiles, not just p99 during spikes. Option D is plausible but less likely without OOM signals; image pull on scale-out is the more common p99 culprit.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A BigQuery workload uses on-demand pricing and shows intermittent slot starvation during quarter-end. The team wants predictable performance for one critical project without paying for capacity year-round. Which option fits?
options: Purchase a flex slot reservation for the duration of the quarter-end window and assign the critical project to the reservation so that it has guaranteed slots only when needed
Move the critical project's queries to BigQuery scheduled queries that run at off-peak hours so that they no longer compete with interactive queries for on-demand slot availability
Purchase a 3-year baseline reservation of 500 slots and assign the critical project to it so that the lower per-slot price compensates for the rest of the year's underuse
Enable BigQuery Editions Autoscaler with a baseline of zero and a max of 1000 slots so that the critical project gets capacity on demand at the lowest possible per-slot price
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because flex slot reservations are short-duration and can be purchased for only the quarter-end window, matching the requirement to avoid year-round cost. Option B is wrong because moving to off-peak helps but does not guarantee capacity. Option C is wrong because 3-year commits are the opposite of the requirement. Option D is wrong because autoscaler also incurs ongoing per-slot cost and is not a short-term flex option.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A team writes a service on Cloud Run that calls an external partner API with high latency. They want to maximize throughput per container while minimizing cost. Which Cloud Run configuration is most appropriate?
options: Set CPU allocation to always allocated and concurrency to 1 so that each container gives the slow external call full CPU and avoids contention with other in-flight requests at scale
Set CPU allocation to request-based and concurrency to 1 so that each container is charged only while actively processing the request and not while waiting on the external partner API
Set CPU allocation to request-based and concurrency to a high value so that the platform can pack many concurrent slow requests onto one container without billing for idle CPU time
Set CPU allocation to always allocated and concurrency to a high value so that one container can hold many in-flight slow requests in parallel while waiting on the external API
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because concurrency 1 wastes capacity when the work is I/O bound. Option B is wrong for the same reason and also drops CPU when waiting, which can stall callbacks. Option C is correct because request-based CPU plus high concurrency is the most cost-effective configuration for I/O bound work with long external waits. Option D is plausible but pays for always-allocated CPU that the workload does not need.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A GKE workload uses VPA in Auto mode. Operators report that pods are restarted unexpectedly during peak hours. Which change preserves VPA's recommendation benefit while reducing disruption?
options: Switch VPA to Recreate mode so that the controller restarts pods only when the cluster autoscaler also schedules changes to the node pool during the same reconciliation window
Switch VPA to Off mode so that the controller still computes recommendations but never applies them, and the team uses the recommendations to set manual resource requests in deployments
Switch VPA to Initial mode so that the controller sets resource requests when a pod is first created and never modifies them again on a running pod, even when recommendations change
Switch VPA to Auto with a PodDisruptionBudget that allows zero unavailable pods so that the controller waits until traffic drops before applying any updated resource recommendations
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because Recreate mode is the same as Auto in terms of restart behavior on most GKE versions and does not reduce disruption. Option B is correct in spirit; however, Initial mode is the better answer because it applies recommendations at pod creation but never restarts running pods. Option C is correct: Initial mode is exactly designed for this tradeoff and is the documented way to avoid running-pod restarts. Option D is wrong because a zero-unavailable PDB blocks updates entirely.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: An application performance issue is reported. Cloud Profiler shows hot CPU time in a function that calls a downstream Cloud Spanner read. Cloud Trace shows the Spanner span dominates the request. What is the most likely root cause focus?
options: The application is CPU bound serializing the response payload, and the Spanner span is large only because the request handler is blocked on its own goroutines waiting for CPU resources
The Spanner query is doing a full table scan due to a missing secondary index, and the CPU hot spot is the result handler iterating over a much larger row set than expected
The Spanner client library is mis-configured with a small session pool, which forces request handlers to spin waiting for a session and consumes CPU on connection-pool synchronization
The application is reading from a stale Spanner replica with bounded staleness, and the CPU hot spot is in the retry logic that re-issues the read until a consistent value is observed
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because Profiler attributes CPU to the function calling Spanner, which points to result handling, not response serialization. Option B is correct because a missing index causes a Spanner scan and the function consuming results burns CPU iterating over more rows than expected, which matches both the Profiler and Trace signals. Option C is wrong because session pool contention would show as waiting time, not as CPU in the calling function. Option D is wrong because bounded staleness does not trigger automatic retries.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A team uses BigQuery clustering on event_date and user_id. A query filters only on user_id and scans almost the entire table. What is the most likely reason clustering is not helping?
options: Clustering only narrows scans efficiently when filters use the leading clustering column, and event_date is leading so a filter on user_id alone cannot use the clustering index effectively
Clustering is only effective on tables with at least 1 TB of data, and the table in question is smaller than that threshold, so BigQuery falls back to a full scan regardless of cluster columns
Clustering requires the table to also be partitioned on the same columns that are referenced in the WHERE clause, otherwise the clustering metadata is not used by the query planner at all
Clustering only applies to queries that also include the cluster columns in the SELECT list, which means a filter on user_id alone cannot benefit from the cluster's data layout on disk blocks
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because BigQuery clustering is order-sensitive; only filters on a prefix of the clustering columns prune effectively, which is documented behavior. Option B is wrong because clustering helps on tables well below 1 TB once they exceed about 1 GB. Option C is wrong because clustering and partitioning are independent features. Option D is wrong because clustering benefits filter pruning regardless of SELECT list contents.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A team operates a regional Cloud SQL for MySQL instance with a high read-to-write ratio. During traffic spikes, replication lag on existing read replicas grows above 30 seconds. What is the recommended mitigation?
options: Increase the binary log retention period on the primary so that read replicas can catch up over a longer window without losing transactions that are needed during the spike for replay
Move the read traffic back to the primary during spikes so that lag is irrelevant, and route only background analytical traffic to the replicas while the spike subsides naturally over time
Provision additional read replicas or upgrade existing replicas to a larger machine type so that each replica has enough CPU and IOPS to keep up with the primary's write throughput
Enable parallel replication on the read replicas with a high number of worker threads and ensure the primary's binlog format is set to ROW so that worker threads can apply changes safely
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because binlog retention does not change apply speed on the replica. Option B is wrong because shifting reads to the primary defeats the purpose of replicas and worsens primary load. Option C is correct because lag is fundamentally a replica throughput problem; more or larger replicas address it, which is the recommended Cloud SQL approach. Option D is plausible but parallel replication tuning is an advanced step usually attempted after vertical or horizontal scaling has been considered.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A Cloud Run service is hosted in us-central1 but most users are in Europe. p99 latency from European users is high while in-region latency stays low. Which architectural change reduces user-perceived latency the most?
options: Front Cloud Run with Cloud CDN and an external HTTPS load balancer so that GET requests are cached at edge nodes near the European users instead of always reaching us-central1 backends
Switch the Cloud Run service to multi-region by deploying identical services in multiple regions and putting them behind a global external HTTPS load balancer with serverless NEGs
Increase the Cloud Run service's CPU and memory configuration so that each request completes faster on the server side, which reduces the absolute end-to-end latency for European users
Enable HTTP/2 on the external HTTPS load balancer in front of Cloud Run so that European users benefit from connection multiplexing on slow international network paths to us-central1
questions.explanation: Option A is correct for cacheable GETs, but the question implies general service traffic where caching does not help most requests. Option B is correct because multi-region deployment with a global load balancer routes European users to a European region, directly removing the cross-continental hop on every request. Option C is wrong because server-side work is not the dominant cost. Option D is wrong because HTTP/2 helps multiplexing but does not remove the cross-continent round trip.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A team is using Cloud SQL Insights to investigate slow queries. They want to drill into one specific query that has high cumulative latency to see the execution plan and wait events. Which Insights feature is the right tool?
options: Open the query in the Insights query details view, examine the query plan visualization, and review the wait event breakdown to identify locks, I/O, or CPU contention as the bottleneck
Export Cloud SQL slow query logs to BigQuery and join them with INFORMATION_SCHEMA tables from Cloud SQL so that the team can build a custom query plan analysis dashboard offline
Run EXPLAIN ANALYZE manually inside a psql session against the primary instance and use pg_stat_statements to gather wait event statistics for the specific query during business hours
Enable Cloud Trace context propagation in the application so that each database call generates a span and the trace waterfall displays the query plan information for slow queries
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because Cloud SQL Insights provides a per-query details view with plan visualization and wait events, which is the supported in-product workflow. Option B is wrong because BigQuery export is for long-term retention and custom analysis, not the primary plan inspection workflow. Option C is wrong because manual EXPLAIN ANALYZE works but is not the Insights-specific feature requested. Option D is wrong because Cloud Trace does not show database query plans.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A team running Cloud Run wants to reduce cost without giving up cold start protection during business hours only. Which Cloud Run capability addresses this directly?
options: Configure a recurring Cloud Scheduler job to send a synthetic request to the service every minute during business hours so that at least one warm instance is always present to serve real traffic

Configure the Cloud Run service with a scheduled minimum instances policy via Cloud Scheduler and the gcloud run services update command so that min-instances is set to a value during business hours and zero outside those hours
Configure the Cloud Run service with always-allocated CPU and a single minimum instance, then accept the cost outside of business hours because the price difference is too small to optimize for
Configure the Cloud Run service to use a Cloud Tasks queue with a fixed dispatch rate during business hours so that the service is constantly kept warm by a controlled stream of synthetic tasks
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because synthetic pings are fragile and not the recommended pattern. Option B is correct because Cloud Run supports scheduled changes to min-instances using gcloud automation, which is the documented way to limit cold start protection to business hours only. Option C is wrong because it does not optimize cost as requested. Option D is wrong because Cloud Tasks-based warmers are still a synthetic-traffic workaround rather than a direct min-instances schedule.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A Cloud Spanner database serves a queue-like table where each row's primary key is auto-incremented by the application. Write QPS will not scale beyond a few thousand. Which redesign best removes the write hotspot?
options: Switch the primary key to a UUIDv4 represented as a STRING so that all keys are random and writes are spread across all splits naturally without changing the application's queue semantics
Add a secondary index on a hashed copy of the primary key so that writes go to the index in a distributed manner while reads continue to scan the table by the original primary key ordering
Pre-warm the table by inserting empty rows across the full key range before peak hours so that Spanner splits the table in advance and the existing primary key can be written into all splits
Switch the primary key to a hash of the original key as the first column followed by the original key as the second column, then implement queue ordering using a secondary index on the original key
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because UUIDv4 spreads writes but breaks queue ordering and is not the documented Spanner pattern. Option B is wrong because indexes inherit hotspots from their own first column. Option C is wrong because pre-splits do not change the actual write key distribution. Option D is correct because the documented Spanner pattern is a hashed key prefix to spread writes plus a secondary index for ordering, preserving queue semantics without a write hotspot.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A team uses Cloud Profiler on a production Python service. They want to ensure profiling overhead remains low. Which Cloud Profiler configuration aligns with Google's recommendations?
options: Run the Cloud Profiler agent in always-on mode for all services in the fleet so that every pod continuously emits CPU and heap profiles to provide complete visibility for any future investigations
Run the Cloud Profiler agent only on a fraction of replicas behind a feature flag so that no more than a small percentage of running pods at any time are paying the profiler's overhead cost
Enable the Cloud Profiler agent on every replica because the agent is designed to use a small percentage of CPU and is intended to run continuously in production with low overhead
Disable the Cloud Profiler heap profiler entirely and rely on CPU profiles only so that the agent's per-pod memory cost stays low across the whole fleet of production replicas
questions.explanation: Option A is misleading because the recommendation is not to manually enable always-on but to enable the agent normally. Option B is wrong because subsampling is not required since the agent is statistically sampled internally. Option C is correct because the Cloud Profiler agent is designed for low overhead and Google recommends enabling it on every replica in production. Option D is wrong because heap profiles are needed for memory issues and the agent's overhead is low.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A BigQuery user runs the same query repeatedly during a debugging session. The query references partitioned tables and is unchanged between executions. Which BigQuery feature reduces cost and latency for repeat executions without code changes?
options: BigQuery materialized views on the underlying base tables so that the second execution reads from the precomputed view rather than scanning the full set of partitions on every run
BigQuery query results cache, which returns identical results for repeated queries from the cache for up to 24 hours when neither the query text nor any referenced tables have changed
BigQuery BI Engine accelerated tables, which keep the underlying data in memory and serve repeated queries from the in-memory copy without requiring slot capacity at execution time
BigQuery scheduled queries that materialize the result into a destination table, which the debugger then queries instead of re-executing the original query against the source partitions
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because materialized views are useful but require explicit creation and refresh management. Option B is correct because BigQuery's results cache automatically serves identical repeat queries from cache at no cost for up to 24 hours, which is exactly the repeated-execution scenario. Option C is wrong because BI Engine requires a reservation and is for dashboards. Option D is wrong because scheduled queries are explicit materialization, not transparent caching.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: An external HTTPS load balancer serves a regional backend service. The team wants to keep latency low for global users while still using a single regional backend. Which load balancer setting most directly improves global latency?
options: Confirm the load balancer is configured with the Premium network tier so that user traffic enters Google's network at the edge nearest the user and traverses the backbone to the backend region
Switch the backend service balancing mode from UTILIZATION to RATE so that the load balancer evenly distributes the global request load across the available backends in the regional MIG
Enable Cloud Armor with adaptive protection on the load balancer so that the global frontend can mitigate latency-inducing attack patterns that affect users far from the regional backend
Add a second backend service in a different region as a failover backend so that the load balancer routes global users to whichever region is currently considered healthier by health checks
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because Premium tier ingress at Google's edge is the documented latency optimization that benefits global users reaching a single regional backend. Option B is wrong because RATE vs UTILIZATION affects fairness within a backend, not global path length. Option C is wrong because Cloud Armor is a security control, not a latency optimization. Option D is wrong because failover is for availability, not steady-state global latency.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A team operates a Memorystore for Redis instance used as a cache aside in front of Cloud SQL. They observe that cache miss bursts overwhelm Cloud SQL during failover events. Which design change protects the database during miss bursts?
options: Increase the Memorystore instance memory size so that the cache holds a larger working set and the number of misses during failover is reduced by serving more requests from cache hits
Switch the cache pattern from cache aside to write-through caching so that every write goes through the cache to the database, which keeps the cache warm and avoids miss bursts during failover

Implement request coalescing or single-flight in the application so that concurrent requests for the same missing key result in only one database read and the rest wait for that read to populate the cache
Disable the Redis maxmemory policy and configure persistence with RDB snapshots so that the cache contents survive an instance restart and miss bursts cannot occur after a failover event
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because a larger cache helps but does not address the thundering herd during a miss burst. Option B is wrong because write-through caches every write through the cache but does not solve coordinated misses. Option C is correct because single-flight or request coalescing is the documented protection for cache miss stampedes; it converts N concurrent misses into one database read. Option D is wrong because persistence does not exist in this form on Memorystore and is not the right primitive.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A team runs batch jobs on GKE. They want to use Spot VMs to reduce cost while keeping their batch SLOs within reach. Which GKE feature lets the cluster fall back to on-demand capacity automatically when Spot is unavailable?
options: Configure a single node pool that mixes Spot and on-demand machine types via the GKE Autopilot fallback feature, which automatically replaces unavailable Spot capacity with on-demand instances
Configure two node pools, one Spot and one on-demand, and use a workload-level nodeAffinity preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution rule that prefers Spot but tolerates on-demand fallback
Configure cluster autoscaler with a Spot-only node pool and a separate on-demand node pool, then rely on cluster autoscaler's automatic Spot-to-on-demand fallback at the cluster level
Configure GKE node auto-provisioning with both Spot and on-demand machine type families enabled, then rely on auto-provisioning's automatic fallback between families when Spot is unavailable
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because Autopilot does not currently offer a single-pool Spot-with-on-demand fallback in this form. Option B is correct because a preferredDuringScheduling node affinity that prefers Spot but does not require it is the documented pattern for soft preference with on-demand fallback. Option C is wrong because cluster autoscaler does not have built-in cross-pool Spot fallback. Option D is wrong because node auto-provisioning does not promise this fallback semantic.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A team uses Cloud Run with concurrency 80 and request-based CPU. A new feature uses heavy CPU for image processing. After deploy, p95 latency increases and CPU is pegged at 100 percent per instance. What change is the most appropriate first step?
options: Increase the maximum instances setting on the Cloud Run service so that more containers can absorb the additional CPU demand caused by the new image processing feature under load
Switch the Cloud Run service to always-allocated CPU so that the service can run heavier CPU work between requests, which reduces the per-request CPU pressure on each container instance
Lower the per-instance concurrency to a value such as 4 so that fewer requests share a single instance's CPU, then let the platform scale out to handle higher overall throughput
Increase the per-container memory limit to a larger value so that the image processing workload has more memory headroom and the latency caused by garbage collection is reduced under load
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because more instances at concurrency 80 still leaves each instance saturated on CPU. Option B is wrong because the workload's bottleneck is during requests, not idle CPU. Option C is correct because lowering concurrency reduces CPU contention per instance and lets the platform scale out, which is the documented response to CPU-bound work on Cloud Run. Option D is wrong because memory headroom does not relieve CPU saturation.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A Cloud SQL instance receives sudden traffic from a new analytics product. Connection counts spike and the application sees occasional timeouts. The team wants to keep the existing application unchanged. Which Google-managed feature offers the most direct relief?
options: Enable PostgreSQL connection pooling support in Cloud SQL via the managed Cloud SQL connection pooling preview, which provides PgBouncer-like multiplexing without operating a sidecar manually
Increase the max_connections database flag to a value that is the sum of all expected client connection counts so that the database is provisioned to accept every concurrent client connection
Add a Cloud SQL read replica and route all new analytics traffic to the replica's connection name so that the primary's connection pool is not affected by the new analytics workload at all
Enable Cloud SQL high availability with a failover replica so that the analytics workload is automatically redirected during connection spikes to the standby and the primary's pool is preserved
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because Cloud SQL managed connection pooling multiplexes client connections without requiring application changes or running a sidecar, which directly relieves connection spikes. Option B is wrong because raising max_connections risks memory exhaustion and does not multiplex. Option C is partially valid but requires the application to use a new connection string, which the question disallows. Option D is wrong because failover replicas do not accept active workloads.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A GKE HPA scales a service on a custom metric exposed via the Google Cloud Managed Service for Prometheus adapter. Pod counts react slowly to load. Which adapter or metric-side change most improves responsiveness?
options: Switch the custom metric from a counter to a gauge so that the HPA evaluates the instantaneous value of the metric instead of computing a rate over the most recent collection window
Increase the HPA target utilization for the custom metric so that the controller scales sooner and more aggressively when the metric exceeds the threshold during traffic spikes
Reduce the HPA scale-up stabilization window to zero so that the controller can react to every poll of the custom metric without waiting for stabilization windows during traffic spikes
Increase the metric collection frequency in the Managed Service for Prometheus configuration and ensure the PromQL query used by the adapter computes over a short averaging window
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because counters vs gauges is a metric type detail that does not by itself fix HPA reaction time. Option B is wrong because lowering the target makes scaling fire sooner but does not address freshness. Option C is wrong because zero stabilization causes thrashing, not stability. Option D is correct because the dominant latency in custom-metric HPA is metric collection and PromQL window length; tightening both produces fresher signals.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A team uses Cloud Run with min-instances of 1 to avoid cold starts. They notice that the minimum instance receives idle CPU charges. Which Cloud Run billing model corresponds to this behavior?
options: Cloud Run is using request-based billing, which only charges for CPU during active request handling and would not show idle CPU charges on the minimum instance during quiet periods at all
Cloud Run is using the per-request invocation billing model, which charges per request plus a small per-second fee, but min-instances are explicitly excluded from idle charges by default
Cloud Run is using always-allocated CPU billing, which charges for CPU time on each instance whether it is processing requests or idle, including on min-instances that exist during quiet periods
Cloud Run is using committed-use discount billing, which prepays for capacity at a discount and therefore does not charge separately for idle CPU time on a per-minute basis during quiet periods
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because request-based billing does not produce idle charges; the question observed idle charges. Option B is wrong because there is no separate invocation model that excludes min-instances from idle charges. Option C is correct because always-allocated CPU charges for the whole instance lifetime including idle time, which matches the observed behavior on min-instances. Option D is wrong because committed-use discounts do not change the per-second billing model itself.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A team running Cloud Spanner wants to ensure that a critical reporting query uses a specific secondary index. The optimizer occasionally picks a different plan. Which approach is the most appropriate to enforce the desired plan?
options: Drop all other secondary indexes that the optimizer might consider for the reporting query so that only the desired index remains available for the planner to choose during query execution
Add a FORCE_INDEX query hint to the reporting query so that the Spanner optimizer is instructed to use the named secondary index, which provides deterministic plan selection at query time
Configure the Spanner optimizer statistics package to a pinned version using the OPTIMIZER_STATISTICS_PACKAGE hint so that plan stability is preserved over time across statistics refreshes
Submit a support ticket to Google Cloud requesting that the optimizer be pinned to a specific version for the database so that plan choice does not change between releases of the Spanner service
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because dropping indexes harms other queries. Option B is correct because FORCE_INDEX is the documented Spanner mechanism to force a particular index for a given query. Option C is partially valid for stability across statistics refreshes but does not select a specific index. Option D is wrong because optimizer pinning is not a customer-facing support request and does not target a specific index.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A team is evaluating Cloud Run minimum instances cost versus the user impact of cold starts. They define an SLO that 99 percent of requests must complete under 500 ms. What is the most appropriate way to set min-instances?
options: Pick a min-instances value equal to the steady-state instance count observed at the lowest 1 percent traffic period so that quiet periods do not require any scale-up at all to meet the SLO
Pick a min-instances value of zero and rely on Cloud Run startup CPU boost so that cold start latency comes in under the 500 ms SLO target without paying for any idle warm capacity at all
Pick a min-instances value of one so that the service always has at least one warm container, then monitor SLO compliance and increase the value only if cold starts contribute to error budget burn
Pick a min-instances value equal to peak instance count seen during the busiest hour of the prior week so that the service never needs to scale out at runtime during the busiest periods
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because the SRE-aligned approach is to provision enough warm capacity to cover the long tail and then verify against SLO compliance, which means provisioning slightly above quiet-period baseline rather than zero or peak. Option B is wrong because zero min-instances often produces cold starts well above 500 ms. Option C is reasonable but option A reflects a more deliberate SLO-driven sizing. Option D is wrong because peak provisioning wastes capacity.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A BigQuery user runs a query that times out after 6 hours. The query joins three large fact tables and groups by 20 columns. Which optimization approach is most likely to bring the query within the timeout window?
options: Increase the slot reservation size assigned to the project running the query so that the additional concurrent slot capacity allows the join and aggregation stages to complete in less wall-clock time
Switch the query from standard SQL to legacy SQL so that the query planner uses the older execution engine, which is more permissive with very wide grouping sets and large self-joins on fact tables
Add appropriate partition filters and clustering filters to each fact table in the query and rewrite the GROUP BY to project only the columns actually consumed by downstream consumers of the result
Increase the BigQuery query timeout setting in the project configuration so that the query has more wall-clock time to complete the join and aggregation stages without being killed by the platform
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because adding slots can help but does not address the fundamental shape of the query, which scans too much and groups too wide. Option B is wrong because legacy SQL is deprecated and not a remedy. Option C is correct because partition and clustering filters reduce bytes scanned and a tighter GROUP BY reduces shuffle and aggregation cost, both of which directly attack the timeout root cause. Option D is wrong because there is no broadly configurable extension beyond the platform's 6-hour cap.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: A team wants to apply Google Cloud Recommenders to drive ongoing performance optimization across a fleet of Compute Engine VMs and GKE clusters. Which recommender output most directly supports right-sizing for performance and cost?
options: The IAM recommender, which identifies unused service account permissions across the project and suggests removing them so that latency on IAM checks during instance startup is reduced over time

The Cloud Storage recommender, which identifies buckets with low access patterns and suggests transitioning their storage class so that read latency from those buckets is reduced for hot reads at peak
The Compute Engine machine type recommender and the GKE cost optimization recommender, which analyze actual CPU and memory usage and suggest right-sizing machine types and resource requests

The Cloud SQL idle instance recommender, which identifies database instances that have low CPU and low connection counts and suggests deletion so that the database fleet's overall efficiency is increased
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because the IAM recommender is for permission hygiene, not VM or container sizing. Option B is wrong because the Storage recommender focuses on lifecycle policies, not VM right-sizing. Option C is correct because the machine type recommender and GKE cost optimization recommender are the documented tools for right-sizing CPU and memory across the fleet. Option D is wrong because the idle Cloud SQL recommender is narrowly about idle databases, not fleet-wide right-sizing of Compute Engine and GKE.
questions.category: Optimizing service performance

questions.question: During a SEV1 incident, your on-call engineer is simultaneously triaging the root cause, updating the status page, responding to Slack messages from stakeholders, and coordinating a rollback. The incident has lasted 45 minutes longer than expected. Which role assignment best improves incident resolution speed?
options: Escalate the incident to the VP of Engineering immediately so that executive visibility creates organizational urgency for a faster resolution outcome
Split the on-call engineer's tasks by bringing in a second engineer to lead rollback while the original engineer continues coordinating all stakeholder communications
Assign a dedicated communications lead to handle status page updates and stakeholder messaging, freeing the on-call engineer to focus exclusively on technical mitigation
Declare a SEV2 downgrade to reduce stakeholder pressure and give the on-call engineer additional cognitive space to focus on diagnosis without interruption
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because executive escalation increases pressure and interruptions and does not directly accelerate technical resolution work. Option B is wrong because having the original engineer continue all communications still splits focus; the comms role should be separated entirely. Option C is correct because the incident commander pattern separates technical mitigation from communications, which is the standard SRE practice to reduce cognitive load during prolonged SEV1s. Option D is wrong because downgrading severity while the outage is active misrepresents impact to stakeholders and violates incident classification integrity.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: Your team is defining incident severity levels. Production is fully unavailable for all customers due to a regional GKE control plane failure that prevents new deployments and breaks customer logins. Which severity classification is most appropriate for this scenario?
options: SEV1 because the service is completely unavailable to all users, which is the highest impact tier and triggers the full incident response process with an incident commander
SEV2 because the underlying GKE infrastructure is the failure source rather than your application code, so impact is shared with the cloud provider's responsibility scope
SEV3 because customer logins still work intermittently when retried and new deployments are typically not a customer-facing concern during normal business hours
SEV4 because Google Cloud will resolve the regional control plane issue under their SLA and your team has no direct mitigation path to apply for this failure
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because SEV1 is defined as a complete outage affecting all users, which matches a scenario where customers cannot log in and the service is unavailable. Option B is wrong because severity is determined by customer impact, not by whether the root cause is in your code or the provider's infrastructure. Option C is wrong because the scenario describes a complete outage, not intermittent degradation, and SEV3 is reserved for minor impact. Option D is wrong because severity reflects current customer impact regardless of who applies the fix, and shared responsibility does not lower severity.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: Your postmortem process keeps producing action items that are never completed because no one is accountable. Engineers complete a thorough document but follow-through is poor. Which change to the postmortem workflow would most reliably improve action item completion rates over time?
options: Add a section to each postmortem requiring the incident commander to personally complete every action item within two weeks of the document being published
Require every postmortem document to be reviewed and signed off by a director-level executive before action items can be considered approved for execution
Move action items into a public dashboard with severity tags but without assigned owners so that any engineer with capacity can pick up work opportunistically
Assign each action item a named owner with a due date and track open items in a recurring SRE review meeting that surfaces overdue items to leadership
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because making the incident commander solely responsible centralizes work poorly and does not scale across teams or domains of expertise. Option B is wrong because director sign-off adds friction without ensuring follow-through; the gap is execution tracking, not approval authority. Option C is wrong because action items without owners predictably go undone; SRE practice requires named ownership for accountability. Option D is correct because named owners, due dates, and recurring review meetings are the standard SRE pattern for ensuring postmortem action items actually get completed.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: During an on-call shift, an engineer receives 47 Cloud Monitoring alerts overnight, of which only 3 required any action. The team is experiencing serious alert fatigue and missed signals. Which approach best addresses the root cause of the fatigue problem?
options: Increase the alerting threshold values uniformly by 50% across all alerting policies in Cloud Monitoring so fewer notifications fire during the on-call shift window
Audit each alerting policy and remove or rewrite any alert that does not require human action within minutes, keeping only actionable alerts tied to SLO burn rates
Add a 30-minute alert deduplication window in PagerDuty so that repeated alerts for the same issue are grouped into a single notification for the on-call engineer
Rotate on-call duty more frequently across more team members so that no single engineer experiences the full cumulative volume during an extended on-call window
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because raising thresholds uniformly is a blunt fix that risks suppressing real signals while keeping noisy alerts; the issue is alert design, not threshold tuning. Option B is correct because the SRE definition of a good alert is one requiring human action; auditing and removing non-actionable alerts directly fixes alert fatigue. Option C is wrong because deduplication helps with storms but does not solve the underlying problem of non-actionable alerts being routed to humans. Option D is wrong because spreading toil across more engineers does not reduce total toil; it just distributes the burden of fatigue more widely.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: Your team is planning a game day to simulate a regional Cloud SQL failover. What scoping decision is most important to make before executing the chaos engineering exercise to protect production while still gaining value?
options: Define the blast radius explicitly by listing which services, regions, and customer segments may be affected, and prepare a documented rollback plan with clear abort criteria
Schedule the game day during peak traffic hours so the failover test is exercised under realistic load and any latency regressions are visible in customer-facing metrics
Invite the entire engineering organization to observe in real time so that maximum learning is distributed across teams who do not normally participate in SRE exercises
Disable all alerting during the exercise window so that the on-call rotation is not paged for the intentionally injected failure events during the chaos engineering test
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because chaos engineering practice requires defining blast radius and rollback criteria before injection to limit damage if the experiment behaves unexpectedly. Option B is wrong because running destructive experiments at peak load amplifies customer impact and is the opposite of safe game day practice. Option C is wrong because broad observation does not protect production; the question asks about safety scoping, not learning distribution. Option D is wrong because disabling alerting eliminates the team's ability to detect unintended consequences and is a dangerous game day antipattern.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: A production readiness review (PRR) is being conducted for a new payment service launching next month. Which item is the most critical PRR gate to require before approving the launch into the production environment?
options: A marketing announcement plan has been drafted and reviewed by the product team so that customer-facing messaging about the new payment service is consistent across channels
Every dependency including database, caching, and external payment APIs has been load tested at three times the expected traffic to ensure abundant performance headroom
The engineering team has completed a unit test coverage report showing greater than 90% line coverage across the payment service's main business logic modules
Defined SLOs, runbooks for the top failure modes, and an established on-call rotation are all in place before any production customer traffic is routed to the service
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because marketing readiness is a launch communications concern and is not part of an SRE production readiness gate. Option B is wrong because load testing at 3x is a useful practice but is not the single most critical PRR requirement; operational readiness comes first. Option C is wrong because unit test coverage is a code quality metric and does not establish that the service can be operated safely in production. Option D is correct because SLOs, runbooks, and on-call coverage are the SRE PRR essentials that ensure the service can be monitored, supported, and recovered after launch.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: Your team is writing a postmortem for an incident caused by an engineer running a manual database migration that locked a critical table. Which approach to the postmortem best reflects blameless culture as practiced in SRE?
options: Document that the engineer should have known better, recommend retraining for that individual specifically, and add their name to the action item list as the responsible party
Focus on the systems and processes that allowed the manual migration to occur, such as missing safeguards, runbook gaps, or lack of automation, without identifying the individual
Omit the incident entirely from the postmortem repository to protect the engineer's reputation and avoid embarrassment, instead handling the issue privately with management
Assign the engineer to lead the writing of the postmortem document themselves as a learning exercise so they understand the full impact of the mistake they personally caused
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because naming the individual and recommending retraining is the classic blameful pattern that suppresses honest reporting and creates fear of postmortems. Option B is correct because blameless postmortems focus on systemic causes such as missing guardrails, automation gaps, and runbook deficiencies rather than individual fault.
