Option C is wrong because omitting incidents prevents organizational learning and is a violation of the postmortem-everything SRE norm. Option D is wrong because forcing the individual to lead the writeup as punishment is shaming under the guise of learning and undermines blameless culture.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: You are designing an on-call rotation for a 6-person SRE team supporting a 24/7 service. The team is geographically distributed across three time zones. Which rotation design best balances coverage, fatigue, and follow-the-sun principles?
options: A single primary on-call engineer covers a full week including weekends, with no secondary, and rotates among the 6 engineers so each person is on call once every six weeks
Two engineers share primary and secondary roles for week-long shifts in their local business hours, handing off to engineers in the next time zone at the end of each day
Follow-the-sun rotation pairing engineers from each region so handoffs happen at end-of-business in each time zone, with weekly rotation and documented handoff procedures
All 6 engineers are simultaneously on call every day so that no individual experiences high pager load and incidents always have multiple responders immediately available
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because a week-long solo rotation with no secondary creates burnout risk and a single point of failure if the on-call engineer is unreachable. Option B is wrong because two engineers covering only business hours leaves explicit gaps and conflates primary and secondary responsibilities. Option C is correct because follow-the-sun rotation with documented handoffs across time zones is the SRE-recommended pattern for global teams to minimize off-hours pages. Option D is wrong because everyone being on call simultaneously means no one is truly on call; accountability is diffused and pager response degrades.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: Your capacity planning forecast shows that based on current growth, a Cloud Spanner instance will reach 90% CPU utilization in 8 weeks. The team typically targets 40% steady-state utilization with headroom for traffic spikes. What is the most appropriate capacity planning action?
options: Wait until utilization reaches 80% before scaling so that the team does not provision resources prematurely and avoid wasting cloud spend on unused Spanner compute capacity
Apply autoscaling policies to the Cloud Spanner instance with a target of 90% CPU so that scale-out events fire only when truly necessary based on the existing utilization curve
Trigger a capacity expansion now by adding Spanner nodes to bring projected utilization back below 40% well before the forecast threshold and re-run the model to validate headroom
Reduce traffic to the service by adding rate limiting at the API gateway so that the instance does not exceed the steady-state target during the forecast growth window
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because waiting until 80% means provisioning under pressure with little margin for surprise traffic; SRE capacity planning is proactive. Option B is wrong because a 90% autoscaling target leaves almost no headroom and contradicts the stated steady-state goal of 40%. Option C is correct because SRE capacity planning adds resources proactively to maintain headroom targets before forecast utilization breaches the comfort zone. Option D is wrong because rate limiting customers to dodge a capacity decision degrades the service and is not capacity planning; it shifts the problem onto customers.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: You are leading a disaster recovery exercise for a multi-region Cloud SQL deployment. The business requires a Recovery Time Objective (RTO) of 30 minutes and a Recovery Point Objective (RPO) of 5 minutes. Which architecture and process combination meets both targets?
options: Cloud SQL with cross-region read replicas, automated failover procedure documented in a runbook, and quarterly DR drills that validate end-to-end recovery time stays within the 30-minute RTO
Cloud SQL single-region instance with hourly on-demand backups exported to a multi-region Cloud Storage bucket, with manual restore procedures executed by the on-call team during DR
Cloud SQL with weekly backup snapshots and a documented restore process, with an annual tabletop DR exercise to validate the team understands the steps without actually executing them
Cloud SQL with point-in-time recovery enabled but no cross-region replication, relying on Google Cloud's regional redundancy to provide adequate disaster recovery for both RTO and RPO needs
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because cross-region replicas keep RPO low through near-real-time replication and a documented automated failover meets a 30-minute RTO; quarterly drills validate the targets. Option B is wrong because hourly backups give a 60-minute RPO worst case and manual restore from object storage typically exceeds a 30-minute RTO. Option C is wrong because weekly snapshots cannot meet a 5-minute RPO and tabletop exercises without live execution do not validate actual recovery time. Option D is wrong because regional redundancy does not protect against region-level failures, and PITR alone does not provide a multi-region failover path.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: A team's toil measurement shows that on-call engineers are spending 45% of their time on repetitive, manual tasks. Google's SRE practice recommends an upper bound on toil. What is the recommended response when toil exceeds the established budget?
options: Add more engineers to the rotation so that the absolute hours of toil per engineer drop below the budget threshold even if total team toil hours remain unchanged across the group
Halt new project work on the affected team and prioritize automation engineering until toil drops below the 50% budget threshold, escalating to leadership if the trend persists
Reclassify some of the toil work as engineering work in the time tracking system so that the team's reported toil percentage falls below the budget without changing actual workload
Outsource the toil tasks to a separate operations team so the SRE team's measured toil drops while the underlying repetitive work continues to be performed by a different group
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because spreading toil across more people lowers individual exposure but does not reduce total toil; SRE practice targets total toil reduction through automation. Option B is correct because the SRE prescription is to cap toil at 50% and respond to budget breaches by prioritizing automation work over feature work, escalating if the trend continues. Option C is wrong because reclassification is gaming the metric rather than addressing the underlying repetitive work that causes burnout. Option D is wrong because outsourcing toil moves the problem and creates a separate team doing pure operations, which violates SRE principles of engineering-first.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: An incident commander is leading a multi-hour SEV1 incident response. Communication cadence with stakeholders has become inconsistent. What communication pattern best supports stakeholders during an extended incident response?
options: Send a final status update only when the incident is fully resolved so that stakeholders are not distracted by partial information during the active mitigation work that is in progress
Send updates whenever new technical information is discovered, with the frequency varying based on the incident commander's judgment about whether stakeholders need to know now
Establish a predictable cadence such as every 30 minutes for SEV1, with status page updates, internal Slack updates, and stakeholder summaries even when no new information is available
Delegate all stakeholder communications to the engineering manager so the incident commander is not interrupted by communication tasks during the technical investigation and mitigation phase
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because withholding updates during long incidents erodes trust and amplifies stakeholder anxiety; regular cadence is required even with partial information. Option B is wrong because update frequency tied to discoveries leads to long silences during stable triage phases when stakeholders most need reassurance. Option C is correct because a predictable cadence such as 30 minutes for SEV1 is standard SRE practice; even no-news updates maintain stakeholder confidence and reduce side channel pressure. Option D is wrong because the incident commander typically delegates to a comms lead, not to the engineering manager whose role and bandwidth differ from a comms lead.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: Your team uses the 5-whys technique during postmortem root cause analysis. After three iterations of asking why, the team has identified that an engineer typed the wrong command. Which next-level question continues a productive 5-whys analysis?
options: Why did the system accept and execute a destructive command without a confirmation prompt, dry-run preview, or automated guardrail to prevent this class of human error in production?
Why did this specific engineer not check the command syntax twice before running it, given the production criticality of the database that the migration was targeting at that time?
Why is this particular engineer still on the team after making such a serious error in production, and should performance management be considered as a corrective action going forward?
Why did the management chain fail to instill a sufficient sense of caution in the engineering team about running production database commands during the on-call shift window late at night?
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because the 5-whys should drive toward systemic factors; asking why the system lacks guardrails moves analysis from individual action to fixable system design. Option B is wrong because focusing on whether the individual was careful enough is blameful and produces no systemic learning or action item. Option C is wrong because performance management is not part of 5-whys and contradicts blameless postmortem principles. Option D is wrong because attributing failure to insufficient caution from management is vague, unactionable, and still blames people rather than systems.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: Your service is launching a major feature next week. Marketing has scheduled a press release and traffic is expected to spike 5x. What change management practice best balances launch readiness with the risk of mid-launch incidents?
options: Cancel the launch entirely and reschedule once traffic forecasts come down, since launching during a high-attention window introduces unacceptable risk to the platform's reliability metrics
Continue normal weekly deployment cadence through the launch window so that any small fixes can ship rapidly and the team avoids accumulating undeployed changes during the campaign
Allow all normal deployments to continue but staff up the on-call rotation with a second primary on-call engineer for the launch week to handle any incidents that may arise
Implement a change freeze on non-launch-critical deployments starting 48 hours before the launch through the first 72 hours post-launch, with documented exception process for hotfixes
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because canceling a planned launch is overcorrection and abandons the business outcome; change freezes manage risk without canceling. Option B is wrong because shipping freely during a high-visibility launch increases the chance an unrelated change causes an incident during peak scrutiny. Option C is wrong because adding on-call staff is reactive; change freezes are the standard preventive control during high-stakes launches. Option D is correct because a scoped change freeze with documented exceptions is the SRE-recommended change management pattern for high-stakes launch windows.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: During a chaos engineering game day, the team injects a failure into a Pub/Sub subscription to simulate downstream consumer outage. The dependent service unexpectedly enters a crash loop instead of degrading gracefully. What is the correct immediate response from the game day lead?
options: Continue the experiment to observe how customer-facing systems behave under the cascading failure scenario, since the unexpected behavior provides valuable learning about hidden coupling between services
Halt the experiment immediately, execute the pre-defined rollback plan to restore the Pub/Sub subscription, and document the unexpected crash loop as a finding for follow-up engineering work
Page the broader engineering organization to mobilize an all-hands response and escalate to incident management protocols since the experiment has triggered a real customer-impacting outage
Restart the crashing service repeatedly using a script while leaving the Pub/Sub failure injection active so the team can observe whether the service eventually self-heals from the loop
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because continuing an experiment that has exceeded its blast radius violates the chaos engineering principle of bounded experimentation. Option B is correct because the standard chaos engineering response to unexpected severe behavior is to execute the rollback plan and capture the finding for follow-up; safety overrides curiosity. Option C is wrong because escalating to full incident response is appropriate only if rollback fails; the first action is to use the prepared rollback. Option D is wrong because keeping the failure injected while restarting the dependent service amplifies customer impact and is the opposite of a safe abort.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: Your team operates a service with a defined SLO of 99.9% availability. A SEV2 incident has caused 12 minutes of degraded performance affecting roughly 30% of users. Which action best reflects SRE incident classification and response practice?
options: Reclassify the incident as SEV3 since only 30% of users are affected rather than all users, which makes the actual impact tier lower than the SEV2 classification originally assigned by the on-call
Maintain the SEV2 classification, run the standard SEV2 incident response process, and ensure the burn from the 12-minute window is captured in error budget accounting after resolution
Upgrade the incident to SEV1 because the SLO is consuming error budget faster than projected, which automatically escalates classification regardless of the percentage of users actually affected
Pause incident response process steps until you can confirm whether the 30% affected users include any enterprise tier customers, since severity tiers vary by customer commercial tier in your model
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because reclassifying mid-incident based on partial impact is inappropriate; SEV2 means major degradation, which matches 30% user impact. Option B is correct because SEV2 represents major degradation affecting a significant user share, and error budget burn is captured post-resolution as part of standard SRE practice. Option C is wrong because burning error budget faster than projected does not automatically promote severity; severity reflects current customer impact, not budget trajectory. Option D is wrong because pausing incident response to assess tier impact delays mitigation and conflates billing tier with severity classification.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: Your team is integrating PagerDuty with Cloud Monitoring for the first time. Which escalation policy design best balances responsiveness with avoiding unnecessary wakeups during overnight pages?
options: Page the entire team simultaneously for every alert so that the first available engineer can respond, removing the dependency on whether a specific person is currently reachable at that moment
Page only the primary on-call with no escalation, since escalation policies add latency and primary on-call engineers are responsible for responding to all pages during their assigned shift
Page the manager first for visibility, then escalate to the primary on-call only after the manager has assessed whether the alert is truly an incident requiring an engineering response action
Page the primary on-call first with a 15-minute timeout, escalate to the secondary on-call if unacknowledged, then escalate to the manager after another 15 minutes if still unacknowledged
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because paging the entire team for every alert causes mass alert fatigue and erodes the on-call rotation discipline. Option B is wrong because no-escalation policies fail when the primary is unreachable, leaving alerts unanswered and SLO commitments at risk. Option C is wrong because routing all pages through the manager first creates a coordination bottleneck and routinely engages leadership for routine pages, slowing response. Option D is correct because the standard PagerDuty escalation pattern pages the primary first with a 15-minute timeout, then secondary, then manager only as a final fallback, which balances responsiveness with avoiding unnecessary wakeups.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: A blameless postmortem document for a major incident has been completed. The author wants to share it broadly. Which distribution practice best supports organizational learning while preserving the blameless culture going forward?
options: Publish the postmortem to a company-wide accessible location with sanitized but specific technical detail, and present highlights at an engineering all-hands to spread systemic learnings
Distribute the postmortem only to the immediate team and the affected service stakeholders to limit exposure of the engineering mistakes that led to the customer impact described in the document
Keep the postmortem private between the SRE team and management to avoid creating a public record that could be referenced negatively in future performance reviews of the engineers involved
Publish a heavily redacted version that removes all technical details so that no one outside the immediate team can identify what specifically went wrong during the incident investigation phase
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because broad publication with technical specifics drives organizational learning, which is the entire purpose of blameless postmortems in SRE practice. Option B is wrong because limiting distribution undermines learning and signals that incidents are something to hide rather than learn from. Option C is wrong because keeping postmortems private to protect engineers from performance review consequences is the antithesis of blameless culture and discourages reporting. Option D is wrong because removing technical details makes the document useless for learning; the goal is to share systemic findings, not redact them.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: Your service depends on three external third-party APIs. A capacity planning exercise reveals that one of these APIs has a rate limit of 1,000 requests per second, while your peak forecast is 850 requests per second. What capacity planning action is most appropriate?
options: Request a higher rate limit from the vendor without making any internal architectural changes, since the vendor is the constraining party and your service has no way to address the limit otherwise
Implement aggressive client-side caching of API responses to reduce calls but defer any conversation with the vendor since the current 850 peak still stays within the 1,000 rate limit threshold

Engage the vendor to increase the rate limit AND add a caching layer or request batching to provide headroom, since 85% utilization leaves little buffer for unexpected traffic spikes during peak periods

Switch to a different API provider entirely to eliminate the rate limit risk, since vendor-imposed limits are a fundamental architectural risk that cannot be properly addressed through capacity planning
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because requesting a higher limit without internal mitigations leaves the service single-dependent on vendor response time. Option B is wrong because operating at 85% of a third-party rate limit with no buffer is unsafe; spikes will breach the limit. Option C is correct because capacity planning for third-party dependencies should combine vendor negotiation with internal mitigations like caching or batching to maintain healthy headroom. Option D is wrong because switching providers is a major architectural change that capacity planning alone does not justify when simpler mitigations exist.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: You are running a disaster recovery test for a service backed by Cloud Spanner with multi-region configuration. The test simulates a complete regional outage. Which test outcome metric is most important to validate against SRE practice?
options: Measure how many engineers participated in the disaster recovery test exercise so that organizational readiness is tracked and reported up to leadership as part of operational excellence metrics
Measure actual end-to-end recovery time during the test and compare it against the documented RTO target, capturing any deviations as findings for runbook or architecture updates
Measure the cost of running the disaster recovery test in cloud resource consumption so that the program can justify its budget allocation to executive stakeholders during quarterly business reviews
Measure the number of runbook pages consulted by the on-call team during the test so that the operations team can prune unnecessary documentation pages from the SRE knowledge repository going forward
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because counting participants is a process metric, not an outcome metric, and does not validate recovery capability. Option B is correct because measuring actual recovery time against the documented RTO is the core SRE DR validation metric; deviations drive improvement work. Option C is wrong because tracking test cost is a financial concern, not a recovery readiness metric. Option D is wrong because counting runbook pages consulted is unrelated to whether the disaster recovery objective was met during the test execution.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: Your on-call engineer is paged at 3 AM for a Cloud Monitoring alert that fires every Tuesday around the same time. The engineer investigates and finds the alert is caused by a scheduled batch job that briefly spikes a metric, which is expected behavior and not a customer issue. What is the correct response?
options: Modify the alerting policy to exclude the batch job's expected spike window, or rewrite the alert to use a longer evaluation window that ignores short-duration metric blips during scheduled jobs

Document the weekly spike in the runbook so future on-call engineers know to ignore the page when it fires on Tuesdays, since this preserves alerting coverage for any other unexpected anomaly patterns
Page the on-call manager to make a decision about whether the alert should be modified, since the on-call engineer should not unilaterally change alerting policies without management approval first

Mark the alert as a known issue in PagerDuty so that future pages from this specific alert are automatically resolved without paging anyone, leaving the alert in place for historical tracking purposes
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because rewriting or scoping the alerting policy to exclude expected non-actionable conditions directly addresses the alert fatigue source, which is the SRE prescription. Option B is wrong because runbook entries telling engineers to ignore pages is exactly the alert fatigue antipattern; non-actionable alerts should be removed, not annotated. Option C is wrong because SRE empowers on-call engineers to tune alerts based on direct experience without needing manager approval for each change. Option D is wrong because auto-resolving without paging keeps a noisy alert active in the system and signals nothing useful when the underlying cause is benign.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: A team's incident management process requires that every incident has an incident commander (IC) and a communications lead (comms). For a SEV3 incident affecting only internal tooling, how should role assignment differ from a SEV1?
options: All roles including IC, comms, and a scribe must be staffed identically regardless of severity to ensure consistency in incident response process and to avoid skipping any standard procedure step

A single on-call engineer typically fills both IC and comms responsibilities for SEV3 because internal-only impact does not warrant separating roles, but the formal IC role still applies to the response
No IC or comms lead is required for SEV3 incidents since these are minor by definition; the on-call engineer simply works the issue without using the incident management process at all
The IC role rotates among all available engineers regardless of expertise so that more team members get practice running incident response procedures on lower-stakes SEV3 scenarios
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because requiring full staffing for SEV3 wastes responder bandwidth on minor incidents that do not justify it. Option B is correct because SRE practice scales role separation to severity; a SEV3 with internal-only impact typically combines IC and comms in one person. Option C is wrong because skipping incident management entirely for SEV3 loses the ability to track and learn from minor incidents, which often surface systemic issues. Option D is wrong because rotating IC for practice on real incidents is risky; structured game days or shadowing are better practice vehicles than live incidents.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: Your team has identified that a particular toil task ”” manually rotating service account keys every 90 days ”” consumes 12 hours per quarter across the team. The toil budget for the team allows up to 50% time on toil, and the team is currently at 35%. What is the most appropriate action?
options: Defer automation of key rotation indefinitely since the team is well within the toil budget and engineering time is better spent on feature work rather than incremental operational improvements right now
Manually rotate the keys but rotate them every 180 days instead of every 90 days to halve the toil burden, accepting the security tradeoff implicit in a longer credential rotation window
Outsource the key rotation work to a managed service vendor who specializes in credential lifecycle management so the SRE team's toil burden is moved off-team to an external operations group

Prioritize automating key rotation using Secret Manager or Workload Identity Federation since automating recurring toil is a core SRE responsibility, even when within budget, to free time for higher-value work
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because being under budget does not mean toil should be deferred forever; toil compounds and crowds out engineering work over time. Option B is wrong because trading security posture for less toil is a poor tradeoff and ignores better solutions like Workload Identity Federation. Option C is wrong because outsourcing operational work to a vendor is generally not aligned with SRE principles of engineering automation in-house. Option D is correct because automating recurring toil is a core SRE responsibility; Workload Identity Federation eliminates the rotation task entirely and frees engineering capacity.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: During a postmortem review meeting, an engineer notes that the same root cause ”” a missing health check timeout configuration ”” has appeared in three separate incidents over the past six months. What is the most appropriate response from the SRE team?
options: Document the recurring root cause in the current postmortem and continue normal action item assignment, since each incident is treated as its own event and lessons accumulate over time across documents
Add a single action item to the current postmortem requiring the responsible engineer to fix the health check timeout in the affected service before the next incident response cycle begins

Escalate the recurring pattern to engineering leadership as a systemic gap, prioritize a cross-service audit of health check configurations, and treat this as a higher-priority action than typical postmortem items

Reclassify the recurring incidents as a single ongoing issue and close all three postmortems with a consolidated document that simplifies the historical record for the team's quarterly reliability review
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because treating recurring causes as isolated events misses the systemic pattern that SRE practice explicitly looks for during postmortem reviews. Option B is wrong because a single-service fix does not address the systemic gap that allowed the same misconfiguration to recur across services. Option C is correct because recurring root causes signal a systemic issue requiring escalation and broader remediation; this is the explicit purpose of trend analysis across postmortems. Option D is wrong because consolidating postmortems hides the historical record of three separate incidents and loses the visibility of pattern recurrence.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: Your team is creating a runbook for a common database connection pool exhaustion alert. Which content makes the runbook most useful to the on-call engineer responding to the alert at 2 AM?
options: Specific commands to check current pool utilization, decision criteria for when to scale connections versus restart pods, and a documented rollback procedure if the mitigation does not work as expected
A high-level overview of how database connection pooling works conceptually so the on-call engineer can reason about the situation from first principles during the incident response process

Links to the database vendor's official documentation and the GKE pod scaling documentation so the on-call engineer can look up the latest authoritative information during the active incident response

A flowchart showing the organizational escalation path for connection pool incidents so the on-call engineer knows who to page if the issue cannot be mitigated within the first ten minutes of investigation
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because runbooks should provide specific actionable commands, decision criteria, and rollback steps; this is the SRE standard for operational documentation. Option B is wrong because conceptual overviews of how pooling works are educational but not what an on-call engineer needs at 2 AM under time pressure. Option C is wrong because pointing on-call engineers to external documentation during an incident wastes time and is the opposite of what runbooks should provide. Option D is wrong because escalation flowcharts are useful but secondary to the actual mitigation steps the on-call engineer needs first.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: A production readiness review for a new microservice surfaces that there is no defined SLI for the service. The team argues that they can define SLIs after launch once they have production traffic data. What is the correct PRR response?
options: Approve the launch with a follow-up action item to define SLIs within 30 days post-launch, since production data provides better SLI inputs than estimates and the team can adjust quickly after launching
Allow the launch but require the service to be marked as preview-only until SLIs are defined, restricting access to internal users only and gating wider rollout on the completion of SLI definition
Reject approval and require the team to define SLIs prior to launch based on stakeholder agreement about what success looks like, since SLIs cannot be retrofitted credibly after a service is live
Define generic placeholder SLIs based on common patterns such as latency and error rate, and let the team refine them after launch with the placeholder values serving as initial baseline guidance
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because deferring SLIs post-launch undermines the entire purpose of the PRR gate; without SLIs the team cannot know if the service is meeting reliability expectations. Option B is wrong because a preview-only workaround dodges the gate rather than addressing the requirement. Option C is correct because SLI definition is a PRR gating requirement; SLIs must be agreed before launch so the service can be measured against expectations from day one. Option D is wrong because generic placeholder SLIs lack the stakeholder agreement that gives SLIs their meaning; they are not real SLIs.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: Your team is reviewing chaos engineering practice maturity. The current state is that the team runs one game day per quarter against the staging environment. What is the most appropriate next step to mature the practice toward Google's SRE recommendations?
options: Increase the frequency of game days to one per month in staging, while also expanding the catalog of failure scenarios tested, before considering any expansion to production environments at all

Move directly to running chaos experiments in production with no further staging exercises, since production is the only environment with realistic traffic patterns and dependency interactions to test against

Begin running carefully scoped chaos experiments in production with bounded blast radius, automated abort criteria, and a documented rollback plan, alongside continued staging game days for new scenarios

Replace game days entirely with synthetic monitoring tests that simulate failures continuously, since automated continuous testing is superior to periodic game days for catching reliability regressions over time
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because while increasing frequency is positive, restricting to staging indefinitely limits learning about production-only failure modes such as real dependency latency. Option B is correct because progressing chaos engineering maturity requires gradually expanding from staging to production with bounded blast radius and abort criteria, while continuing staging exercises for new scenarios. Option C is wrong because skipping the controlled expansion phase and moving directly to production with no further safety work is reckless. Option D is wrong because synthetic monitoring complements but does not replace game days, which test novel scenarios humans devise.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: An incident commander is leading a SEV1 response. After 90 minutes, the team has applied a mitigation and customer impact has ended. Which step best reflects the correct procedure for declaring the incident over?
options: Declare the incident resolved immediately once the customer-facing metrics return to normal so that the on-call team can stand down and resume normal duties without further procedural overhead
Wait for the executive sponsor to formally approve closure of the incident so that organizational visibility is maintained through the highest-stakes decision points during the response cycle

Close the incident in PagerDuty and the status page system, archive the incident channel, and consider the response complete with no formal stabilization confirmation or postmortem assignment yet scheduled

Verify metrics have stabilized, confirm with the team that no follow-up actions are pending, communicate resolution to stakeholders, close in PagerDuty and the status page, and schedule the postmortem within 24 hours
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because declaring resolved the instant metrics normalize risks reopening if the fix was not durable; SRE practice requires a stabilization window. Option B is wrong because executive approval is not part of the standard incident closure flow and adds unnecessary procedural friction. Option C is wrong because closing tooling state without stabilization confirmation or postmortem scheduling skips essential steps of the SRE incident lifecycle. Option D is correct because formal closure includes stabilization verification, stakeholder communication, system closure in PagerDuty and the status page, and postmortem scheduling within 24 hours.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: Your team is reviewing the postmortem follow-up tracking dashboard. 60% of action items from the past quarter are overdue or unfinished. Which intervention best addresses the root cause of the follow-through failure?
options: Establish a recurring SRE review meeting where overdue action items are surfaced to engineering leadership, with the meeting cadence creating accountability and the surfacing creating peer-visible pressure
Reduce the number of action items per postmortem by being more selective during the document review process, so that fewer commitments are made and the completion rate appears higher on the dashboard
Move all postmortem action items into the engineering backlog with the same priority weighting as feature work, allowing product managers to triage them alongside other work during sprint planning
Assign all action items to the original incident commander permanently so that one individual is responsible for tracking and ensuring completion of every postmortem follow-up across the entire team
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because recurring review meetings with leadership visibility are the standard SRE practice for ensuring follow-through; accountability comes from surfacing not from individual responsibility alone. Option B is wrong because reducing commitments to improve the dashboard is gaming the metric rather than improving execution. Option C is wrong because mixing postmortem action items into feature backlogs lets them be perpetually deprioritized; reliability work needs its own track. Option D is wrong because centralizing all action items onto one person is unscalable and creates a bottleneck inconsistent with distributed ownership.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: Your on-call rotation has 8 engineers. Currently each engineer is primary on-call for one week per rotation, with no secondary defined. The team experiences frequent burnout complaints and missed pages during weekends. Which rotation redesign best addresses both issues?
options: Reduce primary on-call shifts to four days instead of seven, eliminating the weekend portion of the rotation entirely so that weekends are explicitly uncovered as a tradeoff for reducing engineer burnout
Introduce a secondary on-call role that backs up the primary, split weekdays from weekends into separate shorter shifts, and document handoff procedures to reduce both burnout and missed-page rate
Keep the week-long rotation but add a financial bonus for each weekend page handled, so engineers are incentivized to remain responsive during off-hours and accept the burden of being on call
Move to a two-week rotation per engineer so that handoffs occur less frequently and the engineer becomes more deeply familiar with active alerts and incidents during their extended on-call window
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because leaving weekends uncovered for a 24/7 service is unacceptable and not a real solution to burnout. Option B is correct because adding a secondary role, splitting weekday and weekend shifts, and documenting handoffs are the recognized SRE patterns to reduce burnout and missed pages simultaneously. Option C is wrong because paying bonuses for off-hours response is a compensation patch that does not fix the root cause of unsustainable shift design. Option D is wrong because longer shifts amplify burnout and miss-rate issues rather than addressing them.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: A capacity planning model for a Cloud Run service projects that current traffic doubles every 90 days. The team currently sets max instances at 100 and observes peak concurrency at 60. What is the most appropriate capacity planning action for the next 90 days?
options: Leave the max instances setting at 100 since the current peak of 60 is comfortably below the cap, and revisit the configuration only if alerts indicate that concurrency is approaching the existing limit
Set max instances to 60 to match current peak concurrency exactly, since Cloud Run autoscales based on demand and provisioning excess maximum capacity wastes potential cloud budget on unused headroom

Increase max instances to at least 150 to accommodate projected doubling within the planning horizon, leaving sufficient headroom buffer above the forecast peak, and update again at the next planning cycle

Migrate the service from Cloud Run to GKE so that capacity is fully controlled by the team without relying on Cloud Run's autoscaling defaults, giving the team finer-grained control over the scaling policy
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because waiting for alerts to indicate cap approach is reactive; SRE capacity planning is forward-looking based on growth forecasts. Option B is wrong because setting max equal to current peak provides zero headroom for the 90-day forecast doubling. Option C is correct because proactive capacity planning increases the cap above the forecast peak with comfortable headroom, then reviews at the next planning cycle. Option D is wrong because migrating platforms is a major architectural change not justified by capacity planning alone when simpler limit adjustments suffice.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: Your team's status page communication template for SEV1 incidents includes technical jargon such as 'pod eviction cascade' and 'control plane reconciliation lag'. Customer support is reporting confusion. Which status page improvement best supports customers during incidents?
options: Add an internal-only addendum with full technical detail while keeping the existing public message identical, so engineers monitoring the status page see the same content as customers during the incident

Remove all technical detail from public status page messages and replace them with generic statements like 'we are investigating an issue' to avoid any possibility of confusing non-technical customer audiences

Require customer support to interpret status page messages and write their own customer-facing translations on the support portal so that technical accuracy and customer clarity are maintained as separate streams

Translate technical descriptions into customer-impact language describing what users experience, such as 'some users may see slow page loads or errors when logging in', and reserve internal jargon for internal channels
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because adding internal addenda does not address customer confusion with the public message; the public message is still unclear. Option B is wrong because generic 'investigating an issue' messages erode trust by withholding information about what customers will actually experience. Option C is wrong because forcing customer support to retranslate every status page update creates inconsistent messaging across channels and adds friction during time-sensitive incidents. Option D is correct because translating technical descriptions into customer-impact language is the SRE communication best practice; status pages should describe what users experience, not internal mechanics.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: A team conducting a blameless postmortem identifies that the engineer who deployed the change should have run a canary first but skipped it under deadline pressure. How should this finding be captured in the postmortem document?
options: Identify the engineer by name in the timeline section and add an action item requiring them to complete additional training on deployment best practices before being allowed to deploy production changes

Document the systemic factors: deadline pressure that incentivized skipping canary, lack of automated enforcement requiring canary gates, and propose action items to enforce canary gates in the CI/CD pipeline

Omit the deployment skip entirely from the postmortem document so the engineer is not personally exposed, and instead document a generic 'change management gap' as the contributing factor in the writeup

Note that the engineer made a judgment call under pressure and document this as an acceptable tradeoff for tight delivery windows, with no follow-up action since deadline-driven shortcuts are part of operational reality
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because naming the individual and prescribing training is the classic blameful pattern that suppresses reporting and ignores systemic factors. Option B is correct because blameless postmortems document the systemic conditions ”” deadline pressure, missing automation ”” and propose systemic fixes such as enforcing canary gates in CI/CD. Option C is wrong because omitting the deployment skip hides the actual contributing factor and prevents systemic learning. Option D is wrong because accepting deadline-driven shortcuts as normal entrenches the pattern that caused the incident.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: Your team is implementing progressive rollouts using a blue-green strategy on GKE. What rollback trigger configuration best balances quick rollback with avoiding false positive rollbacks during minor metric blips?
options: Configure automated rollback based on error rate exceeding a threshold for a sustained duration such as 5 minutes, combined with manual rollback authority retained by the on-call engineer for edge cases

Configure automated rollback to trigger immediately when error rate exceeds the threshold for any duration, prioritizing fast rollback over the small risk of unnecessary rollbacks from transient metric spikes

Disable automated rollback entirely and rely on the on-call engineer to make all rollback decisions manually, since automation cannot account for context that humans understand about the deployment state

Configure automated rollback based on absolute count of failed requests over a 30-minute window, ignoring relative error rate since absolute counts are simpler to reason about during incident response
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because pairing a sustained-duration error rate trigger with retained manual authority balances responsiveness with stability and matches the SRE progressive rollout pattern. Option B is wrong because triggering on any-duration spikes causes false-positive rollbacks from transient blips, which themselves create instability. Option C is wrong because disabling automation puts the entire rollback decision on the on-call engineer under time pressure, which is slower than well-tuned automation. Option D is wrong because absolute counts ignore traffic volume context; a count threshold useful at 1 million requests is overly sensitive at 100 thousand.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: A team's disaster recovery runbook for failing over to a secondary region has not been tested in 18 months. The DR team argues testing is risky and the runbook is well-documented. Which response best reflects SRE practice on DR readiness?
options: Trust the documented runbook and avoid testing as long as no changes have been made to the underlying architecture during the 18-month window, since unchanged runbooks should remain valid by default

Schedule a tabletop DR exercise where the team walks through the runbook on paper to validate completeness, but avoid actual failover testing in production to eliminate any risk of test-induced incidents

Schedule a live DR test on a regular cadence such as quarterly or semi-annually, since untested DR procedures predictably fail in real disasters; the risk of a controlled test is far lower than an untested failover
Move all DR testing into a separate staging environment that mirrors production, so the failover can be exercised without any chance of touching production systems regardless of the test outcome
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because trusting an untested runbook is exactly the antipattern SRE warns against; runbooks degrade silently as systems change around them. Option B is wrong because tabletop exercises are useful but insufficient; only live tests reveal hidden coupling and runbook gaps. Option C is correct because regular live DR tests are SRE practice; the risk of a controlled test is small relative to the certainty of failure during an untested real disaster. Option D is wrong because staging DR tests do not exercise real production state, networking, or dependency behavior, so they cannot fully validate DR readiness.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: An on-call engineer is paged for a Cloud Monitoring alert tied to an SLO burn rate. The burn rate alert indicates 2% of monthly error budget will be consumed in the next hour at current rate. What is the appropriate response priority?
options: Treat the burn rate alert as low priority since 2% per hour means the budget will not be exhausted within the month, and continue with currently planned work before investigating the contributing cause

Acknowledge the page but defer action until business hours, since burn rate alerts indicate trends rather than active outages and can typically be addressed during normal working hours by the assigned engineer

Forward the page to the engineering manager for triage and prioritization decision-making, since burn rate alerts span longer timeframes than typical SEV-classified incidents typically do during operations

Investigate immediately as a high-priority issue since fast burn rate alerts indicate active error budget consumption that, if unchecked, threatens SLO compliance and may indicate an ongoing reliability issue
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because burn rate alerts are designed to alert when budget consumption is fast enough to matter; treating them as low priority defeats their purpose. Option B is wrong because deferring burn rate response until business hours allows ongoing consumption to compound and may exhaust budget before action is taken. Option C is wrong because forwarding to a manager adds delay; on-call engineers are empowered to respond to burn rate pages directly. Option D is correct because fast-burn alerts are explicitly designed to demand immediate investigation; sustained fast burn predicts SLO breach and requires active mitigation.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: A new junior engineer is joining the on-call rotation. The team uses PagerDuty for paging and Cloud Monitoring for alerting. Which onboarding practice best prepares them for productive on-call duty without overloading them?
options: Place the engineer on the primary on-call rotation immediately so they learn fastest through real incident exposure, with the team available on Slack to answer questions during their first few shifts

Have the engineer shadow an experienced on-call engineer during their shift for two weeks, then serve as secondary on-call with the primary handling decisions, before taking primary on-call responsibility
Restrict the engineer to documentation-writing duties for three months before any on-call exposure so they have time to read every runbook and become a domain expert before being paged

Add the engineer to the on-call rotation but reduce their shift length to one day initially, so they get exposure without the burden of a full week of pages while ramping up their incident response skills
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because dropping a junior engineer into primary on-call immediately is overwhelming and risks both the engineer and incident response quality. Option B is correct because shadowing followed by secondary then primary is the SRE-recommended progressive onboarding pattern that builds capability safely. Option C is wrong because three months of documentation-only delays meaningful on-call experience and is overcorrection. Option D is wrong because a shorter shift duration does not address the deeper need for guided exposure under more experienced supervision before primary responsibility.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: Your team is preparing a postmortem template. Which sections are essential to include for the document to support both immediate learning and long-term trend analysis across postmortems?
options: Timeline of events, root cause analysis, contributing factors, customer impact, action items with owners and due dates, and lessons learned with searchable tags for cross-postmortem trend analysis later on
Executive summary describing the incident in one paragraph, plus a detailed technical writeup of the root cause, focused on conveying the situation upward to leadership without operational specifics

Customer apology letter draft, regulatory disclosure assessment, and public relations talking points for the communications team to use when responding to media inquiries about the incident occurrence
Cost calculation of revenue lost during the incident, engineering hours consumed, and projected SLA penalties so that finance can assess the business impact and inform future investment decisions
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because the SRE postmortem template includes timeline, root cause, contributing factors, customer impact, owned action items, and tagged lessons learned for trend analysis. Option B is wrong because executive summaries alone do not support engineering learning and omit critical operational detail. Option C is wrong because regulatory and PR content belong elsewhere and are not core to operational learning. Option D is wrong because cost calculation is a business metric, not the structural content of a learning-focused postmortem document.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: A game day exercise is being planned to test the team's response to a simulated Cloud Spanner regional outage. Which preparation step is most important to maximize learning while protecting production?
options: Notify customers in advance via the status page that a planned exercise will occur, so that any actual customer impact during the test is contextualized as expected and reduces unnecessary support tickets

Run the exercise during off-peak traffic hours to minimize blast radius, but provide no internal notice to the responding on-call team so their reactions reflect a genuine unannounced incident scenario

Define explicit success criteria, blast radius limits, abort conditions, and pre-stage the rollback plan; brief participating teams ahead of time about scope and ensure all necessary stakeholders know the exercise is happening

Allow the exercise to escalate naturally without predefined abort criteria so that the team's full incident response capability including escalation to leadership is tested under realistic ambiguity conditions
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because customer-facing notification of routine internal exercises is unusual and not the key safety preparation step. Option B is wrong because surprising the on-call team without abort criteria or pre-staged rollback is reckless and contradicts game day safety practice. Option C is correct because game day preparation requires explicit success criteria, blast radius limits, abort conditions, rollback staging, and stakeholder awareness. Option D is wrong because letting an exercise escalate without abort criteria is the opposite of bounded chaos engineering and can cause real customer harm.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: Your team's incident management process requires a scribe role to capture timeline and decisions during SEV1 incidents. What is the most appropriate primary purpose of the scribe role in incident response?
options: Provide live communication updates to executive stakeholders during the incident so that leadership has real-time situational awareness without needing to join the response call directly during active mitigation
Coordinate handoffs between rotating responders during a multi-shift incident so that incoming engineers have a clear summary of what has been tried and what is currently in flight as actions

Perform live root cause analysis during the incident by tracking patterns in the timeline and surfacing hypotheses to the incident commander as the response progresses through different mitigation attempts

Capture an accurate timeline of events, decisions, and actions during the incident so the postmortem author has a reliable factual record rather than relying on participant memory days after the event
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because executive updates are typically handled by the comms lead, not the scribe; these are distinct roles. Option B is wrong because handoff coordination is a function of the IC and shift handoff documentation, not the primary scribe purpose. Option C is wrong because live RCA during the incident is the IC and engineers' job; scribes capture, they do not analyze. Option D is correct because the scribe role exists to produce an accurate event timeline so the postmortem is grounded in real-time facts rather than imperfect memory.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: Your team is reviewing change management practice. Currently any engineer can deploy to production at any time with no approval. After several incidents traced to risky deployments, what change is most appropriate?
options: Require all deployments to go through a CAB (change advisory board) review meeting that meets twice a week and approves each individual change before it can be released to production environments

Introduce risk-tiered change management: low-risk changes deploy freely, medium-risk changes require automated checks plus a peer reviewer, and high-risk changes require explicit sign-off with documented rollback plans
Block all deployments outside of business hours so that incidents from deployments can only occur during periods when engineers are present in their primary workplace and available to respond quickly

Mandate that all deployments require approval from a senior staff engineer, since senior judgment will filter out the risky changes that have been causing incidents over the past several months of operation
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because traditional CAB review on every change is slow, creates a bottleneck, and is widely recognized as a low-value control compared to modern progressive delivery practices. Option B is correct because risk-tiered change management matches control rigor to actual change risk; this is the modern SRE-aligned change management pattern. Option C is wrong because time-window restrictions reduce flexibility without addressing the underlying risk, and after-hours emergency deployments still need to be possible. Option D is wrong because routing every change through one senior engineer creates a single point of bottleneck and judgment burden.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: A team's on-call rotation has experienced three engineer departures in six months, all citing on-call burden. Exit interviews mention pager volume and weekend disruption. What is the highest-leverage intervention to retain remaining team members?
options: Investigate root causes of pager volume by auditing alerts for actionability, prioritizing automation of recurring toil sources, and reducing the on-call burden directly rather than addressing symptoms downstream

Increase on-call compensation pay to make the disruption financially worthwhile, since cash incentives can offset the personal cost of weekend pages and reduce attrition from on-call-related job dissatisfaction

Reduce on-call shift length from a week to two days so each engineer experiences less concentrated burden during their shift, with the cost being more frequent rotations and additional handoff overhead

Hire more engineers onto the team so that the rotation expands and each engineer's frequency of on-call duty decreases, addressing burden through dilution across a larger group of available responders
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because the SRE root-cause approach to on-call burnout is reducing pager volume and toil through alert hygiene and automation; this addresses the cause not the symptom. Option B is wrong because compensation alone does not reduce sleep deprivation, weekend disruption, or alert fatigue; it papers over an unsustainable design. Option C is wrong because shorter shifts redistribute burden without reducing it and add handoff overhead. Option D is wrong because hiring takes months and dilutes burden only marginally; if alerts are non-actionable, more engineers absorb more noise.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: Your team is performing a quarterly DR test on a service backed by Cloud SQL with cross-region read replicas. During the test, the failover took 47 minutes against an RTO target of 30 minutes. What is the appropriate post-test action?
options: Update the documented RTO target to 60 minutes to reflect the actual achievable recovery time observed during the test, since stakeholders should know the realistic capability rather than an aspirational target

Repeat the test multiple times to confirm whether the 47-minute result was an anomaly, and only investigate gaps if subsequent tests also exceed the 30-minute RTO target observed in this single test run

Investigate the specific steps that consumed the most time, identify automation or runbook improvements that close the gap, schedule follow-up tests to validate improvements, and report findings to stakeholders

Accept the 47-minute result as within acceptable variance since the team did successfully recover the service, even if 17 minutes over target, and prioritize other reliability work ahead of further DR optimization
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because moving the target to match poor performance is silently lowering commitments rather than improving capability. Option B is wrong because deferring investigation until a second confirmation wastes the diagnostic value of the first test, where memory of bottleneck steps is freshest. Option C is correct because the SRE DR practice is to investigate gaps surfaced by tests, drive improvements, and re-test to validate; this is how DR readiness compounds. Option D is wrong because accepting a 57% overshoot of RTO normalizes missing reliability targets and erodes the value of the target itself.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: A production readiness review surfaces that the service has no documented runbook for any of its top failure modes. The team argues they will write runbooks based on real incidents after launch. What is the appropriate PRR response?
options: Approve launch with a 30-day post-launch deadline for runbook creation, since real incidents will provide the most accurate scenarios and pre-launch runbooks based on hypothetical failures may not match reality

Conditionally approve launch contingent on runbooks being available before the service is exposed to any external traffic, even if the runbooks are initially based on hypothetical failure modes from the design review

Allow launch without runbook requirement but require the team to commit to writing one runbook per incident as they occur, building the runbook library organically through real production experience over time

Block launch until runbooks are written and reviewed for the top failure modes identified during design review, since on-call engineers need operational guidance from day one of production traffic exposure
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because deferring runbooks post-launch leaves on-call engineers without guidance during their first production incidents, which is exactly when runbooks are most valuable. Option B is wrong because conditional approval still launches without runbooks if internal traffic precedes external; this is too lenient on PRR gates. Option C is wrong because incident-driven runbook creation means the first occurrence of each failure mode happens with no runbook support, which is the gap PRR is meant to prevent. Option D is correct because PRR practice requires runbooks for the top design-identified failure modes to be available before launch so on-call engineers have operational guidance from day one.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: Your incident commander training program requires new ICs to shadow before leading. Which experience progression best prepares a new incident commander for handling SEV1 incidents independently?
options: Shadow at least three live incidents at various severity levels, lead one or two lower-severity incidents under mentorship from a senior IC, then take primary IC role for a SEV1 with a senior IC available as backup

Complete a written exam on incident response procedures and read the team's IC handbook end-to-end before being immediately added to the IC rotation for any severity level including SEV1 with no further shadowing

Lead a single tabletop exercise simulating an incident and then take primary IC role for SEV1 incidents based on demonstrated procedural knowledge, since real incident dynamics cannot be fully practiced in advance regardless

Shadow only SEV1 incidents specifically since lower severities provide insufficient practice for the role's full responsibilities, even if SEV1 incidents are rare enough that shadowing opportunities take years to accumulate
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because the SRE IC progression is shadow then lead under mentorship at lower severity then independent leadership at higher severity with backup; this builds capability progressively. Option B is wrong because written exams and reading do not develop the real-time judgment IC role requires; live exposure is essential. Option C is wrong because a single tabletop is insufficient and skips graduated live exposure that builds practical judgment under pressure. Option D is wrong because SEV1 shadowing alone is impractical and ignores valuable practice opportunities at lower severities.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: A team's postmortem culture is showing signs of strain: engineers privately complain that 'blameless' is only theoretical and that postmortems are referenced in performance reviews. What is the most effective leadership intervention?
options: Issue a public reminder from engineering leadership that blameless culture is a stated value, while continuing the existing performance review process which references reliability incidents as part of engineer accountability

Audit recent performance review processes for any instances where postmortems were referenced, publicly commit to firewalling postmortem content from performance evaluations, and discipline managers who violated the firewall

Move postmortem documents to a more restricted access list so that performance review participants cannot reference them, while leaving the existing performance review process unchanged in all other respects

Replace the term 'blameless' with 'just culture' in team communications so that the language better reflects organizational practice, while acknowledging that some accountability for repeated mistakes remains in scope
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because reminders without real change reinforce the cynicism that blameless is rhetoric while practice differs. Option B is correct because the credibility of blameless culture requires concrete action: auditing past reviews, firewalling postmortems from evaluations, and holding managers accountable for violations. Option C is wrong because restricting access undermines the learning purpose of postmortems and treats the symptom rather than the breach of culture. Option D is wrong because renaming the practice without changing the underlying behavior does nothing for engineer trust.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: Your team is creating an escalation policy for after-hours pages. Which escalation chain pattern best balances responsiveness, on-call sustainability, and avoidance of unnecessary leadership engagement?
options: Page primary on-call first with 10-minute timeout, then page the secondary on-call with 10-minute timeout, then page the engineering director immediately so leadership has full awareness of unresponded pages

Page primary on-call first with 5-minute timeout, escalate immediately to all team members in parallel if unacknowledged, ensuring some engineer responds quickly even at the cost of waking the entire team

Page primary on-call first with 5-minute timeout, page secondary on-call with 15-minute timeout, then page the engineering manager with 15-minute timeout if both primary and secondary fail to acknowledge

Page primary on-call first with 15-minute timeout, escalate to secondary on-call with 15-minute timeout, then escalate to manager only if both fail, with documented criteria for when manager notification is warranted
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because escalating to a director after two engineer steps is overaggressive and routinely engages leadership for routine pages. Option B is wrong because waking the entire team for any unacknowledged page is the alert fatigue antipattern at the rotation level. Option C is wrong because a 5-minute primary timeout often fires before a sleeping engineer can acknowledge, causing unnecessary secondary pages and amplifying on-call burden across the team. Option D is correct because a 15-minute primary timeout balances response time with allowing a sleeping engineer to wake; manager escalation only after primary and secondary both fail, with documented criteria, prevents routine manager paging.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: A capacity planning exercise reveals that your service's projected peak traffic in 6 months will require 4x the current Cloud Run instance allocation. The team's manager wants to defer scaling capacity until traffic actually grows. What is the best response?
options: Defer to the manager's request and revisit capacity planning when traffic doubles, since Cloud Run autoscales automatically and provisioning headroom in advance does not change actual resource consumption

Bypass the manager's decision by adjusting Cloud Run max instances directly without further discussion, since SRE engineers have ownership over capacity decisions and should not be blocked on management approval

Present a written risk assessment showing the consequences of insufficient capacity at peak traffic, including SLO breach probability and customer impact estimates, to inform the deferral decision with data

Escalate the disagreement to the manager's manager immediately so that the capacity decision is made at a higher organizational level, removing the ambiguity about whether the current manager should be making this call
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because Cloud Run max instances limits autoscaling; without raising the cap in advance the service cannot scale to projected peak, regardless of demand. Option B is wrong because bypassing the manager creates trust and accountability problems and is not a productive way to resolve disagreement. Option C is correct because presenting a structured risk assessment with quantified consequences is the professional SRE approach to surfacing capacity risks for informed decision-making. Option D is wrong because skipping the manager to escalate above damages the working relationship and is premature before structured data has been presented.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: Your team is reviewing its on-call handoff process. Currently, outgoing and incoming on-call engineers exchange a Slack message at the start of each rotation. Several incidents have been worsened by missed context from previous shifts. Which handoff improvement best addresses the issue?
options: Eliminate the handoff entirely since incidents that span multiple shifts are rare; instead require the incoming on-call to review the previous week's PagerDuty incident log if they want context on prior shift activity

Adopt a structured handoff meeting at the start of each shift with a template covering active incidents, ongoing investigations, recent alerts, and pending follow-ups, with the conversation recorded for reference

Require outgoing on-call to file a written shift report in a ticketing system, with incoming on-call expected to read it before their shift starts, replacing the lightweight Slack message approach entirely without meeting

Extend the on-call shift duration so handoffs occur less frequently, since the fewer handoffs the smaller the cumulative context loss across rotations and the less work spent on handoff procedural overhead
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because eliminating handoffs makes the context loss problem worse, not better; cross-shift context is exactly what handoffs preserve. Option B is correct because structured handoff meetings with templates and recordings are the SRE-recommended pattern for reliable context transfer between shifts. Option C is wrong because written reports without meetings provide no opportunity to ask clarifying questions and miss the nuance of synchronous discussion. Option D is wrong because longer shifts increase burnout and do not address the fundamental need for reliable context transfer at the inevitable handoff points.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: A SEV2 incident is in progress. The on-call engineer suspects the issue may be related to a recent deployment from another team. What is the most appropriate incident management practice?
options: Engage the deployment owner team immediately into the incident response, share suspected linkage, and coordinate a possible rollback if their deployment is confirmed as the cause, treating it as a joint response

Roll back the suspected deployment unilaterally without engaging the deployment owner team, since fastest mitigation should take priority over coordination and the deployment owner can be informed afterward

Wait for stronger evidence linking the deployment to the incident before contacting the deployment owner team, since false alarms damage trust and the on-call should not interrupt other teams without confidence

Document the suspicion in the incident channel but continue troubleshooting independently, since cross-team coordination during incidents adds communication overhead that slows down technical mitigation work
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because engaging the suspected deployment owner team early is the SRE incident response pattern for cross-team incidents; joint response shortens diagnosis and rollback time. Option B is wrong because unilateral rollback of another team's deployment without engagement breaks trust and may cause unintended secondary issues the deploying team would have warned about. Option C is wrong because waiting for stronger evidence prolongs the incident; the cost of a false-alarm engagement is far less than the cost of a prolonged outage. Option D is wrong because solo troubleshooting while suspecting a cross-team cause wastes time the suspected team could be using to investigate from their side.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: Your team operates a service with a tight SLO of 99.95% availability. A change freeze has been in effect for a week during the holiday traffic peak. A critical security vulnerability has been disclosed requiring an immediate patch. What is the correct change management response?
options: Continue the change freeze and document the security vulnerability for patching after the freeze ends, since the freeze policy was established for reliability reasons and exceptions undermine the discipline of the practice

Lift the change freeze entirely for the duration of the holiday peak, allowing normal deployments to resume, since security patches indicate the freeze was an unnecessary control given the security risks it created

Reject the security patch deployment outright, since the SLO is the primary commitment and any change during peak traffic threatens that commitment more than the unpatched vulnerability does in the short term

Invoke the documented change freeze exception process for security patches, scope the deployment narrowly to the minimum required change, follow progressive rollout, and prepare a rapid rollback plan in case of issues
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because pure freeze adherence ignoring security patches creates dangerous unpatched windows; freezes need documented exception paths. Option B is wrong because lifting the freeze entirely overcorrects and discards the reliability protection for non-security changes during peak traffic. Option C is wrong because rejecting a security patch trades a near-term unpatched vulnerability against an SLO concern, which is the wrong risk balance. Option D is correct because mature change management includes documented exception processes for security; narrow scope, progressive rollout, and rapid rollback are the safety controls that make exceptions safe.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: A team's incident management dashboard shows mean time to acknowledge (MTTA) has increased from 3 minutes to 9 minutes over the past quarter. Which investigation step is most likely to surface the root cause of the regression?
options: Replace the current paging tool with a different vendor since the MTTA regression coincides with the team's continued use of PagerDuty and a tool change may inject fresh responsiveness into the on-call workflow

Analyze recent alerts for actionability and noise volume, since rising non-actionable alerts predictably correlate with slower acknowledgment as on-call engineers learn to ignore or delay responding to low-signal pages

Mandate that all on-call engineers respond to pages within 2 minutes regardless of context, with manager-level enforcement for repeated violations of the response time target to drive MTTA improvement metrics
