Add more on-call engineers to the rotation to ensure that any single engineer is less likely to miss a page, since the most likely cause of slower acknowledgment is individual engineer availability gaps
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because replacing the tool is a heavy intervention before investigating the root cause; tool change rarely fixes acknowledgment behavior issues. Option B is correct because rising MTTA commonly correlates with alert fatigue; auditing actionability is the highest-leverage investigation step. Option C is wrong because enforcing response time targets without addressing alert quality punishes engineers for a systemic problem. Option D is wrong because adding engineers does not directly address why current engineers are slower to acknowledge; it dilutes the problem without solving it.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: Your service has just experienced a major incident affecting paying customers. The postmortem author wants to make the document customer-facing as part of transparency. Which approach best balances transparency with operational integrity?
options: Publish the internal postmortem verbatim to a public location, since maximum transparency builds trust and customers deserve to know all technical details about what went wrong during the incident impact period

Refuse to publish anything publicly about the incident, since internal documents contain operational details that competitors or attackers could leverage against your team during future incidents or investigations

Create a customer-facing summary that includes impact description, root cause in non-internal terms, and remediation steps, while keeping the full internal document with sensitive operational specifics for internal use

Publish only the action items from the internal postmortem without root cause or impact description, since customers want to know what is being done about the problem rather than what specifically went wrong
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because internal postmortems often contain sensitive architectural details inappropriate for public consumption; verbatim publication is overcorrection on transparency. Option B is wrong because refusing all public communication after major customer-impacting incidents damages trust and is the opposite of mature incident communication. Option C is correct because the standard SRE practice is to produce a customer-facing summary covering impact, cause in customer terms, and remediation, while preserving the internal detailed version. Option D is wrong because omitting root cause and impact leaves customers without the context they need to assess whether to continue trusting the service.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: Your team is establishing toil tracking. Engineers will self-report time spent on toil weekly. What measurement approach best surfaces actionable insights for toil reduction work?
options: Track toil hours by specific task category (e.g., manual deploys, key rotation, on-call ticket triage, capacity reviews), aggregate across the team, and identify the highest-volume categories for automation prioritization

Track only total toil hours per engineer per week without category breakdown, since the absolute number is what matters for budget compliance and detailed categorization adds reporting overhead to engineers

Track toil as a percentage of working time per engineer without comparing across engineers, since individual variation in toil work assignments makes cross-engineer comparison misleading for team-level decision making

Track toil only when it exceeds an engineer's personal threshold of bothering them, since not all repetitive work is experienced as toil and self-reported friction is the most accurate measure of toxicity
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because categorized toil tracking surfaces which specific tasks consume the most engineering hours, enabling targeted automation investment where it returns most value. Option B is wrong because untracked categories prevent prioritization; the team knows it has toil but cannot decide what to automate first. Option C is wrong because cross-engineer comparison is not the goal; cross-task aggregation is what informs automation decisions. Option D is wrong because subjective bothered-by thresholds are inconsistent across engineers and miss high-volume toil that engineers have learned to tolerate quietly.

questions.question: A SEV1 incident has been resolved after 4 hours. The incident commander wants to schedule the postmortem meeting. Which scheduling and participant choice best supports a productive blameless postmortem?
options: Schedule the postmortem meeting within 24 hours, invite only the immediate responders, exclude any engineers whose code was involved in the root cause to avoid embarrassment, and produce the document privately

Schedule the postmortem meeting two weeks after resolution to allow emotions to settle, invite executive leadership for visibility, and require the meeting to focus exclusively on action items and timeline review

Schedule the postmortem meeting one month after resolution so participants have time to forget the emotional intensity of the response, invite only senior engineers who can analyze the timeline objectively without bias

Schedule the postmortem meeting within a few days of resolution while memory is fresh, invite all responders and engineers whose systems were involved, focus on systemic causes, and document publicly for organizational learning
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because excluding engineers involved in the root cause loses critical knowledge and signals that blame is in play despite the blameless label. Option B is wrong because two weeks delays memory decay and inviting executives adds pressure that can chill open discussion of contributing factors. Option C is wrong because waiting a month essentially loses the timeline fidelity that makes postmortems valuable; senior-only restriction also excludes voices closest to the failure. Option D is correct because timely scheduling with broad responder participation, systemic focus, and public documentation is the textbook SRE blameless postmortem approach.

questions.question: Your service runs in two Google Cloud regions in an active-active configuration. A disaster recovery test simulates failure of one region. During the test, the active-active failover succeeds but traffic to the surviving region briefly exceeds its capacity, causing latency spikes. What is the correct response?
options: Accept the latency spike as acceptable since the failover succeeded overall, and document the result as a successful DR test without further capacity changes since the surviving region eventually recovered to normal

Reduce traffic in normal operation by rate limiting customers, so that when failover occurs the surviving region has more headroom available because steady-state usage is lower than peak capacity in either region alone

Provision additional capacity in each region so that either region alone can handle the full failover load with appropriate headroom, even if this means higher steady-state cost from each region being individually overprovisioned

Move the architecture from active-active to active-passive so that the passive region is sized for the full load specifically, eliminating the issue of one region handling more load than steady-state operations require
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because accepting latency spikes during failover means customers experience degradation during the very event DR is meant to handle gracefully. Option B is wrong because rate limiting customers in normal operation degrades service quality continuously to handle a rare failover scenario; this is poor capacity planning. Option C is correct because active-active capacity planning requires that each region can absorb the full load during failover; this is the standard active-active architecture requirement. Option D is wrong because moving to active-passive trades active-active benefits like lower failover latency for capacity simplicity that can be solved within the active-active model.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: Your team is reviewing its incident response role separation policy. Which combination of role assignments is most appropriate for a SEV1 incident affecting customer-facing services?
options: A single on-call engineer who handles all roles including technical investigation, communications, decision-making, and timeline documentation, since centralizing reduces handoff friction in rapidly evolving incident situations

Incident commander, communications lead, technical lead for primary suspected failure domain, and scribe; with the IC having clear authority to declare resolution and coordinate the response without owning technical work themselves

Incident commander only, with the IC responsible for technical investigation as well as coordination, since separate technical roles add coordination overhead during the rapid mitigation phase of SEV1 response

Communications lead only, with the lead responsible for both internal Slack updates and customer-facing status page updates, while the technical team works independently on mitigation without a designated IC role
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because one engineer handling investigation, communications, decisions, and documentation under SEV1 pressure leads to dropped tasks and slow resolution. Option B is correct because the canonical SRE SEV1 role separation is incident commander, communications lead, technical lead for the suspected failure domain, and scribe; the IC coordinates without owning technical work. Option C is wrong because conflating IC with technical investigation eliminates the coordination role that prevents IC from being pulled into deep technical work. Option D is wrong because no incident commander role removes the coordination authority needed during SEV1 response.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: Your postmortem template includes a 'lessons learned' section. Which content style best supports cross-team and cross-incident learning over time as the postmortem library grows?
options: Write generic lessons such as 'we should test more thoroughly' or 'communication could have been better' so that the lessons apply to many situations and are easy to extract patterns from across postmortems

Write incident-specific lessons with concrete service names, configuration details, and specific operational situations, since this preserves the context needed to recognize whether a lesson applies to a future situation

Omit the lessons learned section entirely and rely on the root cause and action items sections to convey learning, since adding a separate lessons section duplicates content already captured elsewhere in the document

Write lessons as broad principles only, such as 'always test failover paths before production' so that lessons remain actionable across many service domains rather than being scoped to one specific service's specifics
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because writing lessons with searchable patterns and concise generalized framing enables the postmortem library to support cross-team trend analysis at scale; this complements the per-incident specifics found in root cause and contributing factor sections. Option B is wrong because incident-specific lessons without abstraction are harder to surface across postmortems when looking for systemic patterns spanning many services. Option C is wrong because omitting lessons learned loses an important distillation distinct from raw root cause analysis. Option D is wrong because principles without any context become slogans; some specificity is needed for actionability rather than purely abstract guidance.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: A team's chaos engineering practice has matured to the point where game days are running quarterly with low-blast-radius experiments in production. What is the appropriate next maturity step?
options: Reduce game day frequency to annual since the practice is mature; if experiments have not surfaced issues in recent quarters, more frequent execution provides diminishing returns relative to the engineering time invested

Stop running game days entirely since automated chaos engineering tools can now continuously inject low-blast-radius failures, eliminating the need for periodic human-led exercises that consume engineering time per quarter

Expand game day blast radius to test full-region failures simultaneously, accepting higher customer impact risk in exchange for testing more realistic failure scenarios that match what real disasters would actually involve

Move from quarterly scheduled game days toward continuous automated chaos experiments using tools like Chaos Mesh or custom infrastructure, while preserving periodic team game days for new scenarios and learning
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because reducing frequency on mature practice misses the ongoing benefit of regular reliability validation against evolving systems. Option B is wrong because eliminating human-led game days loses the creative scenario design that automation cannot replicate. Option C is wrong because expanding blast radius without proportional safety improvements increases customer risk without corresponding maturity gains; blast radius expansion should be gradual not jump-cut. Option D is correct because the next maturity step is moving toward continuous automated chaos experiments using tools like Chaos Mesh while preserving periodic team game days for novel scenario design that humans contribute.
questions.category: Applying SRE practices to a service

questions.question: Your team is establishing a toil budget policy. The policy proposes that when team toil exceeds 50% for two consecutive quarters, new feature work pauses and the team focuses entirely on automation. Which addition to the policy best ensures it functions as intended?
options: Allow the team manager to waive the toil-budget pause if business priorities require continued feature work, since management judgment should prevail over rigid policy thresholds during business-critical delivery windows

Make the policy advisory rather than enforceable, so that engineering culture rather than process determines whether toil reduction is prioritized, since healthy teams self-regulate without needing explicit enforcement mechanisms

Define automatic escalation: if toil exceeds 50% for two quarters, SRE leadership formally communicates the pause to product leadership with documented rationale, automation roadmap, and re-entry criteria for resuming feature work

Require an executive sign-off from outside the engineering org to invoke the toil-budget pause, so that an external authority can validate that the technical team's pause request is justified before feature work halts
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because manager waiver authority allows the policy to be bypassed precisely when it is most needed, defeating the budget mechanism. Option B is wrong because advisory toil budgets predictably do not change behavior; the whole point is enforceability when culture alone has not driven the change. Option C is correct because automatic escalation when toil exceeds 50% for two quarters, with formal SRE-to-product leadership communication including rationale, automation roadmap, and re-entry criteria, is the SRE-recommended toil budget enforcement pattern. Option D is wrong because external sign-off adds bureaucratic friction and signals that the SRE team does not own its own toil discipline, undermining the team's accountability for the budget.
